review:
Wow, the ratings seem all over the place. Well, it's not horrible. If you like that atmospheric, airy and space-y Pink Floyd style, you'll be served since it's basically all this album is. You can tell it's Pink Floyd, it's all sounding pretty "Floydian" but it's missing a little something, that little "touch" that makes them, you know, "them". I miss a "Marooned", a "Learning to Fly" or an "Astronomy Domine". Something that makes you stop and pay attention, that makes you come back to it over and over. It's also missing vocals, which is a bit unfortunate, even though many will say they were never the focal point in Pink Floyd.

There's a few standout moments like "Sum", "Anisina" (actually, the entirely of the 4 tracks dubbed "side 2" are pretty good) and "Louder than Words" but overall, The Endless River is unfortunately a bit on the weak side, especially waiting 20 years for this.

review:
So... It was twenty years ago today (well, not really today, but sure twenty years ago), Sgt Pink Floyd delivered us his last album, "The Division Bell".
This one was programmed to be recorded soon after, under another name ("The Big Spliff"), but recording was aborted after a few sessions, and the band, minus Wright who died in 2008, recorded it last year (for the tracks who weren't recorded in 1994). It's an ambient album, almost entirely instrumental, full of 18 tracks, and 9 of them are under the 2-minute mark. 
53 minutes of music. 
A really, really great album, for fans only. I think it will take a few listening to really begin to appreciate and love it, but this "Endless River", the very last PF album (Gilmour and Mason said it recently : the band is over, really, and there will be no concerts) is frankly a very,very, very, very good album.

At first I was pretty hyped when I heard that Pink Floyd (or rather a part of the gang) are releasing a "new" album, but then I heard that it's mostly instrumental and the songs are merely leftovers of the Divison Bell era etc.
The Endless River flows together very well, always sounds pleasant, but it's also reminiscent of songs you've already heard before (Shine On You Crazy Diamond for example).
Is it a necessary album for Pink Floyd's discography?
Probably not, but it's a great reminder how important Richard Wright was for Pink Floyd and their sound.

All in all, The Endless River is a beautiful hommage to Rick.

We all know there will never be another classic line-up  Pink Floyd album. Having said that I feel I understand the purpose of of this collection of songs. Gilmour and Waters received the lion share of the writing credits for this group, but it was Richard Wright, who shaped their sound and uniqueness more than any member of the group during their early and latter period. He carried the torch that Syd Barrett lit more than anyone but Waters seem to be the one always standing in the spotlight. David Gilmour recognized this and along with Mason cobbled together this release to show how over the years those familiar sounds revisited on this album [at times] were Richard Wright's and without his presence Pink Floyd will never be that great again.  Gilmour and Mason chose to complement what was left of Wright's work rather than steal the show which may make people think it to be boring. But as with any good Pink Floyd album you have to be in the mood and listen to the whole rather than parts, and for that Gilmour and Mason did an amazing job and Richard Wright would be flattered.

Disclaimer: 
Favourite Releases:

Dark Side of the Moon
Meddle
Wish You Were Here
The Piper At The Gates Of Dawn
A Saucerful Of Secrets

The above are in no particular order depending my listening mood.

" There is no dark side of the moon, it's all dark "

Well, well.

Pink Floyd, or rather what remains of them - since they've been scaled back down to a duo of David Gilmour and Nick Mason (although the ghost of Rick Wright appears on these tracks, which hail from The Division Bell pre-production rehearsals), release an album based on loose jams.

When was the last time they did that? Frankly, never. One would have to go back to 1967's Tonite Let's All Make Love In London and 1970's The Complete Zabriskie Point Sessions for something similar, but both of these are unofficial releases. They also highlight the problem with The Endless River. Whereas the 17-minute version of "Interstellar Overdrive" was an acid scorch and "Fingal's Cave" was a hallucinatory wasteland, The Endless River is muzak: sentimental fodder, second-rate New Age meditating, a re-imagining of Space Rock as Lounge senility.

It doesn't help that these generally shapeless tracks borrow heavily from past glories like "Shine On You Crazy Diamond" ("It's What We Do") and "Another Brick In The Wall" ("Allons-y"), neither that the album concludes with the ghastly elegy "Louder Than Words". If this is their last album, it's an embarrassing one.

And finally, let's admit it, Pink Floyd were heading that way already in the 70's, before Roger Waters' sociopath tendencies gave them a new lease of life with the vitriol of Animals and the bitterness of The Wall (before he himself lost the plot in the 80's).
Oh God. I am a Gilmour-era Pink Floyd apologist and I am terrified that this is what The Division Bell sounds like to normal people.

(That's a Land Of Confusion reference, up there ,) ).

WAY COOL! AND I ALREADY HAD GIVEN UP ON MY PRECIOUS DREAM - of having a new or relatively new PF album released after I had become a fan!

At the same time I was a bit scared - "what if it's meh?", I thought. Still, of course I had to buy it, I still keep the cash-out cheque! 

And after I had pressed "play"... I started to... seriously... get some kind of Floydian experience... as if high on music... It didn't let me go till the album was over, and it was magical. Magic, I tell you.

Okay, so, not too much epic tracks to report here, LTW is alright. but the experience, oh the experience...

I think of the Pink Floyd catalog as falling into three distinct eras. The first era spans the first six albums, and this is all about discovery. The band is discovering itself, its direction, and its music. The second, ranging from "Obscured by Clouds" to "The Final Cut" is all about conceptualization. Now that they've discovered new musical topography, they can now explore that topography and see what it can provide. The final era is all about bringing culmination to the band, solidifying its place in the pantheon of musical gods. 

This album brings all of that to its rightful end with a set of musical pieces that echo all three phases of the band's career. As I sit and listen to this album, I hear a touch of "Echoes" here, a bit of "Us and Them" there, and  little "Run Like Hell" over there. There are movements that mirror those in "Atom Heart Mother" and "Saucerful of Secrets" and "Shine on You Crazy Diamond." There's bits and pieces that come from every single one of their studio albums. But the pieces all fit together in an organic fashion that act as one musical piece. It is one last statement from the band, a coda or reprise if you will, that tells me where they've been over their long and storied career.

And then they sum it up with the very poignant "Louder Than Words" which not only plays well as one final "see, look what we did when we worked together" for the band, but in true Floydian fashion, they make a larger statement about human nature and human society. When we stop fighting and work together, when we communicate, when we actively seek cooperation (even with our differences) look what we can do. That, for me, is one hell of an optimistic message to leave on.

If this is indeed the last studio effort from Pink Floyd (and I do not doubt that it will be), I think it is just about as perfect an end as there could be. I also suspect that, over time, that 2.8 rating I see up there will increase as people further digest this album and its placement within the context of the larger catalog.

review:
Sort of like a clip-show episode of your favorite TV series. Those episodes are usually rather disappointing to the regular viewer, of course, because what you really want is some new stuff, not repeats of old stuff stitched together with some creaky connecting tissue. But what makes this clip-show particularly disappointing is that they didn't put in enough of the good bits of the previous episodes.

So... it's dull. Nice, yes, but dull. Given all the hype beforehand about the major role that Richard Wright's keyboards were to play in it, I had had high hopes (no pun intended) for this release. But there's nothing here that even comes close to the majesty of 'Marooned' from The Division Bell.

A bit of a shame they went out like this.

review:
In my teenage years I was obsessed with Pink Floyd. But "The Division Bell" wasn't an album I listened to that often and I never considered it one of their best albums. "The Division Bell" has grown on me 10 years after first hearing it, but I still wouldn't exactly ask for a sequel or a collection of leftovers from those sessions.

"The Endless River" is being marketed as a brand new Pink Floyd album, or perhaps a long lost Pink Floyd album. The songs are mostly leftover ideas and demos from the 1993 recording sessions, but expanded upon, completed and re-recorded by surviving active members Nick Mason and David Gilmour. Keyboardist Rick Wright's fingers are all over this record, yet you wonder how much input he had on it, having passed away a couple of years ago now.

"The Endless River" has some good things going for it. It is an album that flows very nicely and has a lot of depth in the sound. It begins with Eno-esque Ambience, spoken word samples and gradually builds into tracks of space rock with funky bass guitars, Gilmour's epic guitar and Wright's brilliant keyboard soundscapes. There are times when it sounds very similar to the instrumental synth intro of "Shine On You Crazy Diamond", using very similar sounds and signatures. The production and mixing is brilliant and it will sweep across your stereo system and fill every inch of the room with a cinematic scope. Closing track "Louder Than Words" is a real keeper, a Pink Floyd gem. "Talkin' Hawkin'" reuses Stephen Hawking's vocals from "The Division Bell"'s "Keep Talking", but I actually prefer this version to the original.

"The Endless River" does have some detractors to it though. For a Pink Floyd album it has a surprising lack of variety. A lot of the songs run into each other. It sounds a little too Pink Floyd to a point where it is atypically Floydian. Pink Floyd aren't exactly a predictable band when you look at how different each album is, but "Endless River" sounds like Floyd at their most cliched. Like I said, there are points where it sounds similar to Shine On or Comfortably Numb or High Hopes. It is also a shame their aren't more tracks with vocals, as "Louder Than Words" is by far the most interesting track on here. I think a problem in the way this album has been perceived by fans lies in the way it has been marketed. It isn't really a brand new album. It still sounds like 1994 Pink Floyd. I would like to think a 2014 Pink Floyd would have come up with something much more innovative, futuristic sounding and ground breaking, which "The Endless River" certainly isn't. Why couldn't they have just included all of these tracks as a bonus disc on the 20th anniversary edition of "The Division Bell" that was released earlier this year? Why did it have to be made into a brand new album?!

All things considered, "The Endless River" is good, pretty solid, brilliantly produced, but really in the minds of many fans it isn't really a true Pink Floyd album and even though Gilmour has spent time working on these tracks and making them sound complete, they still sound like demos or leftovers. Fortunately, considering the brilliant Day 1 sales and exciting fan response, it seems nobody is overly disappointed or amazed. Perhaps it's a case of die hard fans will take what they can get no matter what form this "new" Pink Floyd album exists in, but I think deep down it will be remembered as "The Division Bell Leftovers" and not the 2014 Pink Floyd album we might have hoped for.

I can't for the life of me fathom why Gilmour and Mason would release this under the guise of a genuine Pink Floyd album. They can't possibly need the money. What wasn't deemed good enough for a mediocre (by Pink Floyd standards) album 20 years ago hasn't suddenly turned into gold. I quite like "Louder Than Words," the only actual song on the album. It's a fitting farewell to Rick Wright and should maybe have been released on its own. They could have sold the rest anonymously for use in elevators and supermarkets.

It's, arguably, more of a Pink Floyd album than Division Bell, A Momentary Lapse of Reason and The Final Cut, all fine albums from where I sit.  The Endless River, however, just has something extra.  There is no supporting tour just an album. A sendoff for Rick Wright.  A chance to tie up loose ends.  Pink Floyd ends their career on a high note. A 4 seems to low, 4.5 too much. Wow, after the ratings on this added up to a collective 2.8 & change, I was hesitant to grab it at all.  So much for populist opinion.  This far exceeded my expectations.  This is the remnants of Floyd doing what they have always done best, creating soundscapes with no regard for track times, song structure, and in this case...lyrics.  Mind you, this is the 90's cum 2014 version of those soundscapes,  so don't go expecting Careful With That Axe and you might just be satisfied.  Within this release, there are touchstones to every era of the band's history, an homage to themselves if you will, and all the better for it.  However, and this is where the negative opinions of this release and I differ,  there are also pointers taken on board from many of the 80's and 90's Floyd-influenced space and ambient voyagers, bringing this to a blend that borders on ambient in spots, but is still unmisstakingly Pink Floyd.  And again, all the better for it. 
      And then, there's THAT guitar.   With all do respect to the Syd-era, and I do likes me some Syd-era,  if there ever was a part of Floyd that defined who they were, it's not Water's lyrics, it's the sound of Gilmour's Strat.  He can wring more emotion and lyricism out of a single note than most guitarists can do in 100.

The Endless River is Pink Floyd's farewell as a recording group.  It is also a tribute to their late keyboardist, Richard Wright.  The have taken bits and pieces from an ambient album they were working on during the time of the Division Bell but never completed.  That material has been woven together with new material to make up a mostly instrumental album that seems to have divided the Floyd loyal.  

Forget the " it would be great if Roger was here" camp as he has not been around for almost 30 years.  This is an ambient record showing what the 3 Floyds brought to the table in the first place.  That would be the music.  No biting lyrics.  If you always wanted more of the beginning or ending sections of Shine On You Crazy Diamond, you came to the right place.  Those looking for the snarling guitars and angst of Animals are going to be greatly disappointed.  

What David Gilmour and Nick Mason and friends have crafted with most of the tracks utilizing Richard Wright's distinctive keys and a good number of tracks giving him a writing credit, is demonstrate what Wright brought to the table in Pink Floyd. 

While not being a great album, it is a fine fitting end to one of Rock and Rolls greatest bands.

A fine ambient slow fade out to a magnificent career. I like that The Endless River has that distinctive Floyd signature sound without duplicating past glories. It grows with each listening as the meticulous detail and the subtleties emerge. A great balance between structured 'rock' and the more fluid soundscapes. Real depth and emotion too

One of the more interesting aspects of Pink Floyd's official catalogue is the complete absence of any reference to marijuana.  Pink Floyd, along with the Grateful Dead, earned their reputation as one of the granddaddies of head music, but throughout their career the band has continued its dogged insistence, as a bleary-eyed Dave Gilmour implausibly insisted in the 1971 stoner paradise film "Live at Pompeii", that Pink Floyd are "not a drug orientated band".

It's worth noting that the Dead seemed to similarly avoid direct reference to ganja, though I can't say this for sure as I'm not as familiar with their catalogue.  The one drug reference I know in their music is to cocaine, in the song "Casey Jones".  Pink Floyd, much later in their career, also referenced cocaine obliquely, in the song "Nobody Home".  So in both cases the absence of marijuana references does stick out a little.

Looking back on the bands that do reference marijuana from this time period may shed some light on the matter.  Some of it was undoubtedly wanting to avoid legal repercussions to the fullest extent possible.  Given that, to my knowledge, no Pink Floyd member was ever picked up on drug charges, this policy seems to have paid off to a certain degree.  However, there seems to be more behind it than coy prudence, as in Pink Floyd's heyday serious music simply did not talk about marijuana usage.  The best-case scenario was that you wound up like Gong, great music to be sure but unquestionably silly.   The worst-case scenario was David Peel and the Lower East Side.  It's not simply a rock-era thing, either- the association of songs about marijuana with goofy novelty music goes back to the era of Mezz Mezzrow and Harry "The Hipster" Gibson, tea-heads who were simply impossible to take seriously.

This hasn't stopped Pink Floyd from, throughout their career, coming to the brink of tacitly admitting drug use.  The closest they came was actually the B-side of their first single, a riff on "Smokestack Lightning" originally titled "Let's Roll Another One", from the days when their set was filled with songs with names like "Stoned Alone".  Certain objections were raised to the subject matter of the song, however, and the song was eventually released as "Candy and a Currant Bun", though nobody seemed to have any objections to the fact that Syd Barrett pronounced "walk" with an "f" in the revised version.

From then on, the spectre of the demon weed occasionally raised its ugly head.  Drugged-out instrumental "Quicksilver" was released in some countries with the alternate title "Water-Pipe".  When the first Pink Floyd box set came out in the '90s, it was originally titled "The Big Bong Theory", before being named to something so bland and inoffensive I can't actually recall the title.  And now we have Pink Floyd's latest and presumably last release, "The Endless River", formerly known as "The Big Spliff", on which one can ascertain not a single overt drug reference.

In fact, the most damning thing you can say about this record is that it doesn't even work as drug music.  Not in a Grateful Dead sense of "this music is so bad you have to be high to listen to it", but more, well, times have changed, and Pink Floyd simply haven't kept up with the stoner-music status quo.  The drug Pink Floyd refused to name all these years is finally well on its way to becoming legal, and for all the stories about them soundchecking "Saucerful of Secrets" on the Division Bell tour, they simply don't have the cosmic reek they had back in those days.  Bands like Spacemen 3, followed by Sleep and Monster Magnet, broke the taboo against talking about weed when making Serious Drug Music, and what started as a half-hearted attempt to retake Pink Floyd's title as "First Band in Space" has wound up as a damp squib of an elegy to their deceased keyboardist that pales next to a record like "Dopesmoker".

I've always been a huge Floydie and I'll always be a huge Floydie. Just a fact of life. So from the getgo there was no way I was going to dislike this record. It might be difficult to determine its purpose, seeming so casually thrown together, consciously mood music. If you are a Floyd fan you must get some degree of enjoyment out of high fidelity textural immersion, and that's the entire purpose here. Gilmour said, "Unapologetically, this is for the generation that wants to put its headphones on, lie in a beanbag, or whatever, and get off on a piece of music for an extended period of time."

The production here has got to be some sort of freaky technical benchmark. It sounds like a Dreamworks film in an Imax theater. You'll know it's distinctively them from the moment Nick comes in with his slow fill. I could have used more of the choral voices but it's fine.

I keep coming back to this for some reason despite only having one fleshed out conventional song, and I'm not even in any sort of post-rock instrumental rock phase. The sound is so detailed that new little timbres keep emerging on different equipment, proving it to be more than casual jams knocked out in a weekend.

Yes it's the most harmless fluffy thing ever and yes it's nerdy as hell. Of course it's not relevant to right now and of course it can't stand up to their classic material. None of that should really matter though. There's nothing wrong with strapping on the nice cans to sink into a recording that simply sounds exquisite.

I don't understand the low rating. First, this isn't even meant to be a normal album, right? People are listening to it as if it was a new album with new material. You're wrong. These are leftovers, and as leftovers, it is a very good album, in some parts even better than The Division Bell (ok, I don't like Anisina, it makes me think of Kenny G). 

Just listen to it and forget the bad reviews. There's a lot of Floyd ingredients in this one (A Saucerful of Secrets, Ummagumma, Meddle, Dark Side, everywhere), maybe that's why people don't like it: they call themselves Pink Floyd fans but they only like the 1973-1979 period.

Uno dei più grandi errori di Syd Barrett è stato quello di bruciarsi il cervello con acidi di tutti i tipi: non tanto perché fu poi estromesso dal gruppo, bensì perché fu sostituito da David Gilmour, ovvero il completo negativo musicale: un ottimo strumentista, pulito e ragionato ma decisamente meno capace sul lato della composizione. Perché se è vero che questo disco si può considerare come l'addio di Richard Wright, è anche vero che in pratica è un collage di scarti (principalmente intermezzi strumentali) di registrazione di The Division Bell, e la colla è tutta del chitarrista. Il punto è che, a conti fatti, The Division Bell è decisamente uno dei dischi più bolsi dei Pink Floyd, nonostante non sia strettamente brutto, ma evidentemente l'idea di prendere in mano un moniker fermo da quasi vent'anni e artisticamente quasi nullo da almeno trenta deve essere parsa fantastica e quindi via, ascoltiamo The Endless River. Iniziamo con le cose buone: Sum è una buona composizione di tastierine minimali e chitarrone ricco di gain, su un beat un pelino sostenuto, e la successiva Skins sembra una coda molto ben riuscita di Interstellar Overdrive (addirittura!) tra casino, space rock e tamburoni. E poi basta. 53 minuti di musica e solo due brani che si possono considerare compiutamente ben riusciti: It's What We Do conferma l'attitudine spacey ma introduce anche evitabili atmosfere new age e il lento Tocco di Gilmour©, il trittico Allons Y(1)/Autumn '68/Allons Y(2) prova a resuscitare malamente certe sonorità di The Wall (in un disco strumentale, no complimenti), On Noodle Street e The Lost Art of Conversation riprendono il discorso new age tra pianolismi onirici e bluesettini leccatini, mentre l'orrida Anisina è un riccardonissimo che uccide TUTTO con tanto di sassofono di plastica che farebbe vergognare Kenny G. Come ciliegina sulla torta c'è Louder Than Words, un poppettino ottantiano da catechismo andato a male, che messo alla fine del disco contribuisce non poco ad abbassare la stima che si ha di questo disco. Mi sarebbe anche piaciuto parlare un po' di più della musica, ma di idee ce ne sono talmente poche che quasi stento a credere che si tratti di un disco dei Pink Floyd, quanto piuttosto di una compilation di intro o intermezzi ambientali. Potevano chiamarlo "Greatest Intros" già che c'erano.

review:
The existence of The Division Bell apologists is bizarre. The Wall was not only a brilliant concept album, it was the soundtrack to a band's collapse. Listening to that album closely, one can hear the musicians straining beyond their reach, falling inwards on themselves and their ambitions. The subsequent albums would be journeys to the remaining fragments following Pink Floyd's death. Anyone expecting what was heard previously to continue lived in delusion, and the same can be said of The Endless River. One should expect a Comfortably Numb-caliber song on the final Pink Floyd release as much as they should have expected an Interstellar Overdrive on Dark Side of the Moon.

This collection of songs contain the last vestiges of Pink Floyd as they dissolve into the ether of non-being. The Endless River has little in the way of dramatic flair and no vocals (save for the closing track) because ultimately, there's nothing left for the band to say at this point. Rather than existing as a nostalgic vessel housing a legion of throwbacks, the melodies solemnly look forward. Gilmour's guitar work retains the same pensive yet not overly technical flow, and the late Richard Wright's compositions stand strong with heavy classical influence.

From an objective standpoint, The Endless River is a pleasant and nonthreatening ambient rock album. The songs work because they never become over-indulgent, as all of the motives are smartly deployed in bite-sized tracks. For the lifelong Floyd fan, this is a fitting if arguably unnecessary coda - a finer companion to what is surely the spirit of the band's final stride than what is now the penultimate release. The Endless River satisfies, even for this old crusty Wish You Were Here apologist.

review:
Pink Floyd never made it to the 50 year mark, but they got close, and “close” is more than I can say for Endless River, and album that features an unremarkable cover of a gent sailing off into the sunset on a warm sea of clouds ... how very prophetic.  But allow me backtrack, and wonder for a bit.

With this many years and albums behind them, Pink Floyd have been many things to many generations, but never the same to all, and I sincerely doubt there’s a fan who hasn’t gotten lost along the way, cherishing several albums that speak to them, and forever selfishly hoping that Floyd and Pink will find their way back to those special moments.  I remember seeing Pink Floyd on their first tour of America, it was an adventure to say the least, then again for Dark Side of The Moon, and Animals.  For me, Pink Floyd were all about Meddle, Dark Side, Wish You Were Here, and Animals ... after that they lost me.  I genuinely became tired and disenchanted by their infighting, and the constant repetition of themes, though it’s been said that an artist paints but one painting, and then constantly repeats it with variations.  So yes, there are those who stand firmly in the camp of Syd, and the mid 60’s Floyd, and those rooted in the 70’s, when home electronics hit their golden age, and yes, many are still enthralled with The Wall, and all that followed.  But even with all their experimentation, Endless River is not a proper Pink Floyd album, no matter how much Floyd’s members try to insist that it is.

First and foremost, it’s nearly a posthumous release, as two original members have drifted downstream, and most of the material harkens back to outtakes and cutting room floor bits that at the time were deemed unremarkable, yet here, strung together with other bits and pieces from the 60’s onward, somehow people have convinced themselves that Floyd are doing what they’ve always done ... drawing from their surroundings to create a sonic landscape of vision and wonder.  But it’s not a sonic landscape, for the most part it’s an instrumental album that drifts nowhere, channeling nothing, and climbs to no remarkable soaring heights.  The lyrics are sparse, and when delivered, still harken to personal imperfections, wrong doings, the fact that time has passed them by, and the notion that no matter how sincere, amends can never be made with either lost family or bandmates.

To be honest, Endless Rive comes off like the final two hour special for a television sit-com series, where there are endless flashbacks, outtakes worked in, and an attempt to tie things together … though sadly always coming off a bit disingenuous.  But, this is the way of the world, people desire things to be drawn together, all of the i’s dotted, and the t’s crossed.  If anyone should know that the curtains can not be drawn, and the door quietly closed, it’s Pink Floyd, a band who made their mark by painting a hallucinatory sky.  

I do not relish this bit of reality …

*** As you sit uncomfortably numb, consider this:

'Endless River' grew out of unreleased recordings from 20 years ago … Gilmour and Mason returned to series of ambient, lyricless recordings from the same period as Pink Floyd's last album, 1994's 'The Division Bell,' to complete 'The Endless River.' These secondary sessions began in late 2013. Additional drumming from Mason was the first new element to be added.

The album included some of Richard Wright's final recordings … the initial leak on this surprise project, courtesy of Gilmour's wife Polly Samson, framed 'The Endless River' as Wright's "swan song," putting into perspective, once more, what the keyboardist meant to Pink Floyd. His 2008 death led many to believe that 'The Division Bell' would be the group's final recording. 'The Endless River' includes work by Wright from as far back as 1969. In all, he is credited with writing or co-writing 12 songs.

Secret sessions were, at first, mistaken for a Gilmour solo album … Durga McBroom-Hudson, a long-time backup singer with Pink Floyd, posted a photo to Instagram last year from sessions being led by Gilmour, and at the time, the assumption was that the guitarist was at work on a follow up to his 2006 solo album 'On an Island.' Instead, McBroom-Hudson later confirmed that they were adding vocals to these original Pink Floyd recordings.

The album's title harkens back to an earlier Pink Floyd moment … 'The Endless River' echoes the penultimate lyric on 'High Hopes,' the last song on 'The Division Bell.' That song was written by Gilmour, with additional lyrical help by Samson, who also collaborated on the new album.

20 years is a long time between between albums, but not the longest … Pink Floyd's two decades between 'The Division Bell' and 'The Endless River,' though certainly the lengthiest expanse in their history, has been dwarfed by other classic rockers. There were, for instance, 24 years between the Who's 'It's Hard' and 'Endless Wire.' Then, there's the Eagles. They waited 28 between 'The Long Run' and 'Long Road Out of Eden.'

review:
Having heard it a few times with pauses in between the turns to let it settle in better, I can only say one thing about it: AWESOME!

This is Pink Floyd at their most spacey since WYWH, at their most experimental since 'Ummagumma' and at their most ballsy since DSOTM. This is a band that risks a big stain on their legacy by releasing such an album at this point in their career, but oh does it pay! It's a big middle finger waving in front of critics' faces, a big 'piss off' to the casual radio listeners and the biggest present ever to the real fans.

I love it.

There were too many requirements to this record, it was impossible that the output were something remarkable. 

First, it was done in memory of Rick Wright, so it had to be something with tons of keyboards. I could fit Pink Floyd style, but they pushed too much in the ambient direction. 

Second, as a consequence of the first requirement and in order to use music recorded by Mr. Wright, it had to be done recycling leftovers of The Division Bell. We all know what happens with extended version CDs, or unreleased material: most times it was discarded from its original record for a very good reason, and it does not get better through the years. Not that the music in this CDs is disgusting, but it´s just passable, and very often it sounds like a rehearsal, with a beautiful mix - Pink Floyd still master that area -, but still demos of songs.

Third, no vocals. Why? I can´t figure out. Mr.Gilmour does not seem to have lost his voice, so I can only come back to requirement one: wanted they to give even more presence to keyboards?

The result is just passable, not bad but nothing remarkable at all. It´s not likely that I´ll be listening to it often.

The last Pink Floyd LP, “The Divison Bell” was released in 1994 and was believed to be the final studio record of the legendary band. The band split up after the tour and only reunited shortly for a 30-minute Live Aid Gig. 

In 2006 keyboarder Rick Wright joined guitarist David Gilmour in the studio to record Gilmour’s solo release “On an Island”. Wright also toured with Gilmour. The two men rediscovered their friendship and were looking forward to work together in the following years. Rick Wright then unfortunately lost his battle against cancer in 2008.
Four years after the passing of Wright Gilmour came up with the idea of releasing a new album with unused material from “The Divison Bell” sessions as tribute to Wright, who was a creative force of these recording sessions. Together with Phil Manzanera Gilmour started to listening to the 50 unused tracks and started to arrange them, cut them and to add guitar and drum parts. Bassist Andy Jackson, bassist Youth also joined them and worked on this releases. Later also Pink Floyd founding member Nick Mason joined them as drummer. 
The result of this sessions is “Endless River”, the final Pink Floyd album, which consists of four instrumental parts. I admit that I was skeptical when the album was released but I really like the album. It only took some minutes until the Pink Floyd feel was there after I pushed play. The instrumental release sounds very 70s-like. The album features the trademark drum sound, the psychedelic touch and the beautiful melodies Pink Floyd was always known for. 

I don’t get it why the album is rated so bad. Of course it’s questionable to release this album under the Pink Floyd banner, since founding member Roger Waters wasn’t involved at all and of course it’s not as good as “The Dark Side of the Moon” or “Wish You Were Here”, but what did people expect of an album that consists of songs, which were not used 20 years ago?. I too was doubting that this album would be any good, but the most important thing is that it sounded like Pink Floyd from the beginning until the end.

After a couple of albums about children's books set in space, Pink Floyd has been all about mourning. Mourning for Syd, then mourning for Waters' childhood, then mourning for Gilmour's lack of hair, and now mourning for Rick Wright. 

Glad to see they now acknowledge that Pink Floyd were always about themselves, a self-mythologizing grandeur of pseudo-existential drama. Music to read The Times by, or entertaining your friends at a middle-class dinner party.

I look forward for the next album, made entirely by PF's accountant department after they're all dead. Why Pink Floyd? Why indeed.

And a love letter to the fans, too. The Endless River is oft dismissed as a cash grab or Division Bell outtakes, and perhaps these things are true to some extent.

But in pulling from the sounds of Saucer ("Skins"), Dark Side ("Anisina"), WYWH ("It's What We Do"), and the Division Bell (pretty much everything else), what remained of Pink Floyd accomplished something sorely needed: a reconciliation with the past, and acknowledgment of newer fans.

I was born the year the Division Bell was released, and the album was never one of my favorite works by Pink Floyd even though they have been my favorite band for as long as I've been an avid music listener. Hearing that after 20 years, and against all odds, more was coming, I didn't know how to react. But listening to the album, I knew it was, despite its flaws, absolutely essential. And for the first time, I felt a part of the experience rather than a historian. My reception of the album has cooled down in the years since it's release. Musically there's no denying it's one of the weaker entries in their discography. But as a capstone, it is well deserving the Pink Floyd name, and recontextualizes all that came before it.

Gilmour acknowledges the weaknesses of his era of Pink Floyd by dropping lyrics almost entirely, makes peace with the passage of time and passing of Richard Wright by releasing four ambient suites based on his unfinished work that recall the periods of the band's history where Wright's contributions were most important. And there's even a nod to Waters in "Louder Than Words"... despite everything, "this thing we do" is timeless, and all the hate is ancient history.

David Gilmour may have been the star of Pink Floyd, and Roger Waters the brain, but the soul has always been Richard Wright, his atmospheric keyboards and organs the elevating factor that raised Pink Floyd to become greater than the sum of their parts. It is no mistake that Gilmour and Mason pay simultaneous tribute to both Pink Floyd and Wright on this album, the two are one in the same.

I remember July 6th, 2014 quite well. That was the day of my 13th birthday. I actually don’t remember much, but one thing I remember was me checking the internet on my dad’s iPad when suddenly I saw a news article about something I thought was impossible back then: a new Pink Floyd album was on its way. I got so excited, the hype was going through my veins. While The Endless River came out on early November 2014, I had to wait until Christmas to listen to it. After playing the whole album on his CD player, little me stood up and said “it was fine”. I listened to the album a few more times, but my mind hadn't changed much until I started reading about the making of this album. Normally, when I read about an album it makes me understand some things that I missed out and it makes me appreciate the album even more, but this time it was different, something hit me. Everything was clear, I finally realized that this album is bad.



Man, where do I even begin? Oh yeah, with the fact that The Endless River is just a bunch of leftover demos from The Division Bell. Now, just because this is a demo tape in all but name doesn't mean it's bad, for example, Ween and Kendrick Lamar demonstrated that you can release a good demo tape. The difference is that Shinola and untitled unmastered were leftovers from albums that were already great on their own, while The Endless River came from an album where its major issue was that it was too boring and repetitive, and those issues are even more noticeable in this album, especially now that the entirety of the album is only instrumentals.



Speaking of which, I'm sure everyone has seen that one meme about Pink Floyd songs being nothing than boring 20 minute solos. Well, I'm sure that meme came from someone who has only listened to this album, because they have never made something so long and boring at the same time. This sounds like a stock music CD you would find at a bargain bin. Not only that, but they had the audacity to divide the album into 4 (as if the average Joe would know when one section ends and the other begins), with the entire album leading up to the only song with vocals, Louder than Words, the cringiest and corniest song Pink Floyd ever made. I'm surprised not many people consider it one of the worst songs from a lyrical standpoint, because just hearing it for one second makes me feel terrible. Speaking of terrible, Gilmour's vocals are really bad, he sounds like he has coughed a lot or something, kinda makes me glad this whole album was nothing but instrumentals. It really ruins what High Hopes unintentionally meant by instead replacing it with the total opposite of it.



I feel the most wasted oportunity on this album is not bringing back Roger Waters or the lack of vocals, but that is a tribute to Rick Wright. If you've know the story of Pink Floyd, you would know Wright started contributing less after Wish You Were Here, the nightmare that was The Wall sessions took a toll on him, and his issues with his first marriage made him focus more on that than the band, which ended up with him leaving the band. Wright was an amazing musician, he made some great deep cuts like Remember a Day, and is probably one of the best keyboardists ever, and the fact that this was the best Gilmour and Mason could do for him compared to what the band did for Syd is kinda disappointing. If this is a tribute to Rick Wright, then he deserves a better tribute.



Normally I'd try to counterbalance all this negativity with something positive, like how the drums on Skins are actually quite good or how Ahmed Emad Eldin, the artist who designed the cover, perfectly evoked the style of Storm Thorgeson (something a lot of artists can't do), but I feel like I can't. Hearing or seeing this album and remembering the thoughts that raced through my mind about what the band could offer after 20 years of radio silence before its release make me feel upset. I really wanted this album to be great, a testament to the greatness and legacy of Pink Floyd, but instead it comes off as one more desperate cash grab. Gilmour decided to end the story of the band not with a bang, but with a whimper, a really boring one.
Better than it could have been but really nothing to see here. Worth a listen if you're a hardcore Floyd fan and want to try and imagine how Wright's keyboards would work on a late 80s Eno collab.

Nothing here is remotely groundbreaking or new but beyond my wildest expectations Pink Floyd (or more accurately what remains of it) successfully recaptures the feel of their early/mid seventies work with this collection.  Reaching back to the sound of the last truly collaborative Pink Floyd album - Wish Your Were Here - Gilmour and Wright pretty much pick up right where they left off in 1975 just before Waters began to dominate the group's musical output.  Avoiding vocals was another brilliant move - Gilmour and crew implicitly acknowledge that they can't create lyrics or vocals that could ever rise above mediocre . . . so they focus exclusively on the music.  Gilmour's guitar and Wright's keyboards are absolutely mind-blowing here - some of the most hypnotically beautiful sounds I've ever heard in pop/rock.  Of course this entirely lacks the driving conceptual themes of their peak work but somehow that doesn't detract from my enjoyment.  And yes, they rip off their own records - particularly "Shine on You Crazy Diamond" - but since that's my favorite Floyd song why would I care?  I never imagined Floyd would release anything new I would want to hear after the disastrous first two Gilmour-led albums but I was proven wrong.  I'm sure this is a one-shot deal but thank you David Gilmour for giving us one more beautiful dose of prime Pink Floyd magic!

This album, all considered, is a good product and I don't hear the lack of inspiration that I heard on parts of "The Division Bell". The construction of the four suites is quite intelligent, and the variations on the classics are done with gusto, so I don't feel that "The Endless River" is barrel-scrapping. The production is excellent too: very clear and warm at the same time. Obviously, this album is not flawless and it would have been hard to make it perfect: finishing a project after so many years is never easy, expecially if, in the meantime, one of the guys passes away. For this reason, there are parts of this album who have an unfinished feel (mostly the first part, in my opinion).

There's always the same question: is this album really necessary? Only Gilmour and Mason can tell us. If they felt that "The Division Bell" was not adequate as a finale and that Pink Floyd had to end with a definitive word, then I guess it was. All I can say is that I think this album is a good "post-scriptum" and that it's a very dignified way to close the carreer of one of the most important bands in rock. The third side is my favorite.

The most unnecessary album in Pink Floyd's discography, The Endless River consists entirely of instrumental tracks from the Division Bell sessions that were left on the cutting-room floor. They should have stayed there.

This album does nothing to justify its own existence. Because it’s an instrumental album, there's no vocals or guiding lyrical theme, both of which have proven to be significant strengths in past Pink Floyd albums. This in of itself wouldn’t be a problem if the instrumentals were good- Lord knows Pink Floyd has their share of solid instrumental tracks- but this is fifty minutes' worth of directionless, insubstantial ambience that meanders its way from the first track to the last. It’s tolerable if you want something calming to play in the background while you do something else, but for a band like Pink Floyd which has always required an active ear, it’s pretty garbage.

What a dismal note to end on.

I'd say "better than The Wall", but that should go without saying. Maybe it's just because I have a taste for ambient new-age shit, but I find this a pleasure to listen to in a way that pretty much none of Pink Floyd's other stuff is.

In the 60s they could get away with it thanks to the psychedelic movement, in the 70s they could get away with it because Roger Waters' lyrics gave the material a sense of thematic focus and theatricality. But for the most famous and storied progressive rock group of all-time to "reunite" after twenty years and deliver an ambient new age instrumental album in 2014? There's no excuse. This is just bad. If this truly is meant to stand as the final Pink Floyd album as David Gilmour claims it is, then not only is it an awful final act, but it's an insult to the band's legacy.

review:
In 2014, Pink Floyd announced The Endless River, composed primarily of instrumental outtakes and experiments recorded during the Division Bell sessions. A small number of additions were made in 2013 to complete the album.



When this was announced, I set my expectations low. Gilmour had proven himself to be an inconsistent songwriter, and the prospect of something stitched together from leftover bits did not leave me optimistic.  



My initial reaction to The Endless River was, “Wow, this is surprisingly not-shitty!” I then proceeded to not listen to it again until writing this essay six years later. That should tell you all you really need to know. It’s passable instrumental space rock in small doses, but nothing makes this record noteworthy or worth revisiting. The ungainly length of this record is a hindrance, and it truly lives up to the endlessness promised in its name.



“Things Left Unsaid” is emblematic of many of the sins of this era of Pink Floyd. A dull synth drone acts as the backdrop to a slow, languid guitar line for four-and-a-half minutes. It’s “Cluster One” trying to be the closing moments of “Shine on You Crazy Diamond”. “It’s What We Do” is an improvement, insofar as it has a pulse. This again feels like a weak, sterile attempt to recreate moments off “Shine on You Crazy Diamond”. With this being an instrumental album, Roger Waters’s presence isn’t missed. He was never a standout bassist, as I’ve mentioned previously, and his playing almost always bled into the background as Wright and Gilmour took the lead. This album’s flaccid opening movement closes with the brief “Ebb and Flow”, which is all ebb and no flow.



“Sum” is the first place where the album does anything interesting. Wright’s organ stutters in a way which evokes many of Floyd’s best songs, including “Astronomy Domine”. It’s too long and lacks direction, but I’ll take aimless jamming over aimless airiness. “Skins” is a callback to early cuts like “A Saucerful of Secrets” and “Up the Khyber” with Nick Mason’s distinct, tom-heavy drumming style taking the lead. When a drum solo is the strongest cut on an album so far, that’s usually not a good sign. “Anisina” is too sweet, and it feels like generic background music to be used in a heartwarming scene on a made-for-TV movie. I also hate the tone of the saxophone on this song.



Another brief, ambient piece—“The Lost Art of Conversation”—doesn’t do much beyond occupy two minutes of time, but “On Noodle Street” is one of the better tracks on The Endless River. It’s not particularly good in absolute terms, mind you, but enough happens on this brief cut to keep me interested. It’s mellow and jazzy, but my ultimate assessment is simply “inoffensive.”



“Night Light” harbors some darker, minor-key tones, and “Allons-y” finally gets something going with its bouncing rhythm and a guitar line that could have been one of the better songs on The Wall. “Autumn ‘68” is a pointless mini-fugue which leads back into the second half of “Allons-y”. “Talkin’ Hawkin’” features more vocal snippets from Stephen Hawking, and it’s nice that this song has enough percussion to maintain an identifiable beat.



“Calling”, which opens side 4 of this album, is an interesting collection of moody synthesizers. This one could have been workshopped into something better, but there are nuggets of good ideas here. “Eyes to Pearls” stays in the same neighborhood but with a bit more muscle, and “Surfacing” is one of the rare cuts to feel like a real song.



The one song with vocals—“Louder Than Words”—closes out The Endless River. It’s a pretty typical Gilmour-era ballad. Soulful background singers in the chorus feel like a crutch, and the instrumentation doesn’t do much to grab the listener. Giving credit where credit is due, the closing guitar solo is quite good.



Looking on this record with relatively fresh eyes, I find its immense bloat and frequent aimlessness hobble any other redeeming qualities. It isn’t actively bad in most cases, but it’s frequently downright anodyne. Oftentimes, boring is worse than bad. Ummagumma's studio disc is an ungodly, unfocused morass, but they at least were trying weird and different things. The Endless River is endlessly safe. It was a disappointing, unnecessary way for Pink Floyd to wrap up their career.

One cannot fully enjoy this album without being familiar with the entirety of Pink Floyd's output. 



While their are no blatant note-for-note bits lifted from the past, this album is jam-packed with references to past Floyd songs, Suggestions of passages that hearken back to familiar riffs and movements from all over their back catalog.



I suppose you might need to be a fan of the later years' Gilmour camp, rather than the Waters era. This album is a textured experience that is meant to cap off the end of an incredible career of a band that had an undeniable impact.



"The endless river," after all, were the last lyrics at the end of "The Division Bell" album. However, the final song on this release is the only one with lyrics...and is talking specifically about the absence of words.



Sublime soundscapes are to be expected. If you are looking for a "Dark Side" copy or "The Wall, part 2", then avoid this album.



However, if whenever you put "Live From Pompeii" on the TV and are unable to look away from it, I would highly recommend this album to you. Be sure to find the extended release with the track "Nervana" for some beefy Gilmour riffage that is not present on the rest of the album.

1001 Albums You Must Die Before You Hear

#10 – “Pink Floyd” – The Endless River
TLDR: Imagine if Shine On You Crazy Diamond was fifty minutes long and had no point.
The Division Bell sessions were quite productive. Wright in particular was out of his drug and depression days and felt creatively energized, producing a solo album immediately after TDB sessions wrapped. The band also worked on an hour long druggy instrumental album called The Big Spliff which they ultimately decided not to release.
Go forward twenty years and its time to get something together as a bonus disc for the 20th anniversary edition of The Division Bell. The material that would’ve been The Big Spliff is a likely contender and then some genius thinks – hey, why won’t we just sell it as a new album?

Mutton dressed as lamb, The Endless River is meandering collage of twenty year old studio doodlings that oscillates between cheesy ezy-listening music and stultifying dullness. It has eighteen songs, almost none of them worth describing individually, and even if I did it would be “the one that sounds like Welcome To The Machine” and “the one that sounds like Us And Them”. There’s a fascinating glimpse of an album themed around the band itself dying, as their sounds and touchstones are brought together and melt away, like a Floydian version of Everywhere At The End Of Time. We do not get that album though. We get instead an embarrassing meditation-and-wine-glass club soundtrack of schmalzy new age piffle.
There are a few bright spots – the band wakes up on Allonsy which sports a rare sighting of an identifiable riff and has a nice, forward driving momentum, but it’s over in a couple of minutes and we’re back to more slow bluesy space-rock where Gilmour just does the same thing over and over again. But perhaps worst of all is final song, Louder Than Words, the only track which features lyrics. One of the most anti-climactic endings to a great band (perhaps it doesn’t quite take the crown from Emerson Lake And Palmer) Louder Than Words is purest, uncut, wet fart in a crowded elevator. It features one of the worst choruses PF have ever done, Gilmour’s voice has lost its magic, and the lyric is piss poor. (Though you do get to hear Gilmour say “diss” and “bitch” in the opening line, revealing an unlikely Flava Flav influence.) And Rick Wright is not to be blamed for a second of it as he passed away in 2008.
Pink Floyd are a great, important band and believe me, I take no pleasure in seeing them off with a kick in the nuts. However, this lazy, shapeless release is not worth anyone’s time or money. More like The Endless Drivel.

This is not great album, not by any means. Pink Floyd, now under David Gilmour, had a successful formula, and they followed it, despite the lack of Waters's lyrical genius. Released before this album was A Momentary Lapse of Reason, which fell flat on its face. Why did this album not do this? I can attribute this to several causes. First, Gilmour didn't try to do everything himself, unlike AMLOR, which was essentially a Gilmour solo album in much the same way the The Final Cut was essentially a Waters solo album. Second, Rick Wright is back in the band, and contributes some great keyboards to the album. Third, we are out of the eighties, and no more would Gilmour stay near the 80s pop movement. Forth, Gilmour found, somewhat, his voice as leader of the band, and did not try experimenting as he did on AMLOR (which, despite Gilmour's amazing vocal skills, had some awful vocal pieces - A New Machine, Dogs of War). 

Yet this album is still not a great album. This is largely because Waters may not have been the best composer, but at least he had vision. Gilmour, on the other hand, was an excellent musician, but had little or no vision. Waters could write always passable, usually good music as well as great lyrics, and could hold an album together through this (as seen on TFC). Gilmour, however, could write some great music when he wanted (but not on AMLOR), but seemed to have little idea where, exactly, he wanted to go with it. Just my opinion, but it shines clearly on this album. 

From the title of the album alone, you should know this would be the band's last. The album is a semi concept album about the breakup of the band (well, Waters leaving...). This is most notable in the song High Hopes (Gilmour's best lyrical effort, hands down no questions asked, none at all), which describes the time around the Wall and The Final cut as, "our thoughts strayed constantly and without boundary/the ringing of the division bell had begun." Hard to get clearer than that. This is also apparent on "A Great Day for Freedom," which talks about when the "wall" came down... hmmm, about the Berlin wall, or perhaps a veiled reference to Waters and his wall... hmmm. Now, on to the actual songs: 

The album opens with Cluster One, a decent instrumental, but a bit too long given the overall pace of the song. It would have worked better if it built faster and ended about a minute sooner or so. It's similar to Signs of Life off AMLOR, but not as good. A decent opening, but it doesn't lead me to expect the next Dark Side of the Moon or Wish You Were Here. 

Any notions of this being a great album evaporated for me after What Do You Want From Me. The music is decent enough, though not great... but, aghhh, those lyrics, they're just awful, and the vocals don't do much to help out in that regard. It's about how Gilmour couldn't tell what the domineering Waters wanted from him. Eh, not great so far. 

Poles Apart is about the seperation of the goals of Waters and Gilmour, and how they wished to go in different directions musically. Musically it's better, and lyrically, it's leagues above What Do You Want From Me, but in all honesty, I prefer almost anything from the classic Floyd period (for me): Meddle to The Final Cut. There is no excuse for this song being as long as it was, as unlike in their classic (for me) period, Gilmour didn't have enough musical ideas to sustain much that long (High Hopes is a notable counterexample). 

Ah, finally some relief. Marooned is a beautiful instrumental. Pure, sheer, unadultered bliss for five minutes. I don't know my instruments, so I can't tell you for sure what happens (and I haven't heard it in a while), but it I think features some stunningly beautiful guitar, proving why Gilmour was a master of his trade. I will say it now, I prefer this instrumental to any of the three off Dark Side of the Moon. 

A Great Day for Freedom, is, as I have said, about the end of Waters's tyrannical rule over the band using the metaphor of the Berlin Wall. It's pretty good, especially (for Gilmour) lyrically. Musically, it could be much better, though. Good, but not great, which sort of sums up the album for you right there. Wearing the Inside Out has never impressed me, however. It's boring musically, and lyrically, it's not very good. 

Take it Back was the album's single, and is, not surprisingly, one of the worst songs on the album (albeit better than What Do You Want From Me). It's too radio friendly, and not very interesting conceptually, lyrically, or vocally. You know, I could say the same thing about all the rest of the songs here except for High Hopes, so I will spare myself the typing (the keyboard I'm typing this on warns that I could suffer serious injury if I don't take a break soon...). Honestly, there is little to distinguish them from the rest of the album. 

But then High Hopes kicks in, and you are given the best post Waters Floyd song there is. And it's truly a winner. It's very good lyrically, talking about the splitting of the band, and it's full of veiled references to everything from the Final Cut to Several Species of Small Furry Animals Gathered Together in a Cave and Grooving With a Pict (yes indeed, it does reference this Waters song from Ummagumma). The music is interesting, more so than any other song on the album, and it ends the album in style. The only problem is the little hidden thing at the end, which sort of ruins it for me. Oh well, I can always skip that. 

Overall, this is a very generous three star rating. It's on the low end of three stars, and it's in all honesty that I say I prefer More, Obscured By Clouds, Ummagumma and Atom Heart Mother to this album. They may not be as polished, but the band at least had interesting ideas, and wasn't simply rehashing old formulas, which they started doing with The Final Cut. I give it three largely because it may well appeal to non-Pink Floyd fans, and so it would be wrong to say it's for collectors/fans only.

This is really embarrassing. Another adult contemporary pop album by everyone's favourite art rock band. At least the previous attempt had a couple of nice pop songs ('Learning to Fly' and 'On The Turning Away'). The only ones that come close here are 'High Hopes', 'Poles Apart' (even if it is way too long) and I suppose 'Coming Back to Life' if I was really pressed. 'High Hopes' has quite a nice refrain, while 'Coming Back to Life' is quite an inspiring kind of song (if only it weren't for the terrible production values). If the tracks from A Momentary Lapse of Reason seemed overlong then they are no match compared to this bloated release from the CD age - 66 friggin minutes. Thankfully there are no electronic monsters on this album. Again there are two instrumentals for the hell of it ('Cluster One' and Marooned'), but there are some really terrible pop songs - 'Take it Back', 'Keep Talking' and easily the worst of the lot 'Lost For Words'. Nothing happens for five whole minutes. 'What Do You Want From Me' is an embarrassing attempt to recreate 'Have a Cigar', 'A Great Day for Freedom' is a pointless piano ballad with a few ugly adult production values thrown in (the harmonies mostly). 'Wearing the Inside Out' is yet another dreary Wright song but I would take that above most of the other junk on this album.

Without question the best Pink Floyd album since 'The Wall' (but not as good as David Gilmour's 'About Face', the best post-Wall outing of all, but that's a different story). This album is more assured than 'Momentary Lapse of Reason' and less redundant than 'The Final Cut' (or, 'The Wall II: My Dad's Still Dead'). Songs like 'What Do You Want From Me', 'Poles Apart' and 'Lost For Words' are reflective and obvious allusions to Gilmour's rancorous relationship with the world's largest head, Roger Waters. They are moving and 'Lost for Words' is a very well-crafted Floyd tune. I particularly like the instrumental 'Marooned', which for you trivia buffs would be the only song in the history of the band to have won a Grammy. An odd stat for sure, and pretty much sums up the asinine voting record of the Grammy folks.



The best song on the album is 'High Hopes', which was very memorable in concert, and again reminisces about past rights and wrongs. Perhaps the most interesting tune is Richard Wright's 'Wearing the Inside Out', an autobiographical song which recounts Wright's struggles since the 70's heydays of Floyd. 'Take it Back' and 'Keep Talking' (complete with Stephen Hawking's metallic mumbles) are both solid tunes, whereas 'A Great Day for Freedom' and 'Coming Back to Life' are annoying and feature the cloying vocal and lyrical aspects of Gilmour that I can do without. All in all, a worthwhile listen.

review: Since The Wall was released in 1974, it seems Pink Floyd haven't released any meaningful material, which isn't surprising and is also acceptable given the amount of quality music they gave us before The Wall. Of their few releases since then, The Division Bell is the one that seems to get a bit of attention, but it mostly serves the band the same way that you'd expect from once great bands continuing to release music well past their prime. There is one song that stands out as a classic, a couple songs that are solid (varying depending on the listener) and the rest is music that simply can't escape comparisons to the band's hay day and that's exactly what The Division Bell is comprised of. If you wade through just about an hour of music over 10 tracks, you'll find "High Hopes" at the end of this album and it's the aforementioned classic track and it's actually pretty good, though I don't enjoy it nearly as much as I did when I first came across. For me the only other track that really is worth mentioning is "Marooned". The rest of the album feels off. Of course the lack of Roger Waters is the major reason for this, even if the rest of the band is in tact. The music doesn't feel so atmospheric and brooding and instead feels like it's trying to gain traction of too many different things that almost feel like fads. The basic point here is that it's not an album that's really worth getting into unless you are curious about what Pink Floyd turned into 15 years after their last true hurrah and if you just find "High Hopes" and ditch the rest you'll be good.

The Pink Floyd story limped to a shuddering halt with this album. Really, the band had been on a downward spiral since their last truly classic album Animals, The Wall and The Final Cut, whilst they had their moments, were blighted by Roger Waters' utter control over the band's creative vision causing so much internal acrimony that they barely functioned as a cohesive unit, whilst A Momentary Lapse of Reason was notable more for the legal battle over the Floyd name than for any of its musical content.

Here, Pink Floyd deploy dated production values which went out of fashion a decade earlier (just listen to what they've done to Nick Mason's drums) and toss in generic Floydisms in a half-hearted attempt to recapture past glories. Gilmour's guitar solos are meandering and pointless, the soul influences thrown in here and there are a shameless attempt to remind listeners of the backing singing on Dark Side of the Moon, but when you strip aside all these embellishments at its heart this is a mediocre 80s AOR album recorded in 1994. In short, everyone involved in this project should have been capable of much better, but simply didn't deliver. Like Momentary Lapse, it's cargo cult Pink Floyd, tossing together all the surface elements of past albums in the hope that this would magically yield the Pink Floyd sound of old. It doesn't work.

Hailed as being the closest to "the Pink Floyd sound" (reference intended) since Wish You Were Here, it certainly does have it's  solutions like on that bittersweet dedication to a friend, however, instead of anti-showbiz tracks and, in places, anger we get more polished production and, I'd say, a bit scientific approach to it (the Stephen Hawking sample being the main source of such impression, also the observatory in the booklet).
This is Waters-less Wish You Were Here, which also means lack of his signature lyrics, but with Gilmour and Polly Samson (and Wright on Wearing The Inside Out, I believe) taking the spot, that said, I would like to give them credit for some good lyrics, but of course they would rarely scrape the heights of their ex-svengali. As a songwriter overall, Waters was in-your-face, to put it mildly, Gilmour has always been "cleaner", more easy-going, maybe, as have been Rick and Nick - especially Nick, that third most cheerful drummer in the world after "Moony" and Ringo. Besides, Dave could not always - actually, hardly ever - relate to lyrics of Waters. If only I had that Schaffner's book next to me right now to find to quote about Dave not digging as deep into his past as Roger (I do have the book, but it's at home right now and I'm not)... 
So, I can quite see why some people turned away from Gilmour and co when they decided to carry on without their ex-principal songwriter. And that 80s production on Momentary Lapse... certainly did not pay off.
But I would not completely agree with Waters saying the last two PF albums are a nice try copying him but not good enough. Because, A) it's simply impossible to copy Waters - he's one and only, and B) the remaining trio proved once again that Waters was not Mr Pink Floyd, that them lot certainly did more than just help out, and this time around they simply put forward the best THEY could do - and it's not lyrics, it's melodies that, despite sounding like 90s prog-rockers in places (aka polished, though with potential) and like a U2 number (Take It Back), still haunt much like Shine On You Crazy Diamond.

review:
Up until late 2014, The Division Bell was the final album in Pink Floyd's extensive discography, and was also the second album to be released after the departure of long time singer and multi- instrumentalist, Roger Waters. After the fairly disappointing Momentary Lapse of Reason album, the fans were hoping for a focused and satisfying record. What we got in the end, is an album that combines elements from the classic era Floyd albums, with elements of 80's pop and rock (you may call that redundant, given that the album came out in 1994, although I've never really had a problem with retro sounding albums). The Division Bell is one of the most divisive records in the Pink Floyd discography. Some fans call this a bland rehash of their older material, and some call it a fitting farewell to the progressive rock legends. For me personally, this album is somewhat of a mixed bag. 

While there's several really high quality tracks, that I feel do stand among the band's classics, there are a few tracks that do feel rather rehashed and lacking in originality. The opening track Cluster One is a prime example of this. The first thing I thought upon hearing this opener is that it borrows way too much from the classic that is Shine On You Crazy Diamond, yet unlike that track, Cluster One doesn't really go anywhere, and seems to meander around for a good six minutes, without really going anywhere. Overall, while I don't hate this song, it does feel like a pretty poor choice of an opener. Thankfully however, this doesn't necessarily set the tone for the whole record.

The tracks is immediately followed up by What Do You Want From Me. This song once again takes a little too much influence from classic Floyd, in this case it borrows from Have A Cigar, especially with the keyboards, and the main melody of the track. The backing vocals on here also remind me a lot of previous Floyd backing vocals, with Dark Side Of The Moon in particular. While there's nothing really wrong with bands reusing certain ideas, this song just doesn't bring enough to the table for it to stand out, to me at least.

Thankfully however, the following track Poles Apart is a vast improvement over the first couple of tracks. While this is really just a slightly progressive pop track, it has a particularly hopeful mood, and the guitars have a bright and calm quality to them that remind me a bit of the calmer moments in Rush's music (albeit with a bit more bass). The use of keyboards on this track, as well as the echoey guitars and Gilmours soothing vocals really help to create a dreamy atmosphere that took me back to the classic days of Pink Floyd. In the case of this song though, it doesn't sound completely ripped off from other songs. The only real complaint I have about this track is the mid section. This completely breaks the flow of the song, and really doesn't add much to it. If anything, this part sounds like background music you'd hear on a TV show more than anything. With that being said however, this is still a powerful song, and shouldn't be ignored.

Several other songs on here, with Marooned and Keep Talking being two key examples of this, once again borrow a lot of ideas from previous Floyd classics. Marooned reminds me a good deal of the song Us and Them from Dark Side Of The Moon. While Gilmour's excellent guitar solo, and the powerful piano chords do just about save the song from feeling completely generic. 

Keep Talking is another track that feels a little too derivative for comfort. It's a pretty decent song in and of itself, however, the groove of this song, as well as a lot of the guitar work reminds me a great deal of the song Another Brick In The Wall. This track also features yet more female backing vocals, that feel rather derivative, and even a little cheesy at this point.

Take It Back is also a key highlight for this album. Like Poles Apart, the song is a fairly basic pop song (at least by Pink Floyd's standards), although this track has much more of a groove to it. The song reminds me a lot of the poppier, 80's work of various progressive rock artists. Asia are one band that definitely jump out to me from this track, along with some of the more simplistic moments from Mike Oldfield's music. Once again, it may seem a little strange hearing this kind of style as late as the mid nineties, but at this point on the album, it's a breath of fresh air more than anything. The bright sound of the synths, as well as the backing vocals in the chorus give the song a real drive that many other tracks on this album are lacking.

One other highlight of The Division Bell is the song Coming Back To Life. This song features an absolutely beautiful, clean guitar solo from David Gilmour early on in the track, which is accompanied by a simple, yet very effective keyboard track. Following this, Gilmour's emotive vocals enter the mix, and later on, the rest of the band come in, and the song still retains this same beauty. Yet another extremely memorable guitar solo is featured in the latter half of the song, that pretty much seals the deal. Overall, this is a fantastic track, and is one of the few songs on the album that truly sound as though Gilmour is trying to keep the spirit of Pink Floyd alive, while at the same time, modernising it.

The album closes with the song High Hopes. Despite the somewhat varied quality of the other tracks, the album is worth purchasing for this song alone. High Hopes is easily one of the best tracks Pink Floyd have ever created, and also feels like a perfect epilogue to both the record, and to the band as a whole. Lyrically, this song deals with themes of nostalgia and reflection. I absolutely love the piano part in this song, as it works with the guitars perfectly to create one of the most uplifting tracks in progressive rock music. Gilmour also throws in a guitar solo towards the end of the song that simply solidifies High Hopes as a true Pink Floyd classic. While it's not a very flashy solo, it has more power than many guitar solos played at two or three times it's speed. This track truly feels like the perfect epilogue to a phenomenal career for the band. (Although since October 2014, this has no longer been the case, say what you will about that).

The production on this album does feel a little more balanced than on previous Pink Floyd releases. Albums such as Animals or Dark Side Of The Moon at times sound somewhat thin or top heavy, whereas this album feels a bit more clear and well rounded. While this is only a minor benefit in the grand scheme of things, it does work in this albums favour.

Overall, while The Division Bell doesn't quite live up to the stellar heights of albums like Dark Side Of The Moon or Wish You Were Here, it does still have enough high points to be worthy of the Pink Floyd name. For me personally, I feel that this is one of those albums that you need to take the time to absorb. Despite being a little inconsistent at times, I do feel that this album has gone under the radar a little when compared with the earlier Floydian works. So if you've only heard up to The Wall in Pink Floyd's discography, then give this album a listen, as there really is plenty to like on here.

Sure, "The Final Cut" did feature Gilmour so it's not really a fair fight in the first place but let the record state that I've never liked "The Wall" so the fact that Gilmour tried to pass something even worse than a forgettable sequel to that album (i.e. "The Final Cut") off as Pink Floyd is pretty astonishing. I liked The Division Bell a whole lot when it had just been released. A few days ago, I listened to it with a close friend. She is more than a decade younger than me. She likes The Dark Side of the Moon and Wish You Were Here, but to my (moderate) surprise, The Division Bell was not at all her cup of tea. She didn't like the sound of Gilmour's guitar, or the overall sound production, or this or that. This doesn't mean that she considered The Division Bell a bad album – more like, not music for her generation. To me, this is an interesting thing if anything ever, because The Dark Side of the Moon seems to be more like her generation's stuff, though it is 21 years older.

I was born in the 1970s. Throughout the 1980s, I heard a lot of contemporary music, listened to some, liked some, even loved some of it. Before coming to know The Division Bell, I knew A Momentary Lapse of Reason as well as The Wall and The Dark Side of the Moon. But I also knew albums like 90125 by Yes, Clutching at Straws by Marillion, Images at Twilight by Saga, Grace Under Pressure by Rush, We Can't Dance by Genesis, Eye in the Sky by The Alan Parsons Project, even Keys of the Kingdom (as well as Long Distance Voyager) by The Moody Blues. I felt quite at home with those kind of records. The sounds of Nirvana, Oasis, Green Day and the likes made me feel alienated. It took several years until I began to like or even stand the new alternative rock sounds of the 1990s.

Back to my close friend. She grew up to the sounds of the 1990s and early 2000s. To her, The Division Bell sounded like something that her father might listen to. Again, she likes the '70s Pink Floyd, almost as much as she likes The Beatles. She likes Radiohead more than I do.

The Division Bell is a fine album, though it must be a little too adult oriented. It just has to be. Looking at the numbers, I am more 'adult' than the aforementioned close friend, and I like the album more than she. I can still claim that it is not about being adult or the music being adult oriented, instead, it is about being used to something and not being used to something else. "What Do You Want From Me" still sounds like prime Floyd to me, "A Great Day for Freedom" is beautiful, "Take It Back" works finely and "Keep Talking" has more than a little of a certain charm. But listening to "Coming Back to Life", especially, I have to admit that she was right.

It worked on ‘Welcome To The Machine’, but sounds pointless and too much here. ‘One Slip’ is an ok new wave song, a better one is ‘On The Turning Away’, the album’s most outstanding pretty much radio friendly song.
