For me this along with The Final Cut is the least enjoyable Pink Floyd record of the post Barrett era.

This whole album is uninspiring. Musically, it feels like they were rehashing an old formula, nothing sounds new, and isn't nearly as engaging as the music of the "prime" Floyd era, from Meddle to Animals. Gilmour, it seems, wanted to distance himself from Waters, but he didn't do a good job. Because here's the deal, Waters could write music (though not as well as Gilmour, admittedly) and lyrics, but Gilmour could only write music. The lyrics here are awful. Yes, they're better than the crap we call pop (for popular - which is why we can't trust the masses, but that's another story), but they aren't anywhere near the brilliance of Waters. I understand that each has his limitations, but it does affect the quality of the record. Also, anyone who has ever heard Gilmour's vocals on Wish You Were Here (the song) or Green is the Colour or Julia Dream or Time or any other early era song he sang knows that he is a better vocalist than he shows on this album (excluding On the Turning Away and Sorrow). A New Machine and Dogs of War are both horrendously grating, and Gilmour could have done better with little effort. 

These are not the biggest problems, though. What really bothers me about this album is that there is none of the ingenuity of the other albums. It really feels like there was simply a lack of creativity here by Gilmour. Consider Ummagumma, and consider this album. Ummagumma simply sounds fresher and like more work went into it's creation than did A Momentary Lapse of Reason. I certainly listen to Ummagumma (both discs) a lot more than I do AMLOR. The same holds true for just about any other Pink Floyd album. 

All of that aside, this album is not without merit. On the Turning Away is my favorite off here, largely based on the fact that it isn't bad lyrically (it's good, but not Waters good, which is about the best we can expect from Gilmour), and that it rings true in today's society (go to www.songmeanings.net and look up this song for a true story involving this song - freaky). Learning to Fly is somewhat weak musically, but it's decent lyrically (again, only decent). Some people like Sorrow, and it's certainly not bad, but I'm turned off by the not horrible, not very good lyrics, including the horrendous chorus. 

Definitely do not invest in this album until you've heard everything else of Pink Floyd, including the Division Bell (a better but still weak album, IMO).

There are two good tracks - one of them ('Learning to Fly') is an absolute classic, while 'On the Turning Away' is more of pleasant track than anything else. Otherwise there are pointless slow "rockers" in 'Yet Another Movie' and 'Sorrow', a couple of pleasant instrumentals in 'Signs of Life' and 'Terminal Frost' (remember art rock bands must have instrumentals! otherwise it is not art). The truly horrific songs are the ones attempting to be as modern as possible - either the very poor pop song 'One Slip' or the excrutiating electronic of 'Dogs of War' and the two versions of 'A New Machine' (whether the electronics is in the synths or electronically processed voices it doesn't matter). In fact 'Dogs of War' is easily the worst song ever released under the name Pink Floyd. Even if Syd Barrett in the early years collapsed in the studio and drooled into his microphone for 23 minutes it couldn't be as offensive as this.

review:
Don't get me wrong, I love David Gilmour's input to Floyd and don't devalue him like many of the die-hard Waters fanatics. The Division Bell, for example, is one of my favourite Floyd albums, up there with Meddle, Wish You Were Here and Dark Side of the Moon, higher than the likes of The Wall and Animals (both good, but not my favourites) and the abysmal Final Cut. It's been said a million times before, but Waters was extremely talented, but needed to have a band to keep him in check, and in my opinion some of Floyd's weakest stuff was when he was given full control at the expense of the rest of the band.



The Final Cut failed partly because it was essentially a Waters solo album. Yet when Waters is gone, one realises that this was not a problem unique to Waters, as A Momentary Lapse of Reason fails for the exact same reason: it is essentially a Gilmour solo album, and one Gilmour recorded when he was far from his peak.



The album now lacks direction. The music is bland, not progressive in the slightest, sounding like a bad 80s pop album more than something from one of the greatest bands of the 20th century. The music is unmemorable, with Learning to Fly, One Slip, Yet Another Movie, Terminal Frost and Sorrow all sounding incredibly bland. After listening, the tracks blurred in my mind: they all sound the same. David's voice grates: he is a marvellous singer, yet somehow appears like a constipated donkey on this one record (I would blame age, but he sounds great again on Division Bell).



There are, of course, a couple of standouts. The Dogs of War, voted Worst Pink Floyd Song EVAR!!!, is, in my opinion, fantastic, and signs of greatness on the album, despite David's awful singing (for once, I think this was a track that would have suited Rodge). On the Turning Away is beautiful, but still not even close to the similar tracks on Division Bell.



The Final Cut was an abhorrent album, more a 40-minute rant against Thatcher than a decent piece of music, but at least it had direction, much more artistic merit than this. While the music was dull on the most part, it had beautiful tracks such as The Final Cut and When the Tigers Broke Free, along with solid songs like The Fletcher Memorial Home (despite its disturbing lyrics) and Not Now John. At least Waters made the album for a reason, while Momentary is just "there".



It pains me to do this, as I love David and detest Roger as a person, but having listened to (listed from favourite to least favourite): Meddle, Wish You Were Here, Dark Side of the Moon, The Division Bell, Relics, Piper at the Gates of Dawn, Animals, The Wall, Atom Heart Mother and The Final Cut, Momentary Lapse of Reason clocks in at dead last. And I find it quite revealing that the only two Pink Floyd albums that I actually dislike (all of the rest are 4 stars upwards) are the only two which are essentially solo albums.

If this album was released as a Gilmour solo album the rating average would probably be about a half point higher.

review:
The only Pink Floyd album that actually truly does suck. Proves that Roger Waters was easily the Floyd's greatest asset. David Gilmour can wail on the guitar, but he doesn't have Waters' mind. The lyrics are atrocious, the melodies are safe, and the over-the-top 80s-style production makes it sound like a period piece and WAY more dated than Dark Side or Wish You Were Here some 10+ years before it.

The Dogs of War and Sorrow are pretty good, honestly. But only sometimes.

Sadly, there's a much longer list of terrible songs: On the Turning Away, One Slip, Learning to Fly, Terminal Frost, and those stupid songs with the voice box effects.

David Gilmour takes control of a ship quite literally left to sink by Roger Waters, and although it’s still Pink Floyd (just about) the result is rather surprising, as it seems they’re trying to become more radio-friendly and/or Bruce Springsteen – the later wouldn’t be such a bad thing, if they weren’t meant to be a progressive rock band – or maybe it’s just David trying to be these things, or maybe he’s turning into Roger…

Anyway, this is particularly noticeable with 'The Dogs of War' and 'One Slip' – certainly the latter comes off pretty well, whereas the former doesn’t. The great riff and construction of 'Learning to Fly' has always (circa 2008 anyway) made it a favourite of mine, and the Pink Floyd of old reappears in 'On the Turning Away', 'Yet Another Movie / Round and Around' and 'Sorrow', whilst 'Terminal Frost' is a pleasant intermediary. It seems to be pretty good on balance, but it just isn’t really right at times, unlike follow-up The Division Bell which is much nearer the ‘proper’ Pink Floyd than this is.

While I think Division Bell is the far better of the two post Waters albums, this one isn't bad.  In some ways, it's kind of like Pink Floyd's Union: you recruit the names, use a bunch of studio musicians to actually play the music, and it sells just by the name alone.  Roger Waters called this album a "pretty fair forgery."  I'll agree that it sounds highly stylized and lacking in substance, but it's not bad by any means, even if it is largely a David Gilmour solo record.  Under the circumstances, it's a miracle this album even got made.  There was a big spat between Roger and Dave at this point, because I believe Bob Ezrin was supposed to produce Roger's Radio KAOS album, and instead went to work for Dave on this project.  At least that was part of the massive spat between Roger and Dave.

Easily the weakest Pink Floyd album that I've heard to date.  That's if you even want to call it a Pink Floyd album, seeing as only David Gilmour and Nick Mason were involved.  The 2.0 stars here are largely due to the phenomenal "Learning to Fly".  The somewhat depressing "On the Turning Away" and the lengthy closer "Sorrow" are also good latter-day Floyd tracks, yet apart from these three cuts I could certainly live without the rest of the album.  "One Slip", "Yet Another Movie", and "Terminal Frost" are all completely forgettable, "Signs of Life" is a rather pointless ambient instrumental, and the two-part "A New Machine" is an equally pointless bit featuring Gilmour's processed vocals over goofy synth.  That leaves "The Dogs of War", which is rightfully considered to be the weakest track in Pink Floyd's entire discography.  There's absolutely nothing about this song that I enjoy- the melody is bad, the lyrics are laughable, and the use of saxophone is entirely inappropriate.



Not recommended to anyone apart from Pink Floyd completists.  I'd still suggest trying to get your hands on "Learning to Fly" and "Sorrow" some other way (I know they both appear on the greatest hits collection Echoes...).

Disappointing. I seem to recall reading somewhere that Roger Waters referred to this album as "a David Gilmour solo album in everything but name," and that seems accurate to me. There's a couple of decent tracks - The Dogs of War and Learning to Fly, but the rest feels like filler.

Considering the bitter, seething, and at points totally inexcusable hostility that existed between the Gilmour and Waters camps which overshadowed - and was exacerbated by - the process of recording this album, you could argue that it was something of an achievement to complete and release the album in the first place. Certainly there isn't much to applaud on a musical level. A Momentary Lapse of Reason is a disjointed muddle, containing instrumental atmospheres which don't go anywhere and seem rather unsure of what atmosphere they're aiming for in the first place, interspersed with soulless pop-prog anthems like Learning to Fly.

Overall, the impression I have is of a dilettante's album: a piece which exists not because the artists behind it needed to create something to earn their daily crust, or because they had any particular artistic statement they wanted to make, but simply because they had all this cool recording equipment lying around and happened to be bored one day. Of course, the process of producing the album was much more laboured than that, but that's part of the reason it's such a disappointment: surely, after fighting so many battles and spending so many months in the studio, Gilmour, Mason and Wright should have been able to put together something a little bit more compelling than this? One for the fanboys only.

As one of my friends used to say, the title of this should have been "A Long Term Lapse of Musical Talent".  The band certainly achieves the pre-Wall Floyd sound here, but the creativity, inspiration and fire are gone.  Although Waters rudely dominated the group for The Wall and The Final Cut, those two were far superior to this as they at least demonstrated original ideas and were the work of a band that had something to say!  This sounds like a lazy, middle aged Floyd cover band cluelessly aping the sound with no underlying purpose.  Mostly inoffensive with some halfway nice sounding melodies here and there, so if you're a Floyd fanatic you might find a little to like. I guess after letting Waters handle all of the lyrics and most of the songwriting for years, the remaining members had gone past rusty and become fossilized in terms of their musical creativity.  

Floyd's goal here was to cash in on their name, and they acheived that but the aesthetic content is nonexistent.

So the sheep rose up and overthrew the dog, and the result is a renewed melodic sound that has to be balm to the ears of everyone who left the ship after "Wish You Were Here". The problem is that, as a songwriter, Gilmour doesn't really have anything to say, and the external people writing lyrics can still only take this so far. The lyricist takes control over the band and you have albums that are all about the lyrics and the music is just a backdrop. Then you get rid of the lyricist and you have albums that musically reproduce what a Pink Floyd album should sound like, but there is no substance to them. Shame they couldn't sort it out I guess. Anyway as to this particular effort, "Learning to Fly", "One Slip" are pretty good, "The Dogs of War" and "A New Machine" are embarrassing, and the rest is mostly boring.

I have never been burdened, in listening to this album, by confining my judgment to what Pink Floyd is "supposed" to sound like, and I was not terribly concerned at the time with Roger Waters not being in the band. I kind of liked "Learning to Fly" so I gave it a shot. I connected very strongly with the general mood of the album and even soaked up and slightly reveled in the overproduction. The album came to me at a time in life when I was in a period of heavy transition and potential crisis, and I was possibly pretty depressed. The opening sounds of the solitary oars and mournful guitar directly tapped into my state of mind and I was hooked for good.

I can understand a lot of the criticisms and doubts surrounding the album, but I will always love it, with the exception of "Dogs of War," which has never quite sat well with me.

"A Momentary Lapse of Reason" is the 13th full-length studio album by UK progressive rock act Pink Floyd. The album was released through EMI/Columbia in September 1987. Bassist Roger Waters left Pink Floyd after the release of "The Final Cut (1983)" creating quite a drama in the process but the rest of the band opted to continue recording and touring using the Pink Floyd monicker. "A Momentary Lapse of Reason" is not far from sounding like a David Gilmour solo project though and indeed the writing sessions initially started out as sessions for David Gilmour´s third solo album. David Gilmour felt that the quality of the material and the sound of said material would be better suited to be released under the Pink Floyd monicker. Drummer Nick Mason joined forces with David Gilmour and gave the project a bit more credibility. Keyboardist Richard Wright also plays on the album but only as a session musician. In addition to the three members of Pink Floyd there is a host of guest musicians contributing to "A Momentary Lapse of Reason". Prolific artists like Tony Levin (bass), Carmine Appice (drums) and Bob Ezrin (keyboards, sequencers, percussion). The latter mentioned also functions as co-producer along with David Gilmour.

"A Momentary Lapse of Reason" opens in pretty convincing style with "Signs of Life", "Learning to Fly" and "The Dogs of War", which is also a pretty decent track. Unfortunately the remaining part of the album is also only decent and very seldom does it reach the heights of the first couple of tracks. The tracks often comes off as too polished and uninspired with female soul type backing vocals, cheesy sax playing and a dominant use of eighties synth sounds. There are redeeming features on the album like David Gilmour´s excellent guitar solos and pleasant vocal delivery, but they don´t manage to pull the project enough in the right direction.

The sound production instantly gives away the fact that this is an album recorded in the eighties and in this case it´s not necessarily a good thing. The warmth of earlier releases by Pink Floyd is nowhere to be found on "A Momentary Lapse of Reason", which sounds quite soulless compared to it´s many predecessors. No body wanted Pink Floyd to be over because of Roger Waters departure from the band, but listening to "A Momentary Lapse of Reason" it´s clear to me, that he took the soul of Pink Floyd with him when he left, and that this album should probably have been released as a David Gilmour solo album. It´s definitely the weakest release in the band´s discography and a 2.5 star (50%) rating is warranted.

With Waters gone, this was a chance for Gilmour to show what he had but unfortunately, he didn't have enough to offer. There are some moments that shine with brilliance but much of this is dull and no matter how hard you try, you can't polish a turd. 

On the downside, 'Signs of Life' is of so little interest that it's simply too long, both parts of 'A New Machine' are just pointless and 'The Dogs of War' just plods frustratingly for 3 minutes before it finally gets going. I'm not usually one to take too much of an interest in lyrics bit when the music is ordinary, I'll go to them for inspiration. Unfortunately, some of the lyrics are just clumsy, lame attempts to make lines rhyme and Gilmour's voice does little to improve matters.

On the upside, this album also contains a few of my all time favourites from this band. 'On the Turning Away' is a sublime ballad and has one of Gilmour's finest guitar moments, probably the closest thing to his Comfortably Numb solos. They managed to get an extra couple of minutes out of that one live. 'Sorrow' is the other major highlight. I simply can't play that opening 1:45 loud enough but then I wanna skip to the 6:00 mark to get more solo. 'Learning to Fly' is also one of the better tracks but overall there aren't enough highlights to call this a success although it's not a complete disaster either.

review:
In my quest to complete my listenthrough of the Pink Floyd discography at the ripe age of 15, I had thought these guys could do no wrong. And I'm still not sure if they can, but they can definitely do mediocre. Even though albums like More, Division Bell, and Endless River bore me to bits (despite some key songs which shoot some breath through my comatose body), none have I shown my distaste to more than this stinker.

The title of the album is apt. What happens when you combine a David Gilmour solo album with cheesy 80s synths and a lack of any other Floyd member to balance out his reverberated guitar squeals? Nick Mason was present for the sessions, but as he usually does, he sat in the back and let Gilmour take the reins. Wright was off flying planes or something. The credits he receives in this album are petty at best. Apparently he recorded keyboards for two songs and one of those wasn't even used in favor for the session musician's work. Sad.

Either way, Bog forbid I have to sludge my way through this wreckage again to find kernels to write about, so just trust this much from my memory, the first three tracks are good. That's it. Hell, the only reason I'm writing this review is because I was looking through my ratings, saw this and thought, "jeez, well, I'd hate to go to bed at a reasonable time so let's write a short review on this."

I don't remember pretty much any of the other songs on here. I just remember a lot of new wavey synth assaulting my ears. Oh, but I do remember Sorrow. I guess some people like this track, from what I've pieced together. But I sure as hell can't fathom why. At almost 9 minutes, its the longest cut on this thing and still manages to fit in more 'feel' than your everyday Biden or Weinstein. Solos which last for minutes-- the only thing I 'feel' in these is the narcolepsy kicking in.

Overall, only listen to it if you're a diehard Floyd completionist like me. If not, just listen to The Final Cut and Division Bell (yeah I prefer either of them to this).

review:
Gone were the days of tyranny where the band was dominated by one member and put out Roger Waters solo albums. It was time for a new, kinder era where the band would instead put out David Gilmour solo albums, something of an about face. (I crack me up.)
This gets a lot of crap but I kind of like it. It is a bit of a sellout, but it’s a likeable, sophisticated sellout, like Invisible Touch the year before which I’m sure Gilmour was influenced by. If you insist on your Floyd being twisted and challenging, run away now, but if you’re willing to go along with Gilmour’s smoother, more relaxed mood there’s plenty to enjoy. After two albums where lyrics hogged the spotlight, ambient opener Signs Of Life underlines the return of the importance of SOUND, muttered voices and the sounds of water swirling around as the restored Richard Wright makes wonderfully enchanting noises on his Kurzweil before a tasteful and evocative solo from Gilmour. We’re then into album standout Learning To Fly, probably the most nakedly song commercial song Floyd ever made, but no worse for it. Gilmour’s calm, almost monotone vocal lets the back shine with its sonically silky guitars and great “delicate sound of thunder” percussive effect before those tasty backing singers take us into the gently uplifting chorus.
I’m also a fan of One Slip, which should have been a hit. The crisp and cracking drum production (which has clearly been looking at Phil Collins’ homework) underscores the pithy lyric of an instantly regretted affair and hits home with a searing rhythm guitar and great multi-tracked vocals that accent the melodies. I want to hate the corny On The Turning Away but I can’t – cheesy as it is, this lighter-waving stadium anthem gets me every time with its slow, pleasing build from acoustic nakedness to full-on 80s AOR excess. Similarly, instrumental Terminal Frost gets into Kenny G territory with its car commercial drums and scenery-chewing saxophone solo but it’s so well put together it still wins me over and has me dad rocking.
There are some clunkers though. The sad sacky Yet Another Movie features some off-putting, dated synth sounds and is far too long, doing all it’s going to do in two minutes and carrying on for four more. The tryhard Dogs Of War is also not great, attempting to sound badass with production that really doesn’t suit it. Gilmour’s guitar sounds great on eight minute closer Sorrow, but there isn’t much of a song there.
So I think it’s OK, a Floyd album I can take or leave but on balance I’d rather take. After the bitterness of the past three albums, AMLOR makes a good palate cleanser.

The difficult and tangled relationships between David Gilmour and Roger Waters is the stuff better left to books on psychology and business issues.  Enough to say that by 1983 the non-aggression pact that had allowed the two to co-exist under the Pink Floyd banner had fallen apart.

After releasing a solo album, three years later Gilmour, drummer Nick Mason and keyboardist Richard Wright reunited Pink Floyd.  Waters was not invited to participate leading to years of acrimony, insults and hurt feelings.  Even though he was part of the reunion, keyboardist Wright was unhappy with his second-tier billing and over Gilmour's decision to bring in multiple studio musicians for additional keyboard and synthesizer support.   Billed as the first official Pink Floyd effort following the split, for all intents and purposes, 1987's "A Momentary Lapse of Reason" was a Gilmour solo effort.  Gilmour was responsible for writing, or co-writing the entire album, handling lead vocals, and lead guitar.  Largely slammed by critics and still view with varying degrees of scorn by some Floyd faithful, to my ears the set wasn't that bad.  Waters' acidic contributions were clearly missed, but on tracks such as 'Dogs of War', the delicate 'On the Turning Away' and 'One Slip' Gilmour managed to create a similar dark and brooding world look.  His smooth voice, spidery guitar and atmospheric compositions ensured that that band's instantly recognizable sound remained largely intact.  The set's supposed commercial orientation was also subject debate.  Lots of hardcore fans were appalled to see radio embrace 'Learning To Fly' and 'On the Turning Away.'  Personally I don't see much merit in the stance.  I 'd argue Gilmour always had an ear for the commercial, so nobody should have been surprised or upset by the LP's massive success.   

And yes, the late Storm Thorgerson's cover art was stunning.  No Photoshop effects here.  Columbia Records actually spend over $500,00 buying and placing 700 beds along a strip of California coast line.  It took two weeks to lay the beds out and when they were ready to start taking photographs they had to wait as it started to rain.  The results were worth the wait.

This can't decide if it wants to be cheesy 80s pseudo new wave or a very bad impression of how this band was in the 70s, the result is ear pain.

1/10

An extension of Gilmour's solo work that in itself is not interesting at all, the band has lost its personality completely. 

The first track and the interludes "New Machine" are attempts to evoke any old feeling of experimentalism or something, "One Slip" is an embarrassing pop rock, "Dogs of the War" turns the band's lyric into a joke, "On the Turning Away", "Yet Another Movie" and "Sorrow" seem to be a single, boring, endless song, a bunch of bullshit and some guitar solos, "Learning to Fly" and "Terminal Frost" have their charms, they are simple, decent songs lost here.

Overall the album is the weakest release of the band so far, Gilmour is the only musician whose presence is notable on the disc and yet he does not stand out in a positive way, a Trojan horse for fans of the band.

This review refers to the 1997 Doug Sax remaster

Music: Like The Division Bell, this one gets slammed by many Floyd fans and I bet most of them are in the Roger Waters' camp. So what if Rick had little input, he is being eased back into the fold gently and treated with respect again - something Waters long ceased doing. Nick Mason also had little involvement, a fact since admitted by Dave Gilmour. I have loved this album from the day it came out and have always felt that it has a typical Floyd sound, it is so much more than a Gilmour solo album, even if it amounts to such. It is a strong and excellent collection and only with "Dogs of War" does the quality drop slightly. I have always been a Gilmore fan and his guitar work on this album is a treat throughout, always soaring, majestic and melodic. Without the overbearing influence of Waters, Gilmour is set free to write some great melodies and with "On the Turning Away", he delivers one of the greatest ever Floyd tracks, and certainly one of the greatest ever rock songs. The solo on this track is every bit as good as his one in the Floyd fans perennial favourite, "Comfortably Numb". 
This is a refreshing Floyd with a real input of positivity, free from the doom-laden misery of the previous, self-appointed leader. The King is dead - Long live the King! Superb!

Mastering / Sound Quality: This Doug Sax remaster is a significant improvement over the other CD version that I own, the original 1987 issue (http://rateyourmusic.com/release/album/pink_floyd/a_momentary_lapse_of_reason_f3/). I had previously often used the aforementioned disc as a test disc when auditioning audio gear and I can see now the error of my ways. In comparison that disc is way back in the mix and a little muffled in the mid range and bass. This new remaster brings the soundstage forwards and opens up the music. There is cleaner, deeper bass and the acoustic guitar sings with clarity. Another one I unreservedly recommend to someone wanting just one version of this album.

Roger Waters was a great bass player who really understood the instrument, and he wrote really good lyrics in the days before fame went to his head. The first Waters-less Pink Floyd album gets shat on a lot, but I find it way more enjoyable than The Wall and The Final Cut. Bass duties are handled by the great Tony Levin, the lyrics are fairly bland and easy to ignore, which is a good thing because they don’t distract from enjoying the music. The music is fantastic, the sound is very deep and subtly moving, it engulfs you like an ocean wave. 



I am really glad that I finally got myself to listen to this album with a fresh ear. With Roger’s departure, the narrative edge is gone, but I surely don’t miss hearing that war is bad, mmmkay, and how terribly hard it is to be a rich rock star. I have been a lifelong PF fan, and I really appreciate that they recorded this 50-minute set of gorgeous songs that I can melt my mind into.

There comes a record every once in a while that seems so utterly unnecessary, so completely uncalled for, that one has to wonder what motivations were there behind releasing it - because seemingly, there are none. The Pink Floyd name had no need to be revitalized, and even less so by David Gilmour, who was not even an original member and never the heart of the group artistically. So why did he decide to slap the Floyd name on what is ostensibly, and by most metrics, a solo record? Greed for money? A need to show Roger Waters whats what? Just cuz?

Who knows, but it certainly doesn't deserve to be called a Pink Floyd album. And I'm no Floyd purist - I couldn't care less, but this has almost nothing to do with the band, ship of Theseus be damned.

Speaking of the album itself, what we have here is something so impressively lackluster that its paradoxically easy to talk about. Usually records like this leave me shrugging but there is SO MUCH here that I want to crap on. 

Firstly, there doesn't seem to be a single worthwhile musical idea present - not a single melody, lyric or riff stuck in my mind, and this must be the third time listening to this. In fact, Gilmour seems interested in ripping off just about every previous idea covered by the Floydsters, but so much worse. "The Dogs of War" goes so far to try to rip off Waters that it has a freudian slip in the title of which specific song its going for. Also present here are vibes from "Welcome to the Machine" and "One Slip" does its darnest to re-do the intro to "Time". 

Musically its a slog to boot, with repetitive mid-tempo drums and bland synth mats blandly blanding all over the place with no seeming necessity except to set some sort of "mood" but no one remembered to ask what mood they were going for. Sure, David Gilmour is a talented guitarist, but none of that talent is present here, as although every song (and I mean every song) ends with a endless solo jam, each solo grinds more than the last. Perfunctory is not only the word, it is the mantra and the mission here. As a plus, not only are they going through he motions, they are doing it poorly, as at times Gilmour's playing is uncommonly amateurish. It would genuinely surprise me if any of these solos were Take 2.

The fact that his vocals are then tasked with taking even more responsibility to save the record doesn't help. Gilmour was never an exceptional vocalist - just always a functional one, with occasional great moments. Here, front-and-center, he does not stack up at all. Weak and without much motivation, not helped by the poor melodies and lyrics, he sounds like someone way out of their depth. 

It actually shocked me to remember his record was from 1987 - there was something so mid-nineties about it. Perhaps the aura of washed-up previously great rock star dishing out a bloated unnecessary mess is something that reminds me more of the early 90s. In that case - ahead of its time.


Just one more thing - smashing cover, so smashing in fact, that it hardly belongs on a record this snooze-inducing. Then again, maybe Storm Thorgerson was trying to tell us something - what with the main element of the cover being thousands of beds.

A Momentary Lapse of Reason is despised among many for effectively being a half-assed David Gilmour solo project masquerading under the Pink Floyd name, but like other reviewers I find it to be Not That Bad™. I never liked Roger Waters in the first place, despite his occasional laudable political stances I find his ego and pretensions to be totally repellent, and The Wall is basically unlistenable to me as a result, so Waters's absence on A Momentary Lapse of Reason isn't necessarily missed. This album is definitely a synth-heavy AOR-ified version of Pink Floyd, but Pink Floyd's glory days were already fairly synth-heavy and proto-AOR-ified, so again that's not that much of a loss. The worst thing you can say about A Momentary Lapse of Reason is that its title is its own punchline and that overall the record feels fairly inessential in a way that you don't want from a Pink Floyd album. But there are worse things than being inessential, and A Momentary Lapse in Reason is far from being an unlistenable disaster. Put it on in the right mood with the right inclinations and it might even sound pretty decent.

Pink Floyd's album A Momentary Lapse Of Reason is an exceptionally good album.  It was done by only Gilmour, Mason, and Wright.  Roger Waters left the band along with his ego hoping they'd flounder, and when that didn't work he tried blocking and suing them over use of the band's name.

Waters lost, and the band and fans won as the album features the stellar self penned songs:

- Learning To Fly
- One Slip
- On the Turning Away
- Dogs Of War
- Sorrow

As good as the album and song writing are, Pink Floyd finally showed they were human by making an album that sounded like previous efforts.  An amazing feat they avoided in the 1970s during their heyday.  One Slip seems similar to Run Like Hell, On The Turning Away sounded like Comfortably Numb, etc.

Regardless, it's still an excellent album overall (and much better than Water's ego obsessed The Final Cut).  And the band giving Waters the middle finger is classic.

Yes, success is the best revenge.

Nobody likes a big ego. I'd wager that it's a huge turn off to most people if it comes through in an artist's music. Look at Bono or Axl Rose. You can add Roger Waters to that group too. The thing is if you've got an inflated ego then the music better be good because you're not getting any bonus marks for charm or likeability. Let's face it, I've no interest in going to the pub with Waters, that would probably severely bum me out. I'll happily give the man's music a listen if it's any good though.



So is it? Because as anyone who's ever had even a passing interest in this album will tell you, this is Waters' baby. Even moreso than The Wall. Well, yes, I like it. It's flawed and Rog's ego definitely shines through but it has a naked honesty to it that makes up in part for all of that. In a similar way to another famous ego's work: John Lennon / Plastic Ono Band.



Musically this is virtually unrecognisable as being a Pink Floyd record. Wright's gone and Gilmour - production aside - has no presence at all, his contributions feeling more like cameos. So it's all on Waters.



The biggest flaw I guess is that the album comes across as being overdramatic on a few occasions, which is understandable when you consider the subject matter that he's taking on. "When The Tigers Broke Free" is Waters finally dealing directly with his Father's death (after years of it informing his work on a more implicit level), and it's a grandiose extended metaphor, made as it is to sound like a war salute. "Not Now John" meanwhile has gospel singers chanting "fuck all that", which is just bizarre.



The strength is in the lyrics though. Take these from "The Gunner's Dream"...



Good-bye Max, Good-bye Ma. 

After the service when you're walking slowly to the car 

And the silver in her hair shines in the cold November air 

You hear the tolling bell, and touch the silk in your lapel 

And as the tear drops rise to meet the comfort of the band 

You take her frail hand, and hold on to the dream



which is probably the star of the show, featuring some respectfully delicate piano. The title track is another stand-out, as is the closer... but really if you can get past this not being a Floyd album and accept that Waters is gonna sing about war for 3 quarters of an hour then you should be able to take something from it. Some may call it pretentious but as far as I'm concerned it's one of the least pretentious Floyd albums. Over the top maybe, I can see that. But there's no deep concept here, just a series of unusually intimate character accounts and an obvious distaste for war.

Wow. I liked all the popular 1970s Pink Floyd albums a lot during my middle school years, but somehow, their two best albums - Piper at the Gates of Dawn and The Final Cut -  escaped me at that point in my life. I guess I got sick of the pomp and the pretentiousness, and fell out not only with Pink Floyd, but with classic rock as a whole. As my music tastes became more obscure, I wrote off Pink Floyd completely as uninteresting stadium rock. 

Later on I discovered Syd Barrett's solo work, which led me back to early Floyd, which I like a great deal. Then today, I found The Final Cut on cassette for $0.25. I'd heard some interesting things about it, but didn't really know what to expect and assumed I'd probably dislike it, but would be willing to waste a quarter to find out.

The first thing that struck me was how fantastic and hauntingly evocative the lyrics were. The only album that has matched the lyrical quality for me is Neutral Milk Hotel's "In the Aeroplane over the Sea," but while that album's fantasy-laden symbolism is far more poetic than Waters' lyrics, Waters' lyrics feel more honest because the death of his father in World War II seems to have haunted him his whole life, and this album is finally expelling his suffering to the world. 

The second thing that struck me was how unpretentious this album is. The darkness of The Wall was always far more interesting than the other mainstream stadium rock acts of that era, but I can't really stand the pomp and egoism that fills that album. Thus, why "Not Now John" is by far the nadir of this album for me. Everything about that song feels entirely unnecessary, evoking everything I hate about "The Wall" or "Dark Side of the Moon." Which, I'm sure, is why many fans love it so much, but I'm a contrarian, I guess. 

Besides that atrocity and a few unfortunate saxophone solos that really date this album immediately, this album goes light on excesses like guitar solos and rock-star posturing. Mostly it's pianos, acoustic guitar and strings, done far more effectively than the best songs on The Wall did. The lyrics also pull the album to a close, personal level. I think the album would be even better without the "rock" parts, because the ballads are so powerful by themselves. The exception to this is "One of the Few," which manages to finally make a stadium rock song that is also really good - the psychedelic flourishes are unexpected at this point in their career, but they work really well. The whole first side is really awesome, as is most of the second half, without the aforementioned shite song. The closer "Two Suns in the Sunset" is marred by saxophone, but otherwise excellent.

OK, it's a Roger Waters solo album - and it has all the personal closeness of a solo album that bands can rarely capture. It's an intensely personal statement, and I wish only that it had been completely undiluted by the rest of the band's footprint. The real failure of this album is that it is not quite a solo album nor is it quite a full band album, which makes it feel somewhat disorganized. The concept is solid, but I feel it would have been an immediate classic without the Gilmour guitar solos, sax solos, stadium rock bridges and other Pink Floyd stereotypes. I kind of wish there would be a remix of this album stripped down to it's core. It didn't work at all for the Beatles' "Let It Be...Naked," because that album had no substance anyway. The melodies (or for Floyd fans, the rest of the band) here certainly aren't what keep it afloat, which is why the this album would be truly fascinating to hear in a minimalist form - stripped to just piano, some strings and synths and his voice - which is the focus of the album, anyway.

The most under-rated Pink Floyd album by far and possibly the best conceptually and lyrically. There is a whole lot going on here: politics, family relationship, personal relationships, war, nationality. The best part is that they're all intertwined and connected with a strain of emotional passion and conviction that makes this unlike any other Pink Floyd album.

The vocal delivery and lyrics in some of these songs ranks amongst the best ever recorded. A brilliant, albeit very divisive, album. This is not just the album after The Wall and before Waters left, it is a great album that coincidentally is by Pink Floyd.

review: A true lost gem, this began (reportedly) as a Roger Waters solo effort, but the band was good enough to play along.  This plays like The Wall, Pt. 2.  The story isn't the same, as this focuses on Waters's own experiences, having lost his father to WWII, but the style of the songs is very reminiscent of its immediate predecessor.  While this doesn't quite measure up to that album, it still has moments of true Pink Floyd greatness (again, not the kind they play on the radio), with tracks like "The Post War Dream," "When the Tigers Broke Free," "The Fletcher Memorial Home," and "Not Now John."  When a casual (The Dark Side of the Moon, The Wall, Wish You Were Here) fan asks me for a recommendation, I'll usually point out that, if they liked The Wall, then this album is a natural follow-up.

An amazingly sudden fall from greatness for Pink Floyd.  Whereas the band's previous The Wall is widely accepted as one of the greatest concept albums of all-time, The Final Cut is decidedly mediocre and made practically zero impact upon the musical scene of the early-80's.  This album perfectly demonstrates the dangers of valuing message over interesting musical content, a pitfall that Floyd miraculously avoided for much of The Wall despite that album's similarly hard-hitting theme.  Nearly every track here is overly wordy, with the listener being repeatedly hit over the head by Roger Waters' anti-war lyrics.  Perhaps more frustratingly, the band's always-impressive instrumental section is largely reduced to textural duties and oftentimes takes backseat to a full orchestra.  David Gilmour's guitar playing is especially absent.  The only notable guitar solo comes at the end of the title track (hardly a highlight), and for much of the album it sounds as if Gilmour isn't even trying.  This is hardly surprising, given the rift that had grown between Waters and Gilmour at this point in the band's career.  And this album was obviously Roger Waters' baby.



The Final Cut is also a significantly more subdued (many would say more boring) affair than The Wall.  Nearly every song is a laid-back ballad and, although the music is consistently well-crafted, memorable melodies are few and far between.  The obvious highlight "When the Tigers Broke Free"- one of the few places where the symphonic rock approach is used to great emotional effect (retelling the death of the protagonist's father in WWII).  Another standout is "Not Now John", if only because it is easily the most energetic thing here, but even that track is marred by annoying backing vocals a la Dark Side of the Moon's "The Great Gig in the Sky".  The rest of the songs are consistently decent but offer me little motivation to listen to the album in its entirely.  Taken as a whole, The Final Cut is a tedious and somewhat depressing listen.  At least it was able to avoid the horrific missteps that sank The Momentary Lapse of Reason- the "Pink Floyd' album recorded by Gilmour following the departure of Waters and Richard Wright.



One of the weakest entries in Pink Floyd's discography.  Although far from horrible, The Final Cut is best left to Floyd completists or die-hard fans of The Wall.

review:
I've tried not buy this album for years, but I'm a neurotic completist and its on I-tunes, so I made the plunge, and while the album itself isn't so bad, like the Wall, this isn't a real Pink Floyd album, but a Roger Waters solo album.  What does this mean?  It's strident, overbearing, obvious, musically limited, declaimed rather than sung, and ultimately a minor bore.  I'm not complaining about Water's material, but in his execution. David Gilmour is mixed out here, and of course Nick Mason's contribution is diluted by studio musicians.  Where is Richard Wright?  His keyboard work and vocals made the Pink Floyd sound from the very beginning.  I guess he didn't like being flogged or being forced to comp somebody else's ideas to the extent that Water's required.  

This is not to say that The Final Cut is worthless, or even that bad-it's not. There are even some good songs on the album, but it's not really Pink Floyd.

It took me several listen to come to a conclusion about this album, but here's what I've got:

First of all, it's depressing as hell. In a bad way. The Wall was depressing but emotionally challenging and intriguing, so it was okay. TFC is just sad for the sake of being sad. Second, it's a little disconnected. I think there's more than one story going on in the album because some of the tracks seem connected only to certain other tracks and not to others. And third, there's really no standout great tracks like on The Wall or DSOTM or even Wish You Were Here. There are a few tracks that are nice, like the title track, The Post War Dream, and Not Now John, but none of them earned regular rotation on my music schedule. 

Overall, a little disappointing. 3.5 stars. It will possibly be raised to 4 after listening a few more times, but no promises.

WATERS NO SUPO RETIRARSE A TIEMPO

Un amigo mío insistía, en una de esas confesiones musicales que solíamos compartir a menudo en esos maravillosos años de la vida de estudiante, en que tenía una serie de álbumes fetiche, perfectos, de concepto, que se podían escuchar como una canción compuesta. "Year of the cat" de Al Stewart o "Famous last words" de Supertramp eran algunos de los elegidos. De Pink Floyd, por supuesto, tenía a "Dark side of the moon" en un altar, cosa que creo que cualquier melómano minimamente entendido comparte o entiende, pero acompañándolo situaba sin discusión a "The final cut". No estaba ni siquiera bien tratado por la crítica, pero a él le fascinaba...

Después de haberlo escuchado buen número de veces y de haber intentado, sin éxito, que la fibra sensible se me activase retroactivamente, he decidido al fin desistir de entender "The final cut". Y mira que los Floyd casi nunca entran a la primera y hay que dedicarles tiempo para que sus enseñanzas se te metan poco a poco en la sangre que va hacia tu corazón, pero ni por esas en este caso.

Pink Floyd debió poner punto final a su aventura con el mastodóntico canto a la desesperación y la paranoia que supuso "The wall". "The final cut", marcado por las agrias disputas entre Waters y los demás miembros del grupo (con expulsiones incluidas y juicios venideros), trata de llevar más allá las ambiciones del bajista e ir más allá en el concepto, esta vez recurriendo a la guerra como tema central. Pero sin éxito, ya que las composiciones no funcionan en su mayoría y muchas recuerdan desgraciadamente esquemas ya oídos en su exitoso antecesor. Sonido ampuloso y más orgánico y compacto, sí, pero, aparte de la historia de guerra que a retales nos intenta contar Waters, "The final cut" fracasa absolutamente no sólo como réplica y continuación de la inspiración que se suponía a un conjunto de space-rock ya legendario, sino incluso como disco menor o anécdota interesante en la que vale pararse unos minutos, tal como pasaba con "Meddle" o "A saucerful of secrets". Y, qué quieren que les diga, los helicópteros, niños, cristales rotos, aviones de guerra y el profesor iracundo me resultan gimnicks más divertidos como aderezo de "The wall", que la grandilocuencia presente en todo este álbum, denotante de una ausencia meridiana de inspiración.

Ya no se trataba de distinguir entre Gilmouristas y Wateristas y de organizar debates en torno a la importancia de uno u otro miembro en la banda, sino de que Pink Floyd había sido incapaz, por primera vez en su historia, de responder a las expectativas de su exigente  y fiel público con más creatividad. Y ya no hubo vuelta atrás: Los dinisaurios habían aparecido...

I think there are a couple of issues that hold this album back from really being good.  First, and perhaps most succinctly describable: it's one-dimensional.  Essentially, the album is a collection of ballads of varying length punctuated by a couple of mid-tempo "rockers" (if you're being generous), so it's extremely downbeat--even by Pink Floyd's standards.  Likewise, the sonic palette is takes little advantage of the band's textural and tonal capabilities--it's mostly composed of unassuming guitar and piano lines and lead vocals are almost exclusively undertaken by Roger Waters, which makes for a homogeneous but but variety-free sound.  

This leads me to the second issue, which is a bit more difficult to sum up but probably more important.  When talking about Pink Floyd, it's easy but unproductive to get caught up in trying to quantify the weight of each band member's contributions and whether the band really IS the band if it doesn't include Rick Wright, Roger Waters, etc.  As far as the Final Cut is concerned, and without getting political, it can be dispassionately observed that Roger Waters is essentially the sole creative contributor, for better or worse.  The problem with that fact, in my opinion, is that he had very little to say--musically and lyrically--that he hadn't recently expressed on Animals and The Wall.  So we get songs like "One of the Few," which is obsessed with damaged veterans taking it out on schoolkids, "Paranoid Eyes" and "The Final Cut," which tread the same ground as most of the inward-probing isolation material from The Wall, and a whole host of anti-war songs that rather unnecessarily develop Waters' obsession with his father's WWII death (already mined in "Goodbye Blue Sky," "Another Brick in the Wall, pt. 1," and probably most effectively on "When the Tigers Broke Free").  Likewise, the music sounds like outtakes from The Wall--dark, often brief, and short on hooks aside from a few stereotypical Gilmour guitar solos.  I'd even venture to say that Waters just didn't have enough material to warrant a new release, but maybe he just needed to get it out of his system.

The Final Cut isn't devoid of redeeming qualities--it's undeniably effective in its mood and message, there's a fair number of memorable lyrics, some undeniable songcraft, and even a few good melodies, but the thing is such a goddamn downer and not close enough in quality to early PF works or later Waters solo albums to justify many listens.

This is one of those albums where you can't listen to it once. But when you listen to it again and again you just get so into it. You realize how perfect and beautiful each of the solos are and there's just so many great things happening...the slow starts to songs leading to intense endings. It's like One of My turns off The Wall made into a whole album. Some people might not like songs like paranoid eyes or southampton dock or the short songs, but I assure you when you listen to the album as a whole they GREATLY advance the whole thing. This album, in my opinion is greatly deserving of a higher rating, and is probably only as low as it is from all the people who skim through it or just give it one listen to. There are just these moments of every song that you look forward to with every listen. For instance, Post War Dream for the most part is a soft song, until the last little bit where it turns into the most amazing ending of a song i've probably ever heard. Then you wait for the amazing chorus of your possible pasts (not to mention the solo). And then there's this absolutely amazing part in the gunner's dream where water's sings "And hold on to the dream!" The last note he sings on "dream" turns into an amazing saxophone solo. There are just amazing little bits of perfected musicianship scattered throughout this album and it carries this sad, sad, feeling...but at the same time it's extremely liberating. Just give it a listen, if you like pink floyd, or you love music...you'll definitely appreciate this album.

review:
As if one  Wall wasn't enough, it was succeeded by another album that feels like a pure definition of overblown pompousness. The Final Cut hits us with another showcase of Waters' megalomaniacal madness.

There are surely some nice instrumentals here and there (though there is nothing that would sound particularly fresh or original), but Waters' pretentious screaming mixed with his equally pretentious mumbling (which are present in literally every track on this album) just start to get unbearable at some point. The preachiness in Roger's lyrics doesn't help as well.

The only advantage The Final Cut has over The Wall is that it is in fact shorter, wasting less of your time and giving you a smaller chance to eventually get bored to tears. It also looks like Roger finally decided to leave Syd alone, which is a nice gesture after years of comercially exploiting your friend's personal tragedy.

However in contrary to it's predecessor, this album doesn't really feature any stand-out tracks. Some songs may feel kinda nice when played separately, but when you listen to the whole album they just blend together into one boring, secondary mess. There are no hits like "Comfortably Numb" or the three parts of "Another Brick..." that would grab your attention for at least a moment. It's really hard to listen to this whole thing carefully and not fall asleep.

In summary i would say that if you don't need Roger Waters complaining about his loss and convincing you that "war is bad" for 45 minutes in your life, you probably want to avoid this album. There are much worse albums in the history of music, but it doesn't make this one less of a waste of time.

15/40

A great Pink Floyd album. Though, it may be more apt to call it a Roger Water's album with guest performances by David Gilmour and Nick Mason. Either way, it is still good. 

The album begins with The Post War Dream. Beginning slow, it then grows into a song talking to Margaret Thatcher ("Maggie") about the dream for the United Kingdom after WW2. 

After Your Possible Pasts and its guitar solo and the short song One of the Few, we get to my least favorite song on the album and the reason why this album isn't rated 5 stars, The Hero's Return. This song has goofy sounding parts such as the deep voice talking while Roger Waters sings his lyrics which sounds strange. The song sounds a little strange for me to like. However, at the end of the song there is a neat acoustic guitar part which I like.

The Gunners Dream is great, from the lyrics to that fabulous saxophone solo, and the explosive ending. 

Get Your Filthy Hands Off My Desert is a short, political, pretty song that transitions into the also political The Fletcher Memorial Home. Although the rest of the song is good, the song deservedly gets most of its praise for its excellent guitar solo by Dave Gilmour. 

That then goes into Southhampton dock, which is effectively two songs stitched into one, a standalone song, and a transition track into the album's peak: the title track, The Final Cut. This song is very sad but pretty at the same time. It also contains a short guitar solo by David Gilmour. 

We then get into the end stretch of this great album. The fastest paced and heaviest song on the album, Not Now John. This song is fastpaced and angry sounding. It is also the only song on the album to feature vocals by David Gilmour. 

Finally, the album ends with the dreary song about a nuclear end to life as we know it, Two Suns in the Sunset, title referencing a the explosion of a nuclear bomb. The song describes the final moments in a man's life as he and presumably many others die in an atomic bomb's explosion. 

Overall, I very much enjoy this album. Other than the flaw I mentioned above, I think the songs are all great. Ignore the bad average rating, this album is great.

The Final Cut is an interesting album by Pink Floyd due to its sheer existence alone. Doomed to live in the shadow of its predecessor, it’s often seen as a footnote by many people, a mediocre spin-off/sequel to The Wall. An assessment which, while dismissive, is honestly justified given the history of this album’s making. The album was originally going to be called Spare Bricks. It was meant to be a collection of songs cut from The Wall, some which are referenced in the movie (such as "Your Possible Pasts"), downright included in it (such as "What Shall We Do Now?" or "When the Tigers Broke Free"), or alternate versions of songs from the movie (like "Bring the Boys Back Home" and "Outside the Wall"). It was only after the breakout of The Falklands War where  Roger decided to scrap the Spare Bricks concept and focus the album more on the war protest concept, writing new songs for it.



Interestingly, "When the Tigers Broke Free" wasn't even on the album originally. It was released as a single b/w "Not Now John" to promote the Pink Floyd: The Wall movie (evidencing the latter’s origin in the Spare Bricks project). It was only added to the tracklisting in a 2004 reissue, shoehorned in between two songs where them being next to each other is pretty fucking important for the flow of the story. Both "One of the Few" and "The Hero's Return" are about the teacher from "The Happiest Days of Our Lives"/"Another Brick in The Wall, Part 2". They add onto the greater universe of The Wall by fleshing out one of the more important characters in the story, revealing that, to an extent, the reason for his abuse comes from PTSD from the war. Meanwhile, "When the Tigers Broke Free" is very unambiguously about Pink and the loss of his father. This is also reinforced by the film.



"One of the Few", "The Hero's Return", and "When the Tigers Broke Free" have their origin in the earliest stages of The Wall concept. While "One of the Few" (first titled "Teach") had similar lyrics to what we know nowadays, the demo for "The Hero's Return" (originally titled "Teacher, Teacher") has completely different lyrics, framed as an argument between Pink and the teacher. Interestingly, however, the lyrics from the second verse of the demo (sung from Pink's perspective) are mirrored somewhat by "The Hero's Return (Part 2)" (sung also by the Teacher's perspective), which is exclusive to the B-side of the "Not Now John" single, not being officially reissued at all to this day. I imagine the reason it's not on The Final Cut even to this day is because it comes right after the "gunner's dying words" line on "The Hero's Return", so including it would break up the story, which makes the sequencing of "When the Tigers Broke Free" all the more puzzling and ironic.



The reason I imagine they included it on The Final Cut decades later has to do with the fact that, alternate versions notwithstanding, "When the Tigers Broke Free" was the only Pink Floyd song commercially released from their golden years (which I consider to be 1973-1983) to not be on any album. Despite it being tied closely to the concept of The Wall, a lot more people are passionate about The Wall than they are about The Final Cut, so in a way including it on the latter was something of a compromise, just to have the song somewhere without sacrificing the "purity" of the album that everyone cares about oh so much. Where they put it though is downright stupid. Personally, I would've put it in between "Paranoid Eyes" and "Get Your Filthy Hands Off My Desert" if I absolutely had to have it in the album. It's right in the middle of the album, in a part where it's ultimately non-invasive in its placement, given the disconnect in the narrative between those two tracks. Though in a perfect world, by now we would've gotten an official extended, uncut version of The Wall with all the cut instrumental sequences and cut tracks where Roger intended them to be.



On the topic of how The Wall and The Final Cut relate to each other, it's really interesting how The Final Cut is something of a Frankenstein's monster of an album due to the nature of its development. It’s comprised of that have their origins in the Wall concept, such as "When the Tigers Broke Free" and "The Final Cut", others that were written entirely for The Final Cut's war concept, such as "The Post-War Dream" and "Get Your Filthy Hands Off My Desert", and some that were presumably written for Spare Bricks, such as "The Fletcher Memorial Home" and "Not Now John". It's an amalgam of different songs written across roughly half a decade, with different intentions in mind. It has its own identity, but is also constrained to being something of an amendment to The Wall. 



All that, not to mention the burnout of the band, having worked on this singular grand concept for years, the tension with Roger gaining full control over the band, and  Rick's absence, all led to The Final Cut being, at least to the other two members and I imagine Roger to some extent a "get it done, get it out of the way" type of album, which is a real shame because there's some great songs there. While musically it's far from my favorite Floyd album (I'm a Syd Barrett fan through and through), it displays Roger's ability to write intriguing characters and stories about the chaos and tragedy that comes with war. It does have its musical high moments though, the dramatic sax solo in "The Gunner's Dream" is one of my personal favorites. Lyrically, it's one of the Floyd's heaviest records, but the lack of much artistic and musical contribution by the rest of the group and the ultimate identity crisis that the album represents makes the feeling that many Floyd fans of this being a lackluster album pretty understandable. It's an acquired taste to say the least.



Personally, and excuse me for my pretentiousness here, The Wall and everything surrounding it has taken up so much of my headspace for years as I put time into researching it, picking it apart, and even making my own extended mixes (my latest of which is over 3 hours long, including pretty much everything I could've shoved into it), that I can't help but love The Final Cut in a way. It's another piece of a greater puzzle that Roger created when he spat on that fan in 1977. Nowadays I don't listen to The Wall, The Final Cut, or even Pink Floyd as much as I used to, but it's something that has stuck with me and compelled me for so long that it went as far as driving me to writing a whole fucking essay’s worth of a review for it.

I own every single Pink Floyd album ever made and this one is by far the best.  It is a complete masterpiece of musical poetry and in that regard not even _The Wall_ can match it.  I have listened to it a couple of thousand times and I'll never get tired of it.



What strikes me about this album is the way in which the lyrics interact with the music.  On several occassions this is depicted by the music changing tone for instance when a certain word or concept is uttered by the vocals and vice versa.  The music also "depicts" the tone inherent in the lyrics.  For instance "Two suns in a sunset" is divided into three parts musically...the first part deals with dreamy observations lyrically, followed by "the accident" and the "aftermath"...in all three sequences the music carries its own message, a message complimenting the metaphors in the lyrics perfectly.



In all of my life I have NEVER heard any other album, apart from another Pink Floyd album that contained the same type of mastery, for that alone, whether you like this album or not, it deserves 5 stars.  This is not just a collection of songs...it is a musical adventure that has its own inherent meaning if taken in totality.  



This is not mood music, nothing you can dance to, there are no catchy riffs or upbeat choruses.  It breaks all the rules inherent in popular music and is totally unique in the wrold of music.  The ONLY way to listen to it is to close your eyes, visualize the imagery and be transported by it...it is not meant to be shared either...it is a solo journey.

I don't see this album as evidence that Roger had finally lost his mind & became a tyrant. I see more like this: the other members of the band had completely checked out & he was the only one offering anything worthwhile. He had no effective check against him
