The Final Cut. By far the most underrated album of all time. Gets a ton of crap for being a Roger Waters solo album and having emotion in it. Well, for the final project in my AP Language class (my Junior year English class), we had to write about a memorial, and I chose this album as that memorial, to both Waters' father and what Waters sees as "the post war dream." Well, here it is. With double space and citiations, it was 20 pages. If you ever get around to reading it, I hope it made some sense. 



	The Final Cut is an album by Pink Floyd, released on March 21, 1983 in England and on April 2 in the United States. The album is partly a memorial to Eric Fletcher Waters, the father of Pink Floyd’s bassist Roger Waters, who died in the Battle of Anzio during World War II, while also serving as a “requiem for the post war dream.” 

	The monument itself can be considered each and every single record of The Final Cut ever produced, a number of over two million as of 1997. The album was released twice, in vinyl record and Compact Disc formats in 1983 and as a remastered Compact Disc (containing one extra song, “When the Tigers Broke Free”) in 2004. While Pink Floyd was still a popular band at the time, the record was their lowest-selling since Meddle, in 1971. The album also received mixed reviews in the press, some highly critical for the album not meeting the quality of the band’s previous work since The Dark Side of the Moon, while others compared it negatively to a Roger Waters solo album. There were still positive reviews of the album, most notably Kurt Loder’s five-star review of the album in Rolling Stone, which compared Waters’ songwriting abilities to those of Bob Dylan. However, The Final Cut is largely seen by fans of the band as one of their lesser records, alongside 1969’s More, 1987’s A Momentary Lapse of Reason, and 1994’s The Division Bell. 

	Pink Floyd first formed in 1965 under the leadership of lead guitarist, lead singer, and primary songwriter Syd Barrett. The band became one of the most acclaimed psychedelic rock bands in London’s underground music scene, beginning to receive more of the public spotlight with their first two successful singles Arnold Layne and See Emily Play, along with their debut album, The Piper at the Gates of Dawn, all released in 1967. After the mental breakdown and release of Barrett, possibly induced by the pressures of the band’s newfound stardom and his own drug usage, the band adopted a new guitarist named David Gilmour in 1968. The band began to create more experimental music leading up to 1971’s Meddle, then began to adopt the progressive rock and art rock sounds that would become their trademark. Their next album, The Dark Side of the Moon, was massively successful for the band in 1973, thrusting them back into the spotlight once again, but in a much higher degree than six years previously. Pink Floyd would go on to make more hit albums while their bassist Roger Waters’ presence in the band grew as a lyricist and songwriter. 

	His power in the band resulted in The Wall, a double-LP released in 1979. The album was a rock opera, a rock record that tells a story throughout the album. The story focuses on the character Pink, based on Waters himself, and his troubled life that results in Pink creating a mental wall to protect himself from the outside world. Pink ultimately takes down the wall at the end of the album. The album created intense stress in the group, thanks to the sacking of their keyboardist Richard Wright during its recording, the filming of the movie based on the album (named Pink Floyd The Wall), and the both physically and the financially draining world tour for the album. The Final Cut would be released four years after The Wall, containing multiple songs that were linked to its predecessor. A tour was never set after the album’s release, and Waters declared the group dead in 1985. Pink Floyd continued without him for nearly a decade after his announcement, effectively disbanding after their last tour in 1994.

	There were three sets of pictures with The Final Cut, the cover art, the pictures in the gatefold, and the back cover photograph. The cover art was designed by Roger Waters  with photographs of a poppy flower and British military awards, all symbols of warfare in some way, meant to serve as an opposite to the anti-war message featured profoundly throughout the album. The photographs were placed on a black background, without the name of the album or the band (however, these were added in the CD releases). The poppy flower is a symbol used during Remembrance Day in England, the British version of Veterans Day. The poppy flower is shown in various photographs for The Final Cut and mentioned in the songs “Your Possible Pasts” and “Southampton Dock.” The military awards on the cover include the 1939-1945 Star, the Africa Star, the Defence Medal, and the Distinguished Flying Cross, all of which were bestowed upon soldiers during World War II.

	The gatefold of the original vinyl record contained the lyrics to the album and three pictures. The first is a picture of a child’s arm holding out poppy flowers in the countryside, with a soldier in the background. The image contains the symbolism of the English countryside, the innocence of youth with the child’s hand, and the warfare message with the poppy flowers plus the soldier. The second photograph is a picture of a welder working, with the flag of the Rising Sun in the welder’s visor. The image is a reference to the outsourcing of shipbuilding to Japan by Britain, a problem addressed in “The Post War Dream” and “Not Now John.” The third and final photograph on the gatefold is a picture from the view of an inside of a car. Inside is a wilted poppy flower (symbolizing the lack of commemoration that veterans receive), a sunset in the rear view mirror, and a nuclear mushroom cloud from the view of the windshield. The last two parts of the picture are direct references from the final song on the album, “Two Suns in the Sunset.”

	The final photograph is on the back cover of the album sleeve is a depiction of a soldier standing at attention in a poppy field with a knife in his back. The imagery in this photograph is by far the most obvious of all the pictures, showcasing Waters’ belief that the British military man has been betrayed, specifically by their government. The photograph is directly referenced in “Your Possible Pasts.”

	In this memorial, the lyrics play the largest role. All of the songs and lyrics were written by Roger Waters. Together, the songs form a concept album, a record driven by a single theme. For The Final Cut, this theme was clearly spelled out on the back cover, where the album was called “a requiem for the post war dream,” signaling Waters’ belief that the ideal society advertised at the end of World War II was gone and that this album served as a tribute for the death of the post war idealism. The album takes two different points of view on this concept: the efforts of a World War II veteran (presumed to be Pink’s abusive schoolteacher from Pink Floyd’s previous album, The Wall) to reenter society (for most of the first side of the album) and Waters’ own commentary on various issues (in “The Post War Dream” on the first side and all of the second side). With this being a concept album that addresses very serious topics, the only proper way to experience it would to be to listen to The Final Cut in its entirety in one sitting, as the power of the record is reduced significantly when the listener listens to each song on an individual basis.

	The first song, “The Post War Dream,” serves as an introduction to both the concept and the actual album. The song starts off with a radio tuning to various stations, news broadcasts which point out some of the problems facing Britain and the world at that time, the potential nuclear holocaust  (“a group of businessmen announced plans to build a nuclear fallout shelter at Peterborough in Cambridgeshire”), the conditions of English politics (“three high court judges have cleared the way…”), the Falklands War and outsourcing (“it was announced today, that the replacement for the Atlantic Conveyor the container ship lost in the Falklands conflict would be built in Japan, a spokesman for…”), and other conflicts around the world (“…they say the third world countries, like Bolivia, which produce the drug are suffering from rising violence…”). The harsh and accusing lyrics begin with Waters rhetorically asking if this was the world that his father and countless others died for (“Is it for this that daddy died?”) and the accusations that would be flying about as to whose fault was the world’s condition for (“Was it you? Was it me?”). Waters then addresses the outsourcing of shipbuilding in Britain (“If it wasn't for the nips [a ethnic slur for Japanese people]/Being so good at building ships/The yards would still be open on the Clyde [a river in Scotland]”), before posing a rhetorical question to Britain’s Prime Minister, Margret Thatcher as to why English society has turned out the way it has, rather than Waters’ ideal “post war dream” (“What have we done Maggie what have we done/What have we done to England“). Waters continues the questioning, asking what should the people of England do since their post war dream has gone (“Should we shout, should we scream“), and asks the listener what really happened to their ideal society that they were promised (“’What happened to the post war dream?’”), and asks “Maggie” once again what had they done (“Oh Maggie Maggie what did we do?”). 

	Following “The Post War Dream,” sounds of machinery, calling back to the theme of the outsourcing of industry, lead into “Your Possible Pasts” and the story of Pink’s teacher (known by the name The Schoolmaster in The Wall). The possible pasts are personifications of the possible decisions that men could have made in the past, decisions that would have affected themselves in the present, but were ultimately never made. These “possible pasts” can still affect the future, as they show men what they could have done, thereby showing decisions that men should not make in the future (“A warning to anyone still in command/Of their possible futures to take care“). Poppy flowers, found in images on the record sleeve as symbols of remembrance for soldiers, are brought up in the music for the first time (“In derelict sidings the poppies entwine”), and the image of men off at war rather than at home (or the men that never returned) is shown in the next line (“With cattle trucks lying in wait for the next time”). The chorus of the song (“Do you remember me? How we used to be?/Don’t you think we should be closer?)  shows the distance among people in society, alongside that we often forget others and the way we used to interact with them. The back sleeve image is recalled in the description of the men in the song (“for the gold in their bags or the knives in their backs”), with great emphasis placed upon the word “backs” by Waters. The men in this song are veterans, presumably of World War II, and comment on how they spent their youth in the war, not leaving the war as “men” as they had been told they would. They, instead, would just grow into old men like the rest of us, valuing their pre-war youth over their supposed title as men (“He said, ‘I was just a child then now I‘m only a man“). After the chorus, the next lines focus on religion and what it does to people. Using imagery and inflammatory rhetoric, it is quite easy to see the meaning of the lyrics following it (“By the cold and religious we were taken in hand/Shown how to feel good and told to feel bad/tongue tied and terrified we learned how to pray/Now our feeling run deep and cold as the clay”). The idea of possible pasts is revisited, with Waters stating that these possible pasts that we look so highly upon (“And strung out behind us the banners and flags/Of our possible pasts“) are really not what we have always perceived them to be (“lie in tatters and rags”). The chorus ends the song, with the final word, “closer,” repeated by tape loop to emphasize the isolation that individuals feel in society. 

	The word “closer” continues to repeat and fade into the short song, “One of the Few.” The song continues the journey of The Schoolmaster’s attempts to reintegrate himself back into society. As he wonders what he will do “to make ends meet,” he thinks of education as a possible career. The word “teach” repeats over and over as the rest of the song goes by. He realizes the power that the teacher can wield over his students (“Make them mad, make them sad, make them add two and two… Make them laugh, make them cry, make them lie down and die“) and how he can mold them into whatever he pleases (“Make them me, or make them you, make them do what you want them to do”). 
	“The Hero’s Return” starts off as one of the most rock-oriented songs on the album so far. The Schoolmaster makes a reference to religion in his way to understand what is going on in his life, as he had before in “Your Possible Pasts” (“Jesus Jesus what’s it all about“). The Schoolmaster is finding difficulties with his plans for his students that he had outlined in “One of the Few,” and wonders why they do not follow orders immediately as he once did (“Trying to clout these little ingrates to shake/When I was their age all the lights went out/There was no time to whine and mope about”). He revisits memories of the war (“And even now part of me flies over/Dresden at angels one five), and reveals his deep secret, that the war still haunts him to this day (Though they’ll never fathom it behind my/Sarcasm desperate memories lie”). The Schoolmaster has a wife, but as previously shown in The Wall‘s “The Happiest Days of Our Lives“ (“But in the town it was well known/When they got home at night, their fat/Psychopathic wives would thrash them/Within inches of their lives“), they are far apart from one another (“Sweetheart sweetheart are you fast asleep good/’Cos that’s the only time that I can really talk to you”). While she is asleep, he reveals his inner most memory from the war, a secret he can no longer keep bottled up anymore (“And there is something that I’ve locked away/A memory that is too painful/To withstand the light of day”). It is the tale “of [a gunner’s] dying words on the intercom”  that he heard during the war. The listener hears normal in-flight sounds and reporting from the gunner, until a sudden explosion. The pilot is frantically pleading over the intercom for something, but his voice is too garbled, as his plane keeps diving towards the ground.

	The final diving sounds of the airplane can be heard right before the piano starts up for the next song, “The Gunners Dream.” The diving fades as Waters begins to sing from the point of view of the fighter pilot as he approaches death. His mind drifts away, as he begins to recount his life, as it is said most people do once they realize death is upon them (“Floating down through the clouds/Memories come rushing up to meet me now”). The next lines are a reference to a British World War I-era poem by Rupert Brooke, titled “Some Corner of a Foreign Field,” which was also written from the perspective of a soldier in the field. The poem’s first three lines are “If I should die think only this of me/That there’s some corner of a foreign field/That is for ever England,” are directly referenced in “The Gunners Dream” (“And in the corner of some foreign field”). Then, the gunner refers to his dream for which the song is named (“I had a dream/I had a dream“). He then jumps perspective, envisioning his family, giving his farewell to them (“Goodbye Max/Goodbye Ma”), and sees them over his own funeral (“After the service when you’re walking slowly to the car”). Waters uses a large amount of imagery in the following six lines, giving a description of the gunner’s mother (“And the silver in her hair shines in the cold November air”), the man he is talking to who is presumably Max (“You hear the tolling bell/And touch the silk in your lapel”), the emotions that Max is facing (“And as the tear drops rise to meet the comfort of the band”), and Max’s last action in the scene to comfort the gunner’s mother (“You take her frail hand”). The music stops. Waters then yells out from the silence “And hold on to the dream," as the blast of a roaring saxophone solo enters in. Once the solo ends, the gunner begins to describe his dream, the post war dream that Waters spells out to the listener for the first time on the album. The dream does not ask for much, requesting “A place to stay/Enough to eat“ (interjected by The Schoolmaster, faintly yelling in the background “Oi! A real one!,” indicating his own want for the dream too). There’s no crime there, as the “Old heroes shuffle safely down the street”. Both the government and society allow you “to speak out loud/About your doubts and fears”. The government is addressed again, where in the dream “no-one ever disappears/You never hear their standard issue kicking in your door”.  It’s peaceful in the dream with no tension between differing groups in society (“You can relax on both sides of the tracks”). The lack of tension is also pointed out in the next line, “And maniacs don’t blow holes in bandsmen by remote control," referring to the Hyde Park and Regent’s Park Bombings, where the IRA set up nail bombs underneath the bandstand in the two parks during performances by military bands at both locations. The bombings took place in July 1982, the same month Pink Floyd began to record the album. The dream also has people fully respecting and looking up to the laws of the land (“And everyone has recourse to the law”). But most of all, there is no war (“And no-one kills the children anymore/And no-one kills the children anymore”). The song changes perspective to The Schoolmaster, and how he is always yearning for that gunner’s dream (“Night after night/Going round and round my mind/His dream is driving me insane”). Waters does his trademark scream that he had used in other Pink Floyd songs (most notably in “Careful with that Axe, Eugene”), sustaining the word “insane” for over ten seconds at a full scream. The last lines of lyrics to the song serve as a memorial to that gunner, stating that “we” must “Take heed of his dream”.

	The closer to side one and the tale of The Schoolmaster is “Paranoid Eyes.” The first lyrics are a call to arms for what many must do in society (“Button your lips and don‘t let the shield slip/Take a fresh grip on your bullet proof mask”) to protect themselves against everybody (“And if they try to break down your disguise with their questions“), hence the reason why they have “paranoid eyes”. The next stanza details how those with “paranoid eyes” try to at least make it seem as if they have adapted to society, the problem that The Schoolmaster has so far faced since the war. He does this with customary trip to the pub (“You put on your brave face and slip over the road for a jar“), talking with his mates there, trying to seem as relaxed as possible (“fixing your grin as you casually lean on the bar“). He still sticks out (“Laughing too loud at the rest of the world/With the boys in the crowd“), and ends up still having to keep protecting himself (“You can hide hide hide“). This time, since he is so exposed, his eyes become “petrified”  rather than his usual “paranoid eyes.” Waters narrates the story of what The Schoolmaster was told about fighting in war before he joined and how he ate it all up (“You believed in their stories of fame, fortune, and glory“), as barely audible horns blast triumphantly. Now he knows it was all a lie (“The pie in the sky turned out to be miles too high“) and is now “lost in a haze of alcohol softened middle age," turning to alcoholism after the war. The line, “You can hide hide hide” appears again, but the lyric after it is different than it has been, ending with “behind brown and mild eyes”. This line could be interpreted in many different ways. One possible analysis is that The Schoolmaster, with his admittance of the horrors of what war really is in “The Hero’s Return” and “The Gunners Dream,” has now shed his paranoia, and accepted what had happened in the war, a somewhat happy ending (for this character at least) in a very dark album.

	Side two begins the shift to Waters telling his own stories with “Get Your Filthy Hands off My Desert,” an ode to the chaos that is the Middle East. The song starts off with the sound of the wind, then a faint cry from someone saying “Oi! Get your filthy hands off my desert!”. Another man goes “what did he say?," as an airplane roars into the picture, dropping a bomb that set off a massive explosion. With a dry string accompaniment, Waters delivers his lyrics on various conflicts, the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan led by Leonid Brezhnev beginning in 1979 (“Brezhnev took Afghanistan”), the Israeli occupation of Lebanon in 1982 by Israeli Prime Minister Menachem Begin (“Begin took Beirut”), the Argentinean attempt to capture the Falkland Islands from Britain in 1982 by dictator Leopoldo Galtieri (“Galtieri took the Union Jack”). The next lines (“And Maggie over lunch one day took a cruiser with all hands/Apparently to make them give it back”)  refer to the sinking of the Argentinean cruiser, the ARA General Belgrando, sunk by a British submarine in the Falklands War, killing 323. The action brought on controversy, as the death toll was estimated to be 1,200 by some accounts (the actual death toll was 323, while 770 others were rescued). Controversy also stemmed from other facts, such as how the ship was heading back to an Argentinean base and Galtieri had suggested a peace agreement with England hours before the ship was sunk.

	The next song, “The Fletcher Memorial Home” (named after Waters’ father, Eric Fletcher Waters), continues with the modern-day politics of the previous song. “The Fletcher Memorial Home” is a fantasy of Waters’, about rounding up all the “overgrown infants” of our world leaders “away somewhere” and building them “a home, a little place of their own”. Waters refers to the Fletcher Memorial Home’s residents as “incurable tyrants and kings.” He suggests that these leaders are all self-obsessed egomaniacs (“they can appear to themselves/On closed circuit T.V./To make sure they’re still real/It‘s the only connection they feel”). After this, Waters transitions from a scathing to a cordial tone, as he begins to announce the members of the home (“Ladies and gentlemen please welcome…”). These members include President Ronald Regan and his Secretary of State Alexander Haig (“…please welcome Regan and Haig”), Israeli Prime Minister Menachem Begin (“Mr. Begin and friend”), British Prime Minister Margret Thatcher and Northern Ireland politician and clergyman Ian Paisley (“…Mrs. Thatcher and Paisley”), Soviet leader Leonid Brezhnev (“Mr. Brezhnev and party”), American senator Joseph McCarthy (“The ghost of McCarthy”), and President Richard Nixon (“And the memories of Nixon“). A rhetorical question is posed after the introduction, presumably to the British people on how the Argentinean people tried to take back land for their people that was owned by a far-off European country in the Falklands War (“Did they expect us to treat them with any respect?”). The focus goes back to the residents of the home, who keep themselves occupied with their massive egos, symbolized by their medals (“They can polish their medals”) and charismatic abilities (“…and sharpen their/Smiles”), alongside playing their war games (“…and amuse themselves playing games for awhile/Boom boom bang bang lie down your dead”). After a guitar solo, the lyrics still focus on the leaders, left by themselves but constantly monitored (“Safe in the permanent gaze of a cold glass eye“) while they entertain themselves (“With their favorite toys/They’ll be good girls and boys”). Waters addresses the namesake of the home much more harshly than at the beginning of the song, calling it a place for “colonial wasters of life and limb.” At the end, Waters tells the residents that “now the final solution can be applied.” A reference to the Holocaust, it is Waters’ creative way of saying that he believes that the world would be better off without these egomaniacal world leaders. 

	The next two songs on the album are perhaps the most personal on the album. “Southampton Dock” is a largely acoustic song, a story about a mother sending her son off to fight in World War II. The music meets the somber mood of the subject matter, as “no one spoke and no one smiled”. The mother sends her son off, possibly not the first one (“she bravely waves the boys goodbye again”), at Southampton Dock. In the last three lines of the second verse, two voices can be heard, one being Waters more-or-less speaking the lines, the other also being Waters, but in a strained voice yelling out the lyrics alongside the other voice. This second voice, thrown in the background in comparison to the other voice, is a symbol for the strong emotions felt by the mother that are kept shut. Waters had addressed this topic of emotions being bottled up not only in “The Hero’s Return,” but in other Pink Floyd works, such as 1973’s “Time” (“Hanging on in quiet desperation is the English way”). The next verse’s first two lines indicate that the soldier had perished during the war, the “dark stain that spreads between/His shoulder blades”  being his mortal wound. His body (the “mute reminder”) serves as a representative to all of those who died in war, who are memorialized by “poppy fields and graves”. The narrator says with a heavy heart that “when the fight was over/We spent what they had made,” but this sacrifice that they were forced to make with their loved 
ones’ lives was “the final cut”.

	“Southampton Dock” makes a smooth transition over to “The Final Cut” with the last line of the song, making the two almost one piece. Largely seen as an outtake from The Wall, “The Final Cut” is a story of a man in recluse, who was once so high up, but fell “down to the hole in the ground where [he] hides”. The narrator is presumably the main character from The Wall, Pink. After detailing his current condition in the first verse, the second verse details the lengths and symbolic obstacles a person must face to get to Pink (“If you negotiate the minefield in the drive/And beat the dogs and cheat the cold electronic eyes/And if you make it past the shotgun in the hall/Dial the combination open the priesthole”). Tension builds up in the string accompaniment as Pink nears the conclusion of his listing, ending with “and if I’m in I’ll tell," but he is interrupted with a shotgun blast and a cry of joy. As shown in the lyrics that came with the album, the full line was “and if I’m in I’ll tell you what’s behind the wall," presumably the same wall featured in The Wall. This sudden and unexpected outburst of his may be a glimpse into Pink’s mental instability at that time. Pink, whose character has always been emotionally fragile, actually tries to reveal some emotion from inside his wall, asking someone (most likely his wife) “if I showed you my dark side/Would you still hold me tonight/And if I opened my heart to you/Showed you my weak side/What would you do?”. But he reverts back to his old ways of secrecy and paranoia, saying that she might “sell [her] story to Rolling Stone” or “take the children away/And leave [him] alone”. He still holds out some hope that she will accept him (“Would you send me packing/Or would you take me home?”). After a guitar solo, the last verse reflects his apparent failure in his attempts with his wife, angrily reflecting that he “thought [he] oughta bare [his] naked feelings”. With his failure in his last hope for contact with other people, he turns to suicide (“I held the blade in trembling hands/Prepared to make it”). But a phone call interrupts him, saving his life and ending the song (“…but just then the phone rang/I never had the nerve to make the final cut”).

	With the final notes of a piano, a guitar comes screeching in for the second to last song, “Not Now John.” By far, the loudest song on the album, it tends to stick out on the album, eloquently labeled by music-reviewing website Pitchfork as a “slab of cock-rock showmanship.” While the music of the song does stick out immensely, the lyrics do not. The first song to contain some of the lead vocals sung by David Gilmour, “Not Now John” starts off with the line “Fuck all that we’ve got to get on with these," showing people’s need to rush to their other needs and concerns, leaving what they had previously been dealing with in the dust. The next line (“Got to compete with the wily Japanese”)  deals with the topic of outsourcing that was been previously addressed on the album. Industry continues to play into the lyrics with the lines after that (“There’s too many home fires burning/And not enough trees“). The next set of lines, sung by Waters, are written in a stream of consciousness fashion and sung quickly in one or two-worded bursts, reflecting the stresses and paranoia found in modern-day society (“Can’t stop lose bomb mind gone silicon/What bomb get away pay day make hay/Break down need fix big six/Clickity click hold on oh no brrrrrrrrrring bingo!”). The style of the lyrics and delivery f0ound in “One of the Few” is reprised in “Not Now John,” but this time for an entirely different purpose. The lines are written to show how an audience should react to a film they had seen (“Make em laugh make em cry make em dance in the aisles/Make em pay make em stay make em feel ok”), with the next verse continuing the movie theme to it. The verse pertains to the rushed feel of the song, to “get on with the film show,” and how “who cares what it’s about/As long as the kids go” to see the movie. These movie-themed lyrics are based on the filming of Pink Floyd The Wall, which was ongoing during the recording of The Final Cut. Waters was dissatisfied with how the film was progressing, so his frustration was channeled into the record. Other lyrics in the song also deal with Waters’ dislike of common entertainment (“Hold on John/I think there’s something good on/I used to read books but [unintelligible noise]… it could be reusable shows”). Waters takes back the lead vocal role as he enters into a satirical rant on English dominance with a cheering audience in attendance. Competition with Japan is addressed (“Got to compete with the wily Japanese“), along with the Vietnam War (“No need to worry about the Vietnamese“), the Cold War (“Gotta bring the Russian bear to his knees“), and the Falklands War (“We showed Argentina/Now lets go show these“), with Waters stating his belief that the Falklands War was just England trying to mentally reestablish itself as a power in the world (Makes us feel tough/And wouldn’t Maggie be pleased”). The song ends with Waters asking where the bar is in Italian, (possibly) Portuguese, and French, before saying in English, “Oi! Where’s the fucking bar John?," ending the song with the rampant machismo that had pervaded the song. 

	In a stark contrast, “Not Now John” fades away as the sounds of a car traveling down a road can be heard, along with an acoustic guitar and an organ as the final song, “Two Suns in the Sunset” enters. Easily one of, if not the most depressing songs composed by Pink Floyd, the subject matter was one of the darkest ideas in the midst of the Cold War in the 1980s, the possible nuclear holocaust, in a very direct fashion. The song starts off as a man driving down a highway, with a setting sun to his back (“In my rear view mirror the sun’s going down”). Paranoia sweeps the driver as he “suffers premonitions/Confirmed suspicions/Of the holocaust to come”. Waters uses a metaphor to describe the world’s anger as a champagne bottle, as “the wire that holds the cork/That keeps the anger in/Gives way”. The anger here translates to nuclear war, hence the next line, “suddenly it’s day again”. The next lines also use the mushroom cloud of a nuclear explosion as a sun-like figure (“The sun is in the east/Even though the day is done”). At this point, the narrator realizes what has just occurred as he remarks “two suns in the sunset," and gives a sigh. The next verse picks up in loudness, with an electric guitar entering, as panic strikes the narrator (“You stretch the frozen moments with your fear“). One of the most poignant moments of the album is during this verse, while Waters sings “And you’ll never hear their voices/And you’ll never see their faces,” a little girl can be heard screaming, “Daddy! Daddy!”. Othe

This seems to be a love/hate album. It seems everyone either hates the thing more than anything else or thinks it is the greatest album ever. Well, I'm taking a stand and giving it a mediocre rating (pun intended). Lyrically, this album is up there with The Wall (my favorite concept album of all time). Even though I do not agree politically with Waters, this album has all the lyrical genius of The Wall, but directed towards war. Unfortunately, the music falls short of what I wanted. There are a few musically riveting songs ("The Fletcher Memorial Home," "Not Now John," "The Post War Dream," "When the Tigers Broke Free" (not available on this issue of the album, but I still include as part of the album)), but the rest of the music just seems like Waters was trying too hard to get his true feelings out to the audience and persuade them to his mindset. He sounded too desperate in his despair.

The Final Cut. No better a title for an album that divides fans so acrimoniously. I have seen the finest Pink Floyd fans of my generation cast their dispersions against this record and lo, I come not to bury Roger Waters, but to praise him.

The beginnings of the loathing were clearly seen in the direction Floyd took with The Wall. For some fans this is that final cut. For others it was merely another brilliant direction. Each previous album was different from the one before in many ways, and each previous album was accepted accordingly. That's one thing that strikes me oddly, because The Wall gets far more accolades than Final Cut, yet they seem the two most closely related in sound of all the Floydian works. In fact, this could easily have been the third LP in The Wall, as many of the themes are continued and expanded on Final Cut, most noticeably the Second World War and the effects it had on Waters' development. It's almost as if Pink has awoken from his mental breakdown at the wall and is trying to make some sense out of the world he's awoken to.

The Final Cut is a two themed piece of art, the first of these themes, WWII, has already been mentioned. It's clear Waters never came to grips with his father's death in that war, and he continues to explore these feelings that he began to explore on The Wall. The other overreaching theme concerns the state of world affairs at the time of the writing. Britain had slipped in status, while the Americans had come to dominate the globe. The two themes are interwoven together to form the central core of the lyrical material. The music is much in the vein of The Wall, and contains many signature Floyd devices, such as slices of dialog slipped into key places in the songs. To my mind the concept here works extraordinarily well, and is Pink Floyd's most overtly topical record. There are a few wonderfully insightful pieces, like The Gunner's Dream, Your Possible Pasts, The Post War Dream and The Fletcher Memorial Home and also a tribute to John Lennon that became a minor radio hit, Not Now John. It seemed that Waters had made a political connection between the past generation and the merging New World Order. And in hindsight he sure seemed to be on to something.

I remember when this LP came out in 1983. Most of my friends and I were rabid Floyd heads and immediately noticed the similarities to The Wall. It wasn't received with the same gusto as its predecessor, but it was well received nonetheless. This is Pink Floyd's last real album as a band, and their last good album.

I think The Final Cut is a brilliant record. I think that the arguement that it isn't as musically strong as some of Floyd's other record does hold some truth, however, it's got a couple awesome guitar solos (The Fletcher Memorial Home, The Final Cut), some mindblowing Sax playing (The Gunner's Dream, Two Suns in the Sunset). Not to mention the keyboard all over the album, especially on "The Gunner's Dream." Also, on every track, Roger's voice is absolutely amazing. His voice is the type that sends shivers down the spine, his voice is so more emotional, and just chilling than any other member of Floyd, and the way the record is set up, only Roger could do the kind of singing that would do justice to the emotional lyrics that make up the record. The quiet vocals, leading to the explosion in "The Post War Dream" sends shivers up the spine. Roger's thundering vocals in "Your Possible Pasts" (DO YOU REMEMBER ME, How we used to beee, do you think we should be clooser (closer)) Are absolutely astounding. The Gunner's Dream is among the most emotional and powerful vocals Roger has done (take her frail hand..AND HOLD ON TO THE DREAAAAAAAAAAMMMMM) And not to mention the title track, which, for me is the hilight of the album, packed with amazing lyrics, a great solo, and powerful vocals, it's definitely one of the best in Floyd's catalogue. Though the concept is a bit recycled from The Wall i think Roger did a good job of making This album its own unique album, rather than The Wall, Part 2.



That being said, this is one of my favourite Floyd albums, because of the combination of the lyrics,vocals, the blistering guitar solos (used sparingly, but to the point where they're very effective- remember too much of something can get tiring) Not to mention the amazing elements scattered all through the record (Keyboard, Sax, the way the tracks segue, it's all perfect, and makes for a great listening experience)



I suggest instead of focusing on how much you dislike it, listen again, and listen for the little things, trust me, if you listen close enough I guarentee you'll find something to enjoy about The Final Cut

_The Wall_ traces the life of a rock star from birth, through childhood and absent father/smothering controlling mother, to an eventual slide into madness and psychosis.  The best known song, “Another brick in the wall (part 2)” with its catchy, chanting anthem “We don’t need no education” could be responsible for thousands of people in their late thirties/early forties flipping burgers and sweeping floors who took those words literally and out of context. review: Prog-Rock-tastic! _The Wall_ is probably the best known Prog-rock album around and more or less the most effective at that. The story/theme format that define these albums can be restrictive to the contents of the songs and this is certainly the case here with Waters struggling to write outside his rigid theme of alienation. Some of the songs on this album are unique to _The Wall_ and would not work outside of it whereas some such as "Comfortably numb" , "Hey you" and "In the Flesh" are just fantastic songs in their own right. Though i do claim to love this album i must admit that it does frustrate me quite a bit for the sometimes wild swing between great tracks and weaker tracks that seem to obstruct the flow of the album. This again relates back to the rigid structure Waters self-imposed upon the album whereby anything seemingly outside of his idea of "The Wall" is not acceptable and unused (except of course Gilmour's excellent "Young Lust", which doesn't necessarily fit with Waters songs). Overall this is fantastically entertaining bravura from a man on a mission, trying to write the Prog-rock-opera of his life and almost succeeding.
The Wall is undoubtedly the best concept album of all time... if I listen to it i will become Comfortably Numb towards it though.

This album to me along with many classic rock albums represents my childhood.  I grew hearing a lot of country and classic rock in my house.  I heard a lot of ELO, Deep Purple, AC/DC (who has become my favorite band), and Pink Floyd.  This album holds many personal moments for me.  Many parts of it frightened me as a child such as "is there anybody out there?" and others.  Then again I also took delight in playing the "goodbye blue sky" opening over and over again.  The radio hits are also fantastic and "The Trial" hgas excellent lyrics.  This album is meant to be heard as a whole to get its significence and I think it's better than "Dark Side of the Moon".  This is very relaxing and leaves you on a high as it finsihes.  I always enjoy hearing it on road trips late at night when I'm in the front seat with my dad, just remincing the memories.  This album and many others shaped me into who I am and God only knows that I would still be listening to disco if I hadn't of acknowledged these classics.  Roger Waters may have an ego inflation bigger than the inflatable pig but this is a story that was meant to be told.  It has shaped my life and can shape yours too.  I fully recommand it for those who want depth and meanings in their music and who want to prove that music is an excellent but unappreciated art.  OK i think I've rambled on enough...lol

review: On no other album, ever, do the best songs actually sound weaker in the context of the rest of the album.  With the White Album, sure you've got some stuff that's just there to be silly, but that just makes the great songs more endearing.  With _The Wall_... hm, where to start?  The excellent riff from "In the Flesh?" gets your hopes up, only to be dashed within minutes.  I remember enjoying "Comfortably Numb" at some point earlier in my life, but the absolute ridiculousness of the vast majority of this album has now ruined it for me.  Plus the song's vocals sound even more annoying after the endless whining of the first twenty songs.  Dammit, Pink Floyd, you could have been one of the best bands ever, but you had to leave a gigantic flaw, or two, or twenty, on every goddamn album.  I don't care that you're trying to produce some commentary on the state of the world.  When you pair that up with decent songwriting, it's tolerable, but when you feel the need to use two LPs to get it all out without actually writing two LPs worth of songs, it makes me worry that your finest moments were just accidents.

Even though  The Dark Side Of The Moon had lived up to all the hype and expectation, it was several years later before I even thought about spending any more money on the band and then it was on this, an overlong, pretentious, conceited, egocentric piece of nonsense which also happens to be inventive, lyrically stunning, imaginative and musically flawless. That dichotomy is the reason for the diversity of opinion which has forever dogged The Wall.

It tells the story of a boy who loses his father in the war and grows up being persecuted by domineering authority figures. In defence, his mind turns inward and he begins to build a wall from which to hide from the world. He finds solace in music which he uses as both an escape and a means by which he can communicate and develop. On becoming famous, he finds life in the fast lane re-ignites the crushing terrors of his past and he spirals into a terminal decline of chemical abuse and madness. It's easy to speculate that the principal character is Roger Waters himself but he has always remained close-mouthed on the subject. The Wall is a great album but only in its entirety. Close examination reveals there are few songs of distinction - "Mother", "Hey You", "Comfortably Numb" - most others are just snippets or simply daft. However it manages to capture the neuroses and psychoses that cripple every one of us and for that it deserves praise.

I don't know if you'd call this the greatest concept album ever or just a great collection of songs that construct a story. _The Wall_ can be listened to for its great songs such as "Another Brick In the Wall (part 2)" and "Comfortably Numb" AND "Run Like Hell" or (like the film) it can be a totally mind-blowing trip that leaves you totally affected.

The Wall is a very personal album and, as such, is one of Pink Floyd’s more difficult album’s to approach and enjoy. There are some interesting musical passages and excellent tracks on the album. But the listener is required to hear them in the context of an unhappy story told from the point of view of a tortured character. This wasn’t the usual symphonic rock that Floyd fans had grown used to. This was some king of unfamiliar art-rock.



The first half of this double album essentially tells the tale of how the character at the centre of the story develops all his various complexes. There’s the death of his father (“Another Brick in the Wall Pt 1), bullying school-teachers (“The Happiest Days of Our Lives” and “Another Brick in the Wall Pt 2”) and the over-bearing influence of his mother (“Mother”). This last one starts with the youth asking his mother various questions. The first one – ‘do you think they’ll drop the bomb’ – was the question rooted in the mind of anyone living in the shadow of the Cold War and WWIII that might follow. The songs continue to chart his descent into insanity as he builds a wall around himself and says goodbye to the world.



The third quarter of the album presents his state of madness. “Is There Anybody Out There?”, “Nobody Home” and the excellent “Comfortably Numb” all manage this in vivid but quite different ways. The last quarter is the least satisfying as part of the story is told in the style of a West-End or Broadway musical. The character becomes a demented sadist and enters a nightmare scenario where he’s put on trial, with the result that the judge orders the wall to be broken down. 



There is some fantastic music, notably from guitarist Gilmour, on tracks like “A Brick in the Wall Pt 2” and “Comfortably Numb”. There is also some interesting guitar work on “Don’t Leave me Now”. This is an early example of the ‘jangly’ style that would become so popular in 80s alternative rock music. The story isn’t the easiest to empathize with, even if it is a baring of the soul on the part of Rogers. It is, however, a significant artistic achievement in popular music and deserves recognition for that.

This album is pretentious and self-important and I don't enjoy it. I have my pretentious, self-important air of a cultured musical connoisseur to keep up, after all. I'm an intellectual, which is why I never stop talking about how smart I am. Too smart for this album, which is completely overindulgent. Concept albums should be about obscure mathematics principles or 1600s nomads, not something personal like all the mistakes one person has made through their life. Real music is written with so many metaphors and obscure literary references that not even the band knows what it's about. This album, on the other hand, is guilty of showing feelings, feelings of an almost human nature. It's just whiny and childish, not at all like you're imagining my voice right now. If I could, I would spit in the face of anyone who calls this "art" and then write my own concept album about how misunderstood I am.

Some of these songs are even played on the radio, and anything that has ever touched the radio is vapid, commercial shit. Screw actually listening to the music.
There are much better bands to discover through the RYM top 100 and post on my profile so people will think I'm cool. Excuse me, I have some Velvet Underground to "listen" to.

The Wall gets my vote as the finest concept album of the 70's.  Alongside Led Zeppelin's Physical Graffiti, I also consider it to be one of the top two double-LPs of the decade (easily the best decade for such ambitious releases).  Unlike the band's four previous studio albums, The Wall clearly falls outside of the realm of progressive rock.  Devoted prog rock fans will be especially discouraged by the album's relatively episodic and song-oriented structure, with only three of the twenty-six tracks clocking in at over five minutes.  After all, it does have a story to tell, so if you're not comfortable with the somewhat different aims of this record you're probably best served sticking to Wish You Were Here and Animals.  Still, like all great concept albums, the storyline here serves primarily as a vehicle for the music and rarely precludes the actual musical content.  The only places where the concept does take over is towards the end of both halves (especially the end of disc two).  It then comes as little surprise that the last three tracks of each LP constitute the most disposable music on the entire album.



The album's first LP starts off in marvelous fashion, with the first ten tracks easily warranting a rating of 5.0 stars.  My favorite cut here is the laid-back "Mother", although the three-track "Another Brick in the Wall" suite and "Empty Spaces/Young Lust" are other clear highlights.  Unfortunately, as previously mentioned, the album suddenly slips into mediocrity with the boring "Don't Leave Me Now" and limps to the half-way point with the superfluous "Another Brick in the Wall, Pt. 3" and "Goodbye Cruel World".  The second LP follows a similar pattern, although the music here is typically far more laid-back.  The best track on the second half is easily the radio hit "Comfortably Numb", which closes with one of the few extended guitar solos on the entire record (and what a solo it is!).  Also noteworthy are the similarly atmospheric "Hey You", the criminally short ballad "Vera", and the album's second version of "In the Flesh".  It's with the final 4-5 tracks that the listener finally bemoans the fact that the album wasn't pared down to a single LP, with the music oscillating between boring filler ("Run Like Hell", "Outside the Wall") and overly-theatrical rock opera of the sort that I've never particularly cared for ("The Trial").



Still a classic album that every popular music fan should hear at least once.  Frustrating only in that, if the band had taken the best music off each half and condensed it into a single album, you would have had one of the best records of all-time.  In any case, The Wall does a far better job than most double LPs of justifying its dual length, presenting enough material for 1.5 excellent releases.  Perhaps they would have been better served saving some of this great music for the subsequent Final Cut- a notoriously melody-deficient work that barely holds a candle to the poorest moments of this record…

review:
One of my favorite albums from my high school years. I bought it simply out of curiosity and it took over a large chunk of my listening hours. The story and subject came at a really useful time in my life, and it helped me through a few rough spots. A local theater had it every weekend as a midnight movie as well, which was a huge bonus. 

There are a couple of songs that haven't aged all that well, but they still serve the dramatic flow of the album and I never skip them. The main thing that I love about this album is the solid vision and method of storytelling. The 'tortured artist' is certainly cliche, but the pacing of the timeline and the changing perspectives that we get with each song of Pink's mental state and history that also reach more deeply into English history and psychology really is done brilliantly.

"The Trial" is one of the best uses of orchestra and characterization along with a band that I've ever heard, and it's done so well that it does not stick out with its change of style.

I have to be careful listening to it as an adult. Even though it's an "enjoyable" experience, those memories of my youth are bonded to the music, and my inner depressed-young-man dredges up the old wounds. (LOL sad sack)

I went to see Floyd on their winter tour in 74 and they played 3 new songs 2 of which ended up on "Wish You Were Here" and "Animals".  When I realised the third song was slated to appear on "The Wall I was convinced that they were running out of ideas.  As usual I was wrong.  Dave Gilmours playing had moved up a notch and he's on magnificent form on this outstanding record.A second magnum opus of their career.

This is an overblown work of art that plays out like a Roger Waters ego trip.  Ironically, the best song on the album, "Comfortably Numb", with one of the greatest guitar solos of all time, was primarily written by David Gilmour.

It is of course spectacular bollocks.

Pink Floyd's 80 minute rock opera is a monster in all possible senses. Trouble is, it isn't really rock and is much more like an opera. In really trying to tell a story, much of the running time is given over to the rock equivalent of recitative and just does not make repeat listening. The number of good rock songs out of 26 tracks on the album you can count on one hand.

The vibe behind the album isn't good. Troubled rock star with mother / women / authority issues has nervous breakdown and emerges as neo-fascist revolutionary before completely rejecting the outside world and finishing up as a vegetable. Or something like that. Not happy stuff. Written entirely by Waters with a couple of meagre co credits for Gilmour here and there and one for the producer Bob Ezrin, this would appear to be the manifestation of Roger's preoccupations, and we have to ask, what was up with the guy?

Before continuing I would just like to mention that my Dad reckons The Trial sounds like Kurt Weill.

But in some ways The Wall works. It makes you pay attention in order to follow the plot, which is set out just clearly enough to feel it is worth persevering with. The production is exemplary, the sequencing and segueing immaculate, though in truth this has been a Floyd strength since Dark Side of the Moon. The packaging is great with the Gerald Scarfe designs and lettering. But you're never going to stick it on repeat play and you're never going to put it on shuffle.

Once Waters took over Pink Floyd, they went downhill...period.  Roger Waters is a lyricist first and foremost, and if you look at Floyd's best early stuff, it was almost all collaborative.  Even great songs like "Money" off of Dark Side of the Moon...credited to Waters, but essentially half of it is a jam session between Gilmour and Wright.  

By the time of Animals, Waters had almost taken completely over the band, "writing" four of the five songs, but Gilmour got his two sense in, writing the music for the 17-minute epic Dogs.

Prior to this album, Floyd was about looseness.  Their music was spacey, and beautiful, and very proggy.  The Wall is the transition from prog rock, essentially into folk rock.  Because Waters wrote the music for most of the tracks here, the melodies suffer.  And worst of all, he sings most of the songs too, and his whiny voice gets real tiring real fast.  However, perhaps if by chance, or perhaps because he's just a better singer, any song that Gilmour sings lead on comes off as a great reprieve from Waters dull vocals.  Take for instance "Mother".  If Gilmour didn't sing the "mother's" part, the song would only be okay.  But as it stands, his vocals, and guitar solo (which I assume he wrote, not Waters) come off as quite lovely, and the song is one of the best off the album.

So I'll sum up my opinions on this.  Some of the music is pretty good, though very rarely reaching the greatness of DSOTM or WYWH, and some is pretty bland (Outside the Wall, Vera, Bring the Boys Back Home, and several more).  The lyrics, while at times quite interesting and sort of insightful (Comfortably Numb, Mother, etc.) come off overall as whining and bitching, and while Waters has the right to do so, that doesn't mean people necessarily want to listen to it.

So I'll give it a mixed review.  Perhaps in the B- range.

review:
The Wall is the most ambitious effort from a band that during the 70's took as many chances as possible without sacrificing their musical integrity. In addition to being a double album, The Wall presents an intriguing (though at times self indulgent autobiography of Roger Waters) concept of increasing levels of isolation and succeeds in not doing the concept half way by reinforcing the story line throughout every track on the album. The two faults of this album that plague nearly every double album I've heard are inconsistency and wasted tracks. Both of these are due in part to the neccesity of tracks that only serve as interludes and nothing more either for the sake of telling the story or bridging multiple tracks together. 



Most of the individua tracks on this album are phenomenal and the first side of the first album rivals only Wish You Were Here for the finest moment in Pink Floyd's career. Side two is a clear step down from side one with only "Young Lust" and the third segment of the "Another Brick in the Wall" suite being points of interest. Similar to side one of the first album, the first side of the second album comes out strong with "Hey You" and "Comfortably Numb", one of the finest radio friendly tracks the band would ever produce alongside "Have a Cigar" and "Wish You Were Here". 



The last half of the second album is where the wheels fall off with only "Run Like Hell" being above average. Even though I may dismiss ten or so tracks here, there is nothing here I would classify as awful or superfluous within the context of the album. Those ten tracks would flounder without the rest of the album to support them but given how integrated every track is into the concept, it's understandable. Overall there are only three double albums that I would consider to be close to the league that The Wall is in. Sadly Waters (ironic) increasing isolation from his band mates and constant ego clashes with Gilmour would make this the last Pink Floyd album that I can qualify as a must listen or "classic". A fitting way for Pink Floyd to end a decade of dominance in 70's, prog, and hard rock.

God, the ending stretch of this album is such a mess. The Trial is posited as the big "ah-ha" moment where Pink is forced to comes to terms with what a selfish asshole he's been, but it fails because: a) the women in Pink's life are still portrayed as nasty, hateful caricatures even when the whole ostensible point is to show much harm he's caused to them and b) the climax of an extremely long and extremely depressing rock opera is the exact worst moment you could decide to inject levity with fucking oompah-pah tuba music. Yes, Outside The Wall shows up afterwards (almost apologetically) to reiterate the album's themes a bit more soberly, but it's such a wisp of nothing of a song that it, too, feels like a cop-out. This is no big surprise, of course: by all accounts, Roger Waters hadn't even begun to take responsibility for his emotional issues in real life, so it makes sense that his fictionalized counterpart would be abruptly dispatched with a half-hearted "whoops!" at his supposed moment of reckoning. This is doubly unfortunate because it's all preceded by the fascist-dictator cosplay of Waiting For The Worms, which somehow fails to make Nazis seem threatening or ridiculous, a misfire akin to accidentally killing someone with a squirt gun at long range. It's also a shame because "fascism is the logical endpoint of emotional repression and violent misogyny" is maybe the smartest thing this album has to say, and would feel incredibly chilling and prescient in 2019 if it had only managed to stick the landing. (Ironically, the title track of The Final Cut would have resolved The Wall's emotional arc beautifully, but in the context of that fiasco it was too little, too late.)

But oh, the sheer spectacle of this thing! The fact that this album is so preposterous relative to how seriously it takes itself could be seen as a fatal flaw, but for me it's the album's biggest saving grace. The Wall is operating at wailing, shirt-rending levels of operatic sturm und drang at all times, and between that and the obscene amount of production gimmicks that it piles on, it's completely off the charts in terms of entertainment value. Talk about "filler" all you want, but there isn't a single second of this album that bores me, and at an hour and twenty minutes, that isn't just impressive, it's borderline-miraculous. Plus, in the rare moments when the album shows more heart than bile, it can still pack a genuine emotional wallop: Nobody Home is poignant for how it feels like the ghost of a kinder, pre-Dark-Side-era Pink Floyd dropping in unexpectedly, while Comfortably Numb might as well be the classic rock era writing its own epitaph, a heart-wrenching admission of failure delivered in the last lucid moment of a terminal barbiturate haze.

I have never been the biggest fan of The Wall, and I still am not. I seem to be the only one who can't see the appeal. Sure, there are some great tracks like both versions of In the Flesh, Comfortably Numb, Mother, and Run Like Hell, but other than that it's mostly filler. It's not bad, but it is definitely not the first album I'll put on if I want to hear some Pink Floyd.

Pink Floyd were on top of the world, and it sucked. After their 1973 opus Dark Side of the Moon broke them through on both sides of the Atlantic, the band suddenly found themselves at a level of popularity at which no other prog bands were operating. Hell, very few other bands in general, excepting maybe Led Zeppelin and Queen, were rivaling Pink Floyd's popularity. They followed up Dark Side with 1975's Wish You Were Here, my personal favorite Floyd album, and 1977's Animals, an anti-capitalist and anti-authoritarian homage to Animal Farm. Neither of these albums really scream "big hit" to me, but both went several times platinum. On top of that, the band began touring stadiums and arenas and bringing elaborate stage productions with them. The band members, of course, hated all of this. They fought bitterly as bassist Roger Waters wrestled away the main songwriter position, made their hatred of music industry self-evident in their songs, and at an infamous concert in Montreal, Waters spat in disgust on fans that he thought were getting too rowdy. Clearly, the vibes were off in Pink Floyd, and Waters responded the way that any self-respecting prog songwriter would: he made a long, ambitious concept album about the traumatic forces in a young rock star's life that send him into a spiral of depression and megalomania that he ultimately has to confront. The Wall is hefty, self-serious, and more than a little pretentious, but ultimately, it still rules.



The Wall, perhaps more than any other Pink Floyd album, is an album that begets sitting down and listening to the whole thing. It essentially works as theater in rock-music form, with a whole storyline and sound effects and dialogue and everything. On the first track alone, there's the sound of bomber jets (representing the rock-star character Pink Floyd's father dying in WWII) and baby noises (representing Pink Floyd's birth), and there's plenty of angry Scottish schoolteacher and groupie dialogue after that. With that theatrical approach, it makes sense that the band brought in Bob Erzin, who had experience working with theatrical bands like Alice Cooper and Kiss, to produce. By the sound of it, Erzin reigned in a lot of Water's more drawn-out, proggier instincts, which makes a much more digestible 80 minutes than it might've otherwise been.  While there certainly is still some degree of filler - it's unlikely that I'll ever listen to "Don't Leave Me Now" again - it's still fairly easy to keep track of the plot and remain invested, which isn't a given for these types of concept albums.



On the topic of that concept, it's probably break this album by each side. Side A, after a brief rockstar intro, dives into the traumatic forces that bore upon Pink as a child. There's quite a bit of them - the death of his father, his smothering, overprotective mother, the fear in wartime England, totalitarian schoolteachers. The quasi-disco hit "Another Brick in the Wall, Pt. 2" is on this part, and it's probably my favorite part, being one of the few songs on the album that locks into any sort of groove. You might notice that there's very little subtlety to any of these lyrics, and while true, I think it helps for following-along purposes (Genesis's A Lamb Lies Down On Broadway, a concept album with lyrics more open to interpretation, is largely incoherent). Besides, even if the specificity removes some relatability, the general theme can still resonate. Plenty of us have childhood traumas that can manifest in ugly ways in adulthood, and I think it's cool that such a big album touches on that. Moving onto side B,  this is where those childhood traumas, the metaphorical wall, catch up with adult Pink when he learns of his wife's infidelity, causing him to break down in a hotel room and isolate himself from the outside world. These types of fall-from-grace stories can be captivating - I like The Godfather and Breaking Bad as much as anyone - but this is honestly my least favorite part of the album. The section feels the most musically inert to me and has too many swathes of dialogue, making it a bit too easy to tune out. Ironically, my favorite song on this part might be "Young Lust", the satire of Zeppelin-style arena rock, while the punishing "Don't Leave Me Now" is this album's low point.



Side C, meanwhile, is probably my favorite section. It captures feelings of isolation, depression, and punishing introspection quite well. Besides the noisy middle section of "Hey You", the section is mainly in a string-heavy, ethereal dream space zone, and it sounds fantastic. Thematically, Pink self-reflects and begins to regret isolating himself from the outside world, coming just short of the conclusion that he needs to confront his demons and past traumas to return as a regular, functioning member of society. It's a resonant message, and coupled with the beautiful production, I think it's delivered quite well. This section also happens to contain my favorite song on this album, that being "Comfortably Numb". That song has the atmospheric, all-encompassing sound that sounds like entering the heavens, and David Gilmour's solos are among the best ever put on tape. It's hard imagine a guitar solo working against production this dreamy and tranquil, but Gilmour makes it sound like magic. It's a truly incredible song.



Finally, we reach side D, where Pink's mangers sedate him with tranquilizers so that he still performs, and when he's out on stage, he imagines himself to be a sort of fascist dictator, allowing himself to become the very traumatic force that had festered inside him for so long. Hearing Pink shout out slurs and execution orders is pretty shocking, intentionally so, and I suppose it's also reflective of Water's complicated relationship with his stardom and fans, like it's an exaggerated version of his spitting incident. In any case, "In The Flesh" and "Waiting For The Worms" capture the menace of the situation quite well, though "Run Like Hell" is a bit too slick for the situation, and these bring us into "The Trial," where Pink finally confronts the various demons in his life. "The Trial" has a rep as an all-time great closer, but I dunno. It ties up the album's storyline pretty well, but it sounds like particularly silly Disney villain song. The voices that Waters gives different characters are particularly goofy, and it stands in stark contrast to the self-seriousness of the rest of the album. Maybe some levity was needed for an album like this, but I'm not sure how I feel about how it was deployed. Overall, while I certainly have my problems with The Wall, for the ridiculous amount of ambition it has, it sticks the landing remarkably well. While it's not my favorite of their albums, I think it deserves a place among their classics.



In a way, The Wall might the endpoint for this kind of 70s album. The progressive rock was fragmenting in the late 70s as the popular tides turned towards punk and new wave, and most of the genre's biggest bands were either broken up or in a state of disarray. The only prog rock blockbuster I know of that came after The Wall was Rush's Moving Pictures, and Rush was late to the prog rock party to begin with. The Wall is also one of the last of the big theatrical rock albums that were big in the 70s. Meat Loaf's Bat Out of Hell and Queen's A Night at the Opera were big hits in the years before, but that kind of album fell by the wayside as rock entered the 80s. The Wall certainly didn't usher in a new Pink Floyd renaissance. It sold a bajillion copies and inspiring a film adaption in 1982, but 1983's The Final Cut, a Roger Waters album in all but name, sold a fraction of what this did.  Waters left the band in 1985, and a Gilmour-led Pink Floyd put out a few albums to varying returns, none of which had anything to do with the pop zeitgeist. That being said, we will see Pink Floyd on this list again.

Hey this is my first review here so it might not be very good but I really wanted to say this. The Wall is my favorite album. Pretty much every song on it (except Young Lust) is at least a solid 7 out of 10. Despite being pretty long compared to the average album it still doesn't have much filler and everything contributes to the overall story about Roger Water's life. My favorite song on it is The Trial which is also one of my favorite song of all time. I have listened to this album several times and despite the dark subject matter it is also quite comforting to me. This album honestly inspires me to write characters and pursue art with meaning. One thing about The Wall that I love is the philosophical questions it raises about putting a wall around yourself. Is it good that the wall came down or is it bad? That question is something that is left to the listener to decide. I will admit The Wall is not flawless. Young Lust is the reason why this album is not a 10/10. Instead it's a 9 out of 10. Young Lust is exactly what it sounds like. It's a stupid love song. Some love songs are good but Young Lust isn't one of them. There is also the annoying "wanna take a bath" quote but it's hard to notice for me since I missed it for my first 3 listens. Despite how bad Young Lust is the rest of the album makes up for it. In conclusion The Wall is a masterpiece that anyone who is a fan of music should listen too.

The Wall, to me, is an anti-capitalist allegory that touches on a society that kills man and leaves only a desolate husk of unrealized ideals. Destroying our creativity during every step of our development, from school, to our parents. Everything is designed to break us down and create puppets that fit the mold of a shallow and soulless society, while those outside that norm are shunned and fed to the worms. 

The only escape from this continuous cycle being to organize ourselves and revolt against our oppressors and the systems they have put against us. And mold our own society outside the bounds of the wall.

Yet in the end, we will fail, be put on trial, and be imprisoned by a society that fundamentally works against us. Only to dream of the could be’s of a world outside the wall that holds us. 

So yeah, basically, 6/10. Pretty ok album, could be better.

review:
Undoubtedly one of the single, most depressing, whiniest, tune free set of songs ever put onto vinyl by a major band. The Wall finds Roger Waters moaning and whining about how his prestigious rock star life has alienated him from his audience, how his father's death during the second world war has deeply affected his personality (yes we know Roger, we get it!) and how his utter disdain for the very fans who fork out hard earned money to see his shows has made him wish for the possibility that a wall may be erected in front of the band so he doesn't have to see the plebs who come to his overly priced shows.

Or is it? Is The Wall merely a metaphor for loftier ideas and philosophies....no it's all of the above, tuneless, sexless, ideologically suspect and played with no feel or passion by a bunch of fat, rich tories. 'The Wall' is the single most depressing album i've ever heard in my life (and believe me i've heard some real downers), Waters and his hired hands...erm i mean bandmates would actually go on to top this monstrosity with their next release, the truly risible 'The Final Cut' but as bad as that album is it is still not as utterly suicidal as this whingefest.
The greatest achievement in music history hands down.

"...we came in?"

Probably the most important album to me during my years in high school. I can distinctly remember that while the other kids were reading books, finishing up homework, studying, talking to friends and making plans for the weekend during the downtime between classes, I was sitting in the classroom, forehead on the desk, reading and noting pages of lyrics from Pink Floyd's 1979 album, The Wall, while listening to the CD on my Discman. I heard the album before I saw the movie and loved it, but once I saw the movie, my obsession with the album was solidified. Analyzing every twist and turn of Pink's tortured existence became an escape of my own.

My obsession left me with the feeling that I knew this album inside and out. I can write out nearly every line from this album straight from memory in less than a half hour. In some ways, it seems like this is the album that I should be able to write the most brilliant review I've ever written. But as I sit in front of my computer my mind draws nothing new to say about an album that already has over 600 reviews. But, for your sake, guy who actually reads the crap I dare to write about music, I will write what I think.

The Wall has been labeled a lot of things. Some say it's not a true Floyd album, rather a Waters solo album. Some say that it is Floyd's greatest work. Others say that, while it is a good album at its core, there is too much filler to drag it down. To some extent, each of these claims are valid, though I would say all of them are only partially true. It is a true Floyd album, Waters takes over a lot of the writing, yes, but the band is still playing their hearts out and Gilmour was at least given some creative room, even if it was just a few great and one absolutely mind blowing solo. As far as it being Pink Floyd's greatest work, I can't say I buy that when they have a catalog that includes Dark Side of the Moon and Wish You Were Here. And as for filler, some tracks may musically be filler, but lyrically everything seems necessary, even if some are on lower scale than others, they are more like bridging tracks than filler tracks.

The first disc begins with a bang, as "In the Flesh?" roars out of nowhere, an impact so powerful it resonates through the intro until the lyrics start coming in. The stage is set, Pink tells the audience the story of how he got to this point. This story carries all the way to disc 2's "In the Flesh." It tells a story that I really think is best heard straight from the album itself, though the statute of limitations is undoubtedly past on Pink Floyd spoilers, but far be it from me to ruin anyone's enjoyment of this fantastic album. The general idea is that Pink undergoes a number of traumatic events that build up in his mind like bricks. The bricks eventually for (tada!) the wall that shuts his mind off from the outside world. By the time "In the Flesh" comes around, Pink is a drugged up, emotionally removed rock star. He brings himself to dictator-like status in the minds of his blindly following fans. The conclusion of the story kind of seems rushed, a bit too deus ex machina, but still has some interesting psychological pieces to it and in ends back where it started. For further info on this, I suggest listening to the damn album already.

I'm not really wanting to go into any more plot details. (I could really talk about the story and the symbolism and plot of both the album and movie forever, if you want to get into that kind of discussion, please PM me.) The story is a brilliant one, at times overblown, pretentious, obvious, but overall, still a masterwork. The music perfectly compliments the lyrics. The album is much more lyrically based than musically based, a big change from their post-Barret directions. Many people cite this as their favorite Pink Floyd album and while I still claim several of the bands works as superior I can't really argue with the people who prefer this. My dad loves this album and constantly argues with me if I try to claim it is inferior to Wish You Were Here. In fact, he really doesn't care for much other Pink Floyd stuff, but since this is one of his all time favorites. Perhaps this is the Pink Floyd album for people who aren't Pink Floyd fans?

Bottom line, I'm not gonna list highlights because it really needs to be heard as a whole, so if you haven't already, listen to it. That's all I need to say.

review: Yeah, it's ridicolously overblown and theres some less than steller tracks here and there. But it's still a really solid album on the whole. While "Another Brick in the Wall Part 2" may be the most overplayed track in the history of rock n' roll, it does little to diminish the wealth of good material on this album.  

Many consider this the band's true master work, and they can hardly be blamed.  Concept album?  Rock Opera?  I rarely use those terms, because many disagree about their meaning, but it could be considered either or both of the those, and Waters's song writing really shines on this album because he's able to tell a cohesive story, with all of its ups and downs, over the course of the album's 81 minutes.  While the band often alternates between Roger Waters and David Gilmour on vocals, here Waters's impassioned singing, which truly seems tinged by madness, is on display.  He's not a great vocalist, and many of these songs are out of his range, but that makes his performance all the more convincing, as he portrays a man slowly going mad with paranoia, xenophobia, and cynicism.  As he reaches for notes he can't quite hit without sounding like it's the effort of a lifetime, the listener becomes more and more convinced of the story.

The concept is not that great but is still interesting: a simple vision(maybe too simple to be taken seriously) of what society is: sheep, dogs and pigs. Evidently, Waters appreciates Orwell's "Animal Farm".
Also the spacey production is great and contributes to the overall greatness of the album.
