I remember when the inflatable pig broke free and flew all over south London as well as the pages of most of the newspapers. I don't know if it was deliberate - probably a stunt by Roger Waters who designed the album art - but it was great. And shooting at Battersea Powerstation was inspired. It even looks like a dead animal (which in some ways it was), lying on its back with all four legs sticking into the sky.



And the music? Well, that's pretty inspired too. Roger gets his sharpened quill and stabs it into the heart of our corrupt and lethargic society. The Dogs who claw their way to the top, or as high as they can get, forming packs with the like-minded and trampling on the Sheep below them: the Sheep who are quite content to follow each other around without a word of complaint until they are butchered. Secretly dreaming of the bloody revolution, but too scared to speak out. And then there are the Pigs, the fascists, those who tell us how to be, how to think, how to behave. The big executive in his leather chair, the miserable old hag who wont accept the changing times, becoming ever more bitter and lonely, and Mary Whitehouse and her Viewers and Listeners Association, censoring every word and image that offends them.



Someone had to say it, and noone ever did it as well as Waters. And with it all wrapped up in some of the most powerful music the band would create, _Animals_ remains as terrifiyng and relevant as it was 25 years ago.

A great album, easily Floyd's best overall, and one of my favorites of all-time.  Only three real songs, surrounded by 2 short "Pigs on the Wing" acoustic numbers.  "Dogs" is the main event, worth every bit of its 17 minutes (and believe me, it doesn't seem nearly that long).  "Pigs" and "Sheep" are the other two songs and are probably the two angriest songs The Floyd ever did.  Many great moments on this record that give me the chills: the repeating of the word "stone" in Dogs that eventually merges with the music only to reappear a few minutes later, the ending passage of Dogs ("Who was born in a house of pain... etc), the creepy synth intro to "Pigs" and its closing bassline, the final triumphant guitar lines in "Sheep".

I'm totally in favour of stretching out a few songs into long performance pieces so long as the songs are especially good and the performances stay focussed - unfortunately the three songs on this album (ignoring the two short acoustic bookends) fail on both counts.  There is a type of morbid fascination to the whole thing although the music only really comes alive during Gilmour's lead playing.

Animals shows us a new Pink Floyd, a Floyd with Roger Waters undoubtedly at the helm. Animals follows a strong Waters dominated theme (first inklings of The Wall) and could easily be called a concept album. It comments on social upheaval but relates different people to different animals - dogs, pigs and sheep. Like Orwell's classic Animal Farm, Animals successfully uses the allusion of animals to criticise the society we live in. Pigs are the greedy people who rule, the materialistic Dogs fight and backstab each other in an effort to get to the top and the Sheep are the simple followers who get used and abused by the Pigs and Dogs. As the album progresses, the Sheep start to realise that they have been deceived all along and eventually revolt and wipe out the Dogs and Pigs in a killing spree. Pigs On The Wing is a pleasant little acoustic song that bookends the album. It shows the Sheep's feelings about people, they generally care about everyone and believe everyone else cares about them. This is followed by the 17 minute epic piece Dogs. It is a multi part song with some great guitar work from Gilmour and more great lyrics from Waters. The song describes the Dogs, who are generally deceitful, selfish and willing to stab a 'friend' in the back to further their own goals. The band also suggest that the Dogs will eventually realise what a waste it all was as they die alone, dragged down by the stone.



The Pigs are represented by the third piece Pigs (3 Different Kinds) They are the tyrants in society who rule over and use all the others to gain profit. Hypocritical and egocentric, the pigs enforce their beliefs on all the others because they think they can't be wrong. Their ego is just a wall to stop them facing the true fears they have about life that hide just below the surface. The music itself is sharp and biting - brilliant considering the context. Gilmour imitates a pig in the solo, squealing away like a madman. Sheep, arguably the best song here, is the most rocking. It obviously represents the Sheep - the simple, hard working animals who believe everyone is working hard together for the good of 'animalism' and don't realise they are being used by the Pigs and Dogs. By the end of the song they realise what is happening and revolt, succeeding in killing the Dogs and Pigs with their greater numbers. The song is one of Floyd's all time best, with a driving tempo and a great feel. The album finishes with Pigs On The Wing part 2 which ties the album together, keeping it cohesive. 



Animals is a brilliant commentary on our own society by Roger Waters and contains some of Pink Floyd's best moments musically as well. The slight Waters dominance proved to be great for the band, but he would end up taking over completely with The Wall and Final Cut which don't hit the heights of Animals. A true masterpiece.

review: This album continued Floyd's ascent to legendary status for me because of the concecpt. They touched on personal and universal levels of dealing with the class struggle. The "story" for me has always been the essence of Floyd. Without the writing you wouldn't be as entertained. Thats not to take away from the playing (which is always wonderful) but when you listen to it with the story in mind, it brings memories of our own experiences to mind and gives those lines a power that you don't get with many other bands. It's great to hear that other people have thought the same things about society and have articulated your feelings better than you can. Soooo much better. Now, on to the playing. They mesh so well together that one takes it for granted. Gilmour's playing is sublime. Nick, Roger, Rick, all turn in stellar performances. The special effects are great as well. I remember when it first came out  everyone thought it was such a big deal that you could hear the dogs barking so well. My how times and fidelity changes. Now with all that said, I don't think Animals is my favorite Floyd album because Dark Side was so semenal nothing they did afterwards beat it. It deserves a listen if you haven't heard it in awhile. It might change your mind about it.

This is THE album for me. I haven't listened to any album as much as i've listened to this one, the reasons are simple, it doesn't get boring. Animals completely blows me away every time. The reasons are simple, i think Pink Floyd peaked lyricly during Animals, and David Gilmours guitarwork stands out here as well, the ending solo on Pigs and all the solos on Dogs are top-class, not to even mention the subtle but oh so haunting keyboardworks of Richard Wright. The only reason this album isn't more well-known outside Pink Floyds fanbase is, atleast how i see it, because the songs are so damn LONG! Animals has five songs, two are below two minutes and three are above ten minutes. Putting that aside, Animals is aggresive, yet moody. At the first listen you might think it is rubbish, but the album really grows on you. Putting that aside, i want to explain every song in detail.



Dogs, the second track, is my favorite on the album. It's probably the greatest 17 minutes you'll ever experience, it has it all, great lyrics (The 'Dogs' are the ones who would do anything climb the hill, make money, and follow the 'Pigs'), increadible singing, the most amazing solos you'll ever hear, aggresive guitarwork and drums, and the background choir of keyworks enhances it's sound even more. 



10/10



Pigs (Three Different Ones) is a great track, it's more haunting and aggresive than Dogs, very in your face, but i sometimes find it repetive. Nevertheless it's a great track that just wants to make you laugh out loud at the lyrics, which are very very harsch. The pigs are the politicians and such, Roger Waters makes fun out of alot of people in this song.



9/10



Sheep opens with a nice keyboardsolo, and then erupts into an explosion of aggresive sound, something that i find really cool is the fact that the singing morphs into guitars, and you can't really tell WHEN that happens. Anyway, Sheep is another real highlight but as Pigs (Three Different Ones) it CAN get a bit repetive.



9/10

This is the least well-known masterpiece of Pink Floyd. Their fourth best album of theirs both overall and in the '70s. Animals doesn't have hits but it has concept. The story behind the album is great, and very metaphoric, it's worth getting into this music just for the lyrics alone. Closely based on George Orwell's 1984 the lyrics tell the tales of typologies of man using animal metaphors.

Pigs (Three Different Types) is the best song on here, Dogs has the most soulful guitar solos and emotional vocals, and Sheep has the most critical lyrics. The intro/outro pieces are 1 minute acoustic songs, both excellent in their own right but it's the middle three mammoths that make the album.

Get The Wall, The Dark Side of the Moon and especially Wish You Were Here before this, but this also is an undisputed masterpiece of prog rock from the masters of the genre.

Animals, Pink Floyd’s 1977 release, saw Roger Waters dominate the songwriting even more than on the previous releases. David Gilmour only co-wrote one of the three big suites, and neither Mason nor Wright received any songwriting credits.



Loosely based on George Orwell’s Animal Farm, Animals is a concept album about the socio-political state of Britain in the mid-1970s. Waters’s lyrics became increasingly on-the-nose here, but it wasn’t yet distracting. If anything, they work quite well in this instance, and the songs on Animals are some of the band’s best.



Animals is bookended by a pair of brief acoustic pieces titled “Pigs on the Wing”. On the 8-track release of this album, these two cuts were stitched into one piece, with a guitar solo by session musician Snowy White acting as a bridge between the halves.



“Dogs” was cowritten by David Gilmour, the only non-Waters songwriting credit on the album. This song starts off with a dark folk backbone, rolling along steadily. Rapid, acoustic strumming and quiet organ and synth provide a subtle backdrop for Gilmour’s strong vocal performance. After a brief, mildly bluesy instrumental interlude, “Dogs” enters its extended, down-tempo midsection. There’s a signature plaintive Gilmourian guitar solo, and Nick Mason’s drumming is restrained but artful.



It’s around the 8-minute mark of “Dogs” that Animals hits its one big snag. The drawn-out section of synth drone and dog sound effects overstays its welcome by a significant margin, and it could have been significantly shortened. In the final minutes, though, the opening theme is revisited with Waters on vocals and a wonderful twist on the lyrics.



“Pigs (Three Different Ones)” is the song with lyrics that have aged the worst. They’re unsubtly about mid-’70s British politics. The general sentiment is discernible, but many of the specific details will be lost on listeners who don’t do their reading. It’s also my least favorite of the three opuses on Animals, but being the weakest song on Animals is like being the worst type of non-pineapple pizza. It’s still pretty good.



There’s a laid-back, funky grooviness to much of the song. This is what “Have a Cigar” strove for but failed to deliver on. "Pigs" carries an air of self-satisfied smugness which suits the subject matter so well. Gilmour’s use of a talkbox is handled quite well. That’s a tool which is often mis- and over-used, but it’s deployed tactfully here.



Animals closes on my favorite of the three main tracks. “Sheep” is a snarling, biting cut that was (sadly) Rick Wright’s last huzzah as a soloist. The languid, jazzy opening piano solo is the perfect lead-in to the charging verses. The guitar (played by Waters) slashes aggressively, Wright’s organ swirls like a hurricane, and the bass (played by Gilmour) thumps and pounds.



The midsection of “Sheep” features big, bright synthesizers that complement the other instruments to build an anxious atmosphere. The vocoded pastiche of Psalm 23 is a little silly for my taste and presaged some of Waters’s most brutally unsubtle songwriting tactics which would crest on Pink Floyd’s next two albums. Still, it’s a forgivable sin in this context.

Is this Pink Floyd's best album? No, I suppose not - I've stopped trying to deny that right goes to Dark Side of the Moon. But this one's sure my favorite.

Animals is a vicious album that does not want to be your friend. It's three ten-plus minute prog monsters sandwiched between two nearly identical acoustic numbers that total less than three minutes. It's Floyd giving the finger back to punk rock, which had made them one of the faces of what was wrong with rock music, and coming out with something as punk as just about any of those bands made. It's Animal Farm turned into a musical apocalypse of electric guitars, relentless bass-lines and twisted keybords. 

I don't mean to only talk in superlatives, but just look at/listen to this thing - it's the kind of album that demands them. Gilmore never committed fiercer solo-ing to any record that I've heard. "Dogs" is some mean business, as much due to all of the extended guitar beatings as Waters' lyrics and delivery. After f-bombing Margaret Thatcher on the second of three tracks with "pigs" in the title, they move into "Sheep", which is Waters' and Richard Wright's show between the creepy mechanized Lord's Prayer spaced-out middle and the berserk, murderous bass that drives the conclusion.

Not remotely radio friendly, which doesn't help it stand up in the casual fan's mind, this is still a great, great album. If this weren't the same band that wrote "Wish You Were Here" and "Time", "Dogs" would easily be my favorite in their catalog. It's also my favorite song to play on the jukebox at bars when I wanna be a dick.

review:
Alright, well first off, best Pink Floyd album I've heard. The Wall is too long and too angsty and too up itself. Piper at the Gates of Dawn is absolutely not my thing and Dark Side of the Moon, while amazing, has always been missing a certain something. This is it. This is their masterpiece. It's deep and complex while at the same time weirdly accessible. That may be due to the opening and closing parts, which make it feel more like a complete story. The complete story in question is, essentially, Animal Farm. How they got away with this when Bowie couldn't get away with making a 1984 album three years ago is up for debate, considering the Dogs-Pigs-Sheep metaphor is taken one hundred percent from Orwell. I love how the album art reflects the same thing as well – high above a building of power, almost unseen but always there. No wonder it's become such an iconic image. Not to imply pretty much all their other album covers weren't, but that isn't the point.

The music is the point. Very proggy. Even by Floyd standards. When your album has 5 tracks, 2 don't trouble 2 minutes and the others are all 10? Seriously, it doesn't even get proggier than that. The best song on here is the middle one, completely incisive and beautifully written. That said, I never listen to this without just playing the whole thing through. It does work that well as a whole, a complete movement and as a concept. Highly, highly, highly recommended.

review:
Overrated, this is a solid album from Floyd but a clear step down in quality from Floyd's peak period of Meddle through Wish You Were Here.  Still, this is the last album with the original members that displays any truly collaborative work.  

"Dogs" is by far the best song here.  Maybe it's not a coincidence that it's the only song co-written by Gilmour and features strong vocal and guitar contributions from Gilmour as well as Wright's keyboards - in other words, it's a group effort rather than a Waters song with the others helping out.  Perhaps "Dogs" doesn't quite acheive the heights of "Shine on You Crazy Diamond" but is just a hair behind that track and is certainly as good as anything else Pink Floyd ever released.  Gilmour's singing in the first part of the song is some of his best and his guitar playing throughout is possibly the best thing he's ever recorded - the guitar solo he repeats several times in the song is just mind-numbingly stunning.  

The other tracks on this are good but not great.  "Pigs on the Wing" is a good tune ruined by Waters' singing - he sounds really nasally on that one.  "Pigs (Three Different Ones)" and "Sheep" have the same problem with Waters' singing but are also not as interesting musically.  The lyrics are also a problem throughout the album - ripping off George Orwell was bad enough but then they did nothing new with this theme.
Adore this album from start to finish. It is

"Animals" is the tenth studio album by the British Rock band, Pink Floyd. The album is widely considered an underrated or overlooked project due to the previous releases by the band being absolutely spectacular - Dark Side of the Moon, Wish You Were Here, and Meddle. Nonetheless, Animals was critically acclaimed to a point where many people started identifying it as Pink Floyd's best album - a reasonable take but honestly, I disagree. The 70s was a great year for progressive rock, thanks to artists like David Bowie and Pink Floyd. "Animals" did not bring a new element to the table though, unlike "Wish You Were Here". Thus, it would be pretty unfair to compare these both albums(I still compared them, bruh)

After the intensive release of "Wish You Were Here", Rogers decided to collect all the lyrical work made by the band to utilise it for this album. Animals are widely considered as one of the greatest albums of all time too! The steller progression throughout the album can be compared to the progression on "Wish You Were Here", nonetheless, Animals had some flaws, flaws that can't be ignored. The 70s was Pink Floyd's prime and Animals seemed to complement that opinion considering the legendary guitar solo's placed throughout the project. "Animals" differs a lot from the previous release, "Wish You Were Here" - the guitar solo's, drumlines, and synths seem to give off a happier and joyful mood instead of giving off a vibe that current R&B records try to portray. 

The album kicks off with a 1-2 minute acoustic guitar solo layered with Roger Waters vocals. In addition to the three long opuses that construct the middle of Animals, the album is bookended by two short pieces, respectively “Pigs on the Wing” parts one and two. Essentially, they are love songs, written by Waters for his wife at the time, they are brisk and simple and are able to stand as mostly disconnected from the middle three songs. "Dogs" is the second track from the album". thematically, the song connives people in life who are symbolized as dogs. “Dogs,” over 17 minutes in length, is one of the band’s longest uninterrupted songs. “Dogs” here refers to the businessmen of the world or at least the conniving people who will do anything to succeed in this world. Acting in the nature of a true dog, where only the instinct to survive takes over. Weaseling one’s way into success by “being trusted by those you lie to,” so that “when they turn their backs on you, you get the chance to put the knife in.” Gaining trust while knowing you will ruin the other unknowing person for a selfish success. But ultimately everything you do will return to harm you because other “dogs” are out there. “Just another sad old man dying of cancer” is an extremely powerful line. “Dragged down by the stone” is a theme employed by Waters in this album. The progressive elements of the track layered over slick electric guitar solo's and peaceful acoustic guitar solo's give off a hardcore atmosphere. The instruments start to distort at the 10-minute mark as the guitar synths take in control, this part of the song sounds reminiscent of "Welcome To The Machine" and it is probably one of my favourite parts of a Pink Floyd track, up there with the vocal harmonies on "Us and Them" and the iconic/uplifting concept of "Another Brick in the Wall pt 2". 

“Pigs (Three Different Ones)” is the second part of the three-song cycle on Animals (bookended by “Pigs On The Wing” parts one and two). Having already introduced the “dogs”, who elevate and enrich themselves at the expense of others, this song describes three “pigs” who perpetrate injustice and hardship whilst maintaining their grip on power. The political standpoint of this track kept on ageing better and better as Donald Trump became the president, this song also became an uplifting track for Pink Floyd fans who despised Donald Trump. The track aims at UK's capitalist societies while revering their ironies and flaws. The pointless length of the track is quite off-putting, nonetheless, the progressive guitar riffs are pretty entertaining and enjoyable. The boosted vocals which ironically sound like a Chicken during the 5-minute mark are also very enjoyable. Lyrically, “Sheep” describes a circumstance where people follow an ideology without knowing the reason. Similarly, in Animal Farm, the novella that inspired the album, the sheep were the animals that would burst blindly into a chant of “Four Legs Good, Two Legs Bad.” They did as they were told without any hesitation - they went along with the ideals of the rest of the sheep and the majority of the animals. This theme evidences itself through the lyrics of the song where the sheep follow the rules set for them. Basically, Sheep are naive people. They don’t question anything, and they accept what they are told to do or think. The high-pitched vocals support the hard-hitting drumlines and upbeat guitar solo's. This track has certainly aged like fine wine. The track was also pretty different compared to other Pink Floyd tracks which utilise soft rock elements to boost the intellectual properties of the track. This track uses the early hard rock elements to compose a masterpiece. “Pigs on the Wing, Pt. 2” serves as the second part of the book-end song, “Pigs on the Wing, Part 1.” This track is a lighter-themed song amid the anger-filled tracks, with a glimpse of hope in society. I always assumed that “the weight of the stone” referred to the gravestone, the knowledge of the brief, finite nature of life and impending death weighing down upon him, pretty dark interpretation. The track utilises similar acoustic instruments that the first track used, it was quite boring though - there was little to no progression throughout the track, nonetheless, it was just a 1-minute track. 

Track by Track Rating: 
Pigs on the Wings 1: 8/10
Dogs: 10/10
Pigs(Three Different Ones): 10/10
Sheep: 10/10
Pigs on the Wings 2: 8/10
Overall Rating: 46/50 OR 92%

"Animals" serves as yet another magnum opus from Pink Floyd's discography, proving that Pink Floyd is still one of the best rock bands of all time. The acoustic production throughout the project is layered over electrical production creating a firey clash as Pink Floyd uses "Animals" to describe humanity while justifying their claims and opinions with the awesome progression of the music. It is pretty rare when musicians meet both criteria's of being deep and making music that sounds appealing to critics/fans/media/label managers. While Rogers started taking Pink Floyd as a more political band due to "Animals", the music still can't be disregarded. The lyrics throughout the project have aged amazingly too. All in all, Animals is just another brick in the wall.

review: Previously I had given this album five stars and I had really loved it in high school, but on giving it another listen now as an older man, I have to downgrade by half a star (it will, however, remain my google account profile pic because I made the account in high school and refuse to ever update it).  There's some indescribable but insurmountable feeling of artificiality in all of Pink Floyd's classic era albums, a cold sheen that serves as a wall (haha) between them and the listener.  Roger Waters may be a very vocal socialist, but there's something profoundly repressed and conservative and, well, quintessentially British about Floyd that certainly rubs me a little bit the wrong way as an American.  CapnMarvel called them "more professional than a swimming pool full of anal-retentive accounting majors" which is very apt, given how slick everything is, but also how carefully planned out every single sound feels.  You can't accuse them of having focus-grouped their music for public accessibility (this album consists of three 10+ minute songs) but it does feel focus grouped to appeal to a certain kind of very vocal (and very annoying) pseudointellectual.  It really is no surprise that the highest esteem I ever held them in was when I was in high school, as the music and lyrics seem to be designed to interest stoners in their late teens.  For the Floyd albums that I do like all I can say is that they're good albums despite being made by Pink Floyd.  They may fundamentally be a mediocre band (as proven without a doubt by their post-Waters work) but they do have some pretty good albums under their belt.

It's funny that during this time punk rock bands like the Sex Pistols attacked bands like Pink Floyd with their images and sneering songs, but the Pistols wished they could make an album as angry and hate-filled as Animals, one of the angriest records ever made. Roger Waters filled this album with some of the most loathsome attitudes ever put on tape and it's glorious. You can really see Pink Floyd push themselves further on this album with tracks like "Dogs" and "Sheep", the latter filled with some of David Gilmour's best guitar work and Richard Wright creating synthetic sounds that are filled with oddity. It's a terrific album though I don't think it's quite the masterpiece that some make it out to me. The first time I listened to it, it didn't instantly click with me like The Dark Side of the Moon or Wish You Were Here did and its anger can be too much to handle unlike The Wall which compliments it with pained sympathy. I still think Animals is a terrific album from Pink Floyd that showed why they were one of the best damn rock bands to ever exist.

They ruined “Sheep.” They ruined the climax of the album. “Sheep” was supposed to be a song filled with mystery and intrigue, the verse beginning with “The Lord is my Shepherd, I shall not want…” is ruined by being clear as day. When Animals was released on that cold January day in 1977 it came with the lyrics on the inner sleeve, the aforementioned passage in “Sheep” was bolded in all caps. It seems that the band was telling us to actually look at the packaging and read-along with the songs.





But in streaming days, people are not bothered to read the lyrics, they just put the song on and dispose of it when it is over. Pink Floyd are a band that is against this kind of behavior, they want you to listen to the album. It’s pretty sad that this remix of a beloved album among Pink Floyd fans, not the general public – that’s Dark Side and The Wall’s business, was postponed because of issues regarding liner notes according to Roger Waters. I rather think he should be upset at how they butchered “Sheep” for the remix, or perhaps he’s just screwing with us if any of you heard the re-recording of Dark Side.





Anyone who prefers the 2018 mix over the original 1977 mix must be drinking the Kool-Aid. All the reviews of the 2018 mix on YouTube compare it to the 1994 remaster which is a load of balls in my opinion considering the remaster from 2011 during their Why Pink Floyd? campaign was the most improved from the previous CD versions according to the Steve Hoffman forums. I cannot speak for the original vinyl versions of the album as I no longer loan my mother’s friend’s copy and generally Pink Floyd and King Crimson are the two bands that sound better on CD anyways.





So then, how does the rest of the album sound? “Dogs” and the ‘Pigs’ trilogy sound okay. I’m listening to the 5.1 mix on my two-channel Edifier speakers. I believe the echo on “it’s gonna get harder… harder… harder… as you get older” was supposed to be in sync with the beat like how it was in 1977 and 2018 just adds an echo like somebody pushed a button. Also it’s probably the download I have but the ending of “Pigs (Three Different Ones)” and the beginning of “Sheep” are inconsistent with each other. This doesn’t happen in the 2018 stereo mix.





Now this is a side note, for every CD reissue of Animals I low-key wish they included the other version of “Pigs on the Wing” with a lovely guitar solo by Snowy White that appeared on the 8-track release. It showed up in a compilation of the soloist back in 1995, but I think it’s a bit unfair to not include it in the CD. There could be silence after Part 2 and the full “Pigs on the Wing” would play. Unsuspecting listeners would initially think the album restarted but then Snowy appears and they are fooled.





I believe that Animals is my Dark Side and I might have an unhealthy relationship with the original mix as I guess many Pink Floyd listeners already do. I think it’s great that new listeners get to engage with Pink Floyd in a new and exciting way, and if you like the new mix I’m all for it. But realistically speaking. 1977 Stereo (2011 remaster) > 2018 Surround > 2018 Stereo, possibly the stereo and surround mixes could be interchangeable.

Quick Disclaimer: I’ve been planning on reviewing this album for quite some time now, but I always felt my words wouldn’t do it justice. After having listened to it many times over the span of the last few weeks, I finally decided to write down my thoughts and feelings regarding what I consider to be one of the best albums of all time. 



I‘m not gonna get too deep into the lyrics and concept of the album because pretty much everyone already knows about it, I‘m just gonna mention it quickly when I talk about the tracks in depth. (Just to be clear, I’m not not talking about them because they aren’t great, but simply because so much ink has been spilled praising them by people who can do it much better than me.)



The album starts off with the little acoustic intro „pigs on the wing 1“. Just by listening to this 84 second track you can tell this isn’t gonna be anything like dark side of the moon or wish you were here. The clean production of these is gone and replaced by this loud strumming acoustic guitar and Waters‘ harsh vocal delivery. 



The next track „Dogs“ -which is the 17 minute crown jewel of this album- also begins with an acoustic guitar, that slowly builds up the atmosphere of the song. As Gilmour starts singing and characterizing the title giving dogs (ruthless capitalists who abuse and betray people who make the mistake of trusting them) he adds some psychedelic e guitar decorations that pave the way for the first guitar solo of the song.

It’s fast, short and pretty aggressive for Gilmour standards. Following that is another verse by Gilmour with his voice drenched in reverb adding an haunting and unsettling element to the song while he sings about loneliness and death in our society. After that, the instrumental slows down and stops  for a short moment: What follows is the most beautifully crafted part of any song I’ve heard in my life. It’s several guitars harmonizing and playing this slow, incredibly moving melody. For me, it feels like all of the emotions which humans go through in our modern world are represented in this part of the song. The suffering, anger and confusion all of us have felt at least once in our live but also an overwhelming sense of hope seems to be pouring out of the guitar right into my body, splitting my heart in two. Waters’ bass is also phenomenal in this part of the song and enhances everything we hear.

The section of the song is followed by a short instrumental ending with a cool drum roll which leads into yet another guitar solo, which -despite being slower and longer- feels even more angry and passionate than the first one.

Another Gilmour verse accompanied by great playing from all members ends with the infamous repetition of the word stone which echoes into oblivion for quite some time and marks the start of a longer synth passage.

It slows down the track and gives you a room to breath after all the energy you’ve just witnessed without breaking the menacing atmosphere of the rest of the track. There are also some trippy dog barks panning around your ears creating a feeling of uneasiness. 

After about three and a half minutes the acoustic guitar returns increasing the tempo again.

Waters replaces Gilmour as the vocalist for the last verse of the track (excluding the climax, which I don’t consider to be a verse) in which he directly  addresses the dogs and their behavior towards society.



Another great and fast guitar solo follows and leads to a reprise of this incredibly gorgeous guitar part i’ve raved about so much earlier on in this review. 

It flows right into the soul crushing climax of Dogs, consisting of some the most direct lyrics on the entire album, loudly roaring guitars and an organ that adds to the cathartic nature of this moment. It’s a truly perfect and epic ending for what might arguably be the greatest song ever.



The third track „Pigs“  manages to be even more aggressive and badass than dogs. 

The keyboard melody is presented first and is followed by the introduction of the main bass and guitar riff. The instrumental is dark, menacing and enhanced by waters sarcastic, anti authoritarian lyrics against the pigs (politicians). The instrumental progresses quite a bit on this track as well, leaving room for a cool talk box solo that makes gilmour sound like a grunting pig. 

The highlight on this track however has to be the final guitar solo concluding the song  after the third verse. I’ve used way too many synonyms for angry for different aspects of animals in this review already but if one passage deserves that description it’s this solo. Gilmours guitar has rarely sounded this dirty and never this brutally honest. It’s a urgent cry for change while simultaneously being a warning for the people in power. The bass melody in this part of the song is also mind blowing and it’s unusually quick as well. Waters plays around the guitar notes, he goes up and down, backing up gilmour and enhancing his solo while being on eye level with him which is pretty rare to see during a guitar solo but I guess that only shows how great they were as musical partners and how both were masters of their instrument.



Sheep is the last and shortest major track on this album with a length of 10 minutes. While I still think it’s a phenomenal track, I believe it’s „the weakest“ out of the three but that isn’t saying much when the first are pretty much the platinum standard for rock in my opinion.

It’s a great track and really interesting because gilmour takes a step back for once and gives waters and especially wright their time to shine. The keyboard and synths on this song are really cool, I especially love the parts where Waters‘ vocals morph into a thick synthesizer. I really don’t have a much else to say about this track besides the fact that when gilmour steps out of the shadow he delivers some awesome, unforgettable melodies, especially in the end section of the track. I also like the way it slowly fades out in contrast to the two previous tracks, making it a fitting closer to the album and its concept.

Overall, Sheep is a really solid track and a great „ending“ for the record.



The actual ending of animals is „Pigs on the Wing 2“ on which Waters underlines the importance of love in our modern world.



Overall, this is one of the best albums I’ve ever listened to and one of a handful of albums I‘d consider to be worthy of a „10/10“. I’ve never heard anything quite like it and even though I love all of the 70s Floyd albums from Meddle to The Wall, this one still stands on top for me due to its rough, dirty and dystopian aesthetic and its universal and urgent political and societal commentary which remains unique to this day even for PF standards.



PS: This was my first actual review for an album and I originally didn’t plan for it to be this long, thank you everybody for bearing with me!

Following the artistic and commercial success of the preceding "The Dark Side Of The Moon" and "Wish You Were Here" albums must have seemed like a challenge to Pink Floyd but for me anyway, at least on a musical level, it certainly succeeds. I do esteem the Moon-Wish duo, but for me there are better musical passages here than on either of them.



I say musical for a couple of reasons. Firstly, whatever have here is another Roger Waters-led concept album, taking Orwell's "Animal Farm" for inspiration. Now I must admit to being something of a "masses against the classes" kind of guy but let me say here and now that I got less from the lyrics here than I did the instrumentation and musicianship of the band. Dave Gilmour, in particular, plays a blinder throughout, extorting a thrilling range of sounds and playing styles within the three extended pieces which comprise the majority of the album's playing time. 



In saying that though, when the individual members coalesce on their respective instruments - and this time there are no contributions from female backing singers or saxophone, making the album sound even more cogent and band-focused than usual - they really do hit the heights.



Edited, apart from Waters two stand-alone guitar and vocal tracks, as one continuous suite of music, I detect a lighter, more melodic feel to the music, at times I could almost have been listening to Yes or Supertramp although they inevitably amp-and-ramp things up for dramatic effect when the occasion calls for it.



Floyd Mark 2's most accessible album? Well, it sure felt that way to me. Interesting to see it rated here as highly as the previous two, I certainly think it's the album of theirs I'll be returning to most in the future.

As with The Dark Side of the Moon, the best things about Animals are extramusical.



This may have been 1977, but the ridiculous dadaist notion of floating a giant inflatable pig above Battersea Power Station (incidentally one of my favourite works of architecture) is surely just a step below the Sex Pistols' Silver Jubilee boat trip down the Thames, especially given that the porker slipped its moorings and caused an airline pilot to report that he'd just seen a flying pig. The album is based around "Animal Farm" by George Orwell (whose collected works remain one of my greatest influences).



So, two pretty good ideas to start with. Unfortunately Pink Floyd proved here that not even on the day pigs learned to fly were they capable of making a record that in any way, shape or form was capable of reaching beyond its own conceit to engage with the audience. Instead they completed the most overrated trilogy of rock albums known to man.



During the accompanying tour, Roger Waters spat on a fan trying to surmount the barrier between audience and band. This provoked soul-searching on a scale significant to support the giant spectacle that became The Wall, at the very moment that punk was busy kicking down any number of barriers, not least that between performer and fan.



Yet Pink Floyd remained blissfully unaware that the real wall was that which surrounded their music. Its claustrophobia has less to do with any imagined barrier between band and audience, and rather more to do with its self-obsession, its need to create its own private space. It is music for the middle classes, for people who live in those carefully demarcated slices of private space bought for way too much money and mortgaged to the hilt.



Which suggests that while Animals may have started out with "Animal Farm" as its source, it had little hope, even at the outset, of adequately engaging with Orwell's discussion of power. If Animals is anything, it is the sound of the Politburo extolling the virtues of the peasantry from its own lofty heights, even as it requires it to live in relative poverty and powerlessness. Or, for that matter, for all of its pretence at social critique, it is essentially "caring" conservatism thirty years in advance. The same paradox exists at the heart of both.



Yes, Pink Floyd tried to grapple with the paradoxes of its own position throughout its career, but I'd rather have the Clash's Complete Control any day of the week. Because however aware Pink Floyd may have been about its own shortcomings, it remained on Animals a bunch of suburban professionals moaning about the state of the nation from its safe middle England home, the dadaist impulses of the inflatable pig incident smothered by immaculate lawns and perfectly trimmed hedgerows.

review:
My friend Michele, who just registered to RYM, rated Animals 5 stars. Visiting the page I noticed it's ranked #2 for 1977 and #87 overall.

"Fuck, wasn't it a load of shit? Ok, it's almost a decade since last time I listened it, let's play it back".

...

C'mon!
You all got to be crazy!
This IS a load of dried crap indeed!

They seem Pink Floyd trying to emulate Pink Floyd without any sign of new inspiration.
It must have gone like: "Dudes, we sold a lot of Wish You and Dark Sides, so why don't we do them again -just more anonymous, insincere and rhetorical, all centered on a topic of sure grip like Animal Farm- and then repackage everything?".
Let's save some bridge (founded in the central "Pigs" and in "Sheep", nothing special anyway) and of course the splendid cover art (the usual great work of Hipgnosis and Peter Cristopherson), as for the rest I will never comprehend what you all find in this 40 minutes of thin air.

Outside of Floyd circles (and this website), ask anybody about Pink Floyd and they say Dark Side, Wish You Were Here, and The Wall.  If you say "what about Animals?" and they will say "oh yeah that one too".  Animals is usually forgotten about, but it stands as one of Pink Floyd's greatest achievements, and I think it is their best album alongside Wish You Were Here.



All Waters' era albums have a theme, or subject most if not all songs are based around, and Animals is no different.  Now I don't want to mess this up, but the album seems to be a commentary about business and corruption, like "Dogs" is about businessmen who do whatever the "Pigs" tell them to do.  "Pigs" are like the leaders or big shots of the bunch, and "Sheep" are the lowest class, who are always being bossed around and told what to do by the "Dogs" and "Pigs".



Floyd album covers are always great, and this one is no exception.  It is my favorite as it just seems to drag me in even though I can't explain what it is about.  The cover is some kind of plant/factory with the trademark Pink Floyd Pig in between some smokestacks.  Weird, but it is indulging and fits the album perfectly.



The album is bookended by two short acoustical pieces called "Pigs on the Wing", and it seems to be a fitting opening and ending, despite the album's overly dark tone.



Then let the epics commence!  The main bulk of Animals is three 10+ minute epics, beginning with the 17 minute "Dogs".  I need to say this now, this is probably Waters' lyrical peak, not just this song, the entire album is pure lyrical genius.  Look at this excerpt from "Dogs",



"After awhile, you can work on points for style, like the club tie, and the firm handshake, a certain look in the eye and an easy smile, you have to be trusted by the people you lie to, so that when they turn their backs on you, you'll get the chance to put the knife in."



Now THAT is great writing, and it is that great for the entire album.  And the music is tremendous as well.  Although Wright is mostly buried (there are a few great parts though), this is Gilmour's finest hour, with some epic solos throughout the songs, especially "Dogs", the best song he ever played (forget "Comfortably Numb", this is better).



Near the end of "Dogs", Waters takes over on vocals and sings the rest of the album.  This album is a turning point, a point when Waters began to take over as leader of The Floyd, and we all know what would happen afterwards.



"Big man, pig man, ha ha, charade you are!"  "Pigs"(Three Different Ones) opens with an oinking pig and a haunting sounding piece from Wright, as well as a great bassline.  Great buildup to another masterful song.  The 10 minute monster is a scathing look at the people who run countries and businesses and tell everyone what to do.  Waters really rips into them too.



"Sheep", the lowest class, who do the Dogs and Pigs bidding, is another piece that needs to be mentioned as one of Floyd's best compositions.



"Have you heard the news?  The Dogs are dead!  You better stay home and do what you're told, get out of the road if you want to grow old."



So apparantly the Sheep revolt and kill the Dogs...or something like that, but still have to deal with the Pigs.  Interesting.  I could go on for hours, but I won't.  You know what?  Forget Wish You Were Here, Animals is my favorite Floyd album.  Spread the word about Animals people, it's too good to be buried and forgotten about.

Dedicated to Syd Barrett, Wish You Were Here was probably the most straight forward record of Pink Floyd's heyday in the 70s.  Gone are the clocks and cash registers of Dark Side of the Moon and the record predates the rock-opera histrionics of The Wall.  Everything on Wish You Were Here is great, from the slowly building nine parts of "Shine on You Crazy Diamond," which climax into a terrific chorus, to the all-time class rock ballad and title track, "Wish You Were Here."  Besides the bookends to "Welcome to the Machine," Pink Floyd seems to have confined its sound to the traditional elements of a rock band, as each of the songs seems to be built on a Gilmour guitar line and/or solo.  Gilmour is particularly impressive on the funky, "Have a Cigar."  The songs rotate between indictments of the record industry and dedication to Barrett.  Perhaps because of this subject matter, the record is often warmer and more direct than the prog-rock exercises that seem to dominate Pink Floyd's 70s catalog.

After the massive success of Dark Side of the Moon, Pink Floyd took a full year to tour, then decided to dedicate their newly found stardom to former bandmate and Pink Floyd founder Syd Barrett on their next album, Wish You Were Here.  The album was as equally successful as its predecessor, and solidified their spot as one of the best bands of the '70s.



Released September 15, 1975, Wish You Were Here begins with the sprawling "Shine On You Crazy Diamond, Pts. 1-5", one of the band's crowning achievements.  The first six minutes or so, Floyd works out a jam, then the band gets into the main tempo and the first lyrics come out so sweetly, "Remember when you were young, you shined like the sun, Shine on you crazy diamond!"  The next song, "Welcome to the Machine", is a dramatic spin on the rough music industry with no real instruments, just synthesizer and a few cymbal crashes.  "Have a Cigar" also deals with the rough-and-tumble task of getting a record deal and making it big, with lead vocals by blues singer Roy Harper.  The song is much lighter than "Welcome to the Machine", the guitar is funky, and the vocals are smooth (Harper is a much better singer than both Waters or Gilmour.)  "Wish You Were Here" is a soft, melancholy track dedicated to Barrett like "Shine On You Crazy Diamond".  Waters, once again, shows his great lyricism, his songs never sound hokey, they are always delivered with passion, and this is no exception.

Pink Floyd is truly the sum of its parts (that those parts didn't necessarily like one another might been a good thing, given the themes of detachment and the dark underbelly of the music), and here the four bandmates came together to create the most sublime record of the rock era. The only song that might date a little is Welcome to the Machine, but even that feels very natural given the context of the album. It's a cold, industrial song.



According to a magazine (Q I think), Damian Albarn described Pink Floyd as a band without passion (very ironic for him to say that, given his most successful project, Gorillaz, is quite Floydian), and I would suggest he listen to Wish You Were Here again. There's passion, but it's Pink Floyd passion--distant, intellectual, misanthropic--but passion nevertheless, and bottled inside these forty five minutes or so is Pink Floyd, collectively, at their most powerful, emotional, and crafty. And let's never forget the album's lush, hypnotic sound, courtesy of the late Rick Wright. 



Argue what you will the credentials of this band, but Wish You Were Here is as worthy of the term masterpiece as any other work of genius from the last fifty years. It's a perfect chemistry of stars.

Absolutely incredible album. A showcase of utter brilliance. 

1. Starting off as unassuming as any album could. The synthesizer hit every note in perfect timing. Pink Floyd show what John Densmore of The Doors once said... "It's not always about what you play, sometimes it's about what you not playing". Pink Floyd show us they have as much patience and genious to create such a masterpiece. Dave Gilmour comes in with his pitch perfect Fender sound. Again, knowing exactly what to do - with the syth growing all the time. Then the next part starts with a completely different Gilmour tone - but, again perfect and playing what need to be played. The first crescendo with Nick Mason making his entrance along with Waters who is soothing and caressing the song along it's way. Some dirty riffs from Gilmour and then awesome soling, again. Wright also, not one fault. Gilmours voice, unbelievably sweet the the crescendos of "Shine". The guitar interplaying with everything then soling. The sax then enters and you thought a song couldn't get better?!

2. Straight into "Welcome to the Machine". Your typical Floyd sound effects with the crisp acoustic. The voices sound really good singing some obvious words. Wright with some more great synth work. The rhythm from Gilmour and timing is great, mixed perfectly with the synth. "It's all right, we told you what to dream".Wright is really strong and professional with the synth, incredible. 

3. Bass and guitar start off the funky sarcastic and satirical song. Gilmour as always just playing and playing so damn well, notes where notes should be played and a perfect tone. Real good! The progression of this song and all Floyd songs is just incredible to behold. Then the fade out or more like the music sucked away.

4. Obviously the most popular song on this album, by far. And it is a beautiful song. Started off, still sounding far away, acoustic. The second guitar comes in, beautiful. The piano in the background is good. "We're just two lost souls swimming in a fish bowl year after year" Gilmour singing the guitar behind the guitar, nice.

5. Then floating back the way we started. This one starting with the bass, 2 notes. And the wind, of course. Then Gilmour entering magnificently, Waters also brilliant. Synth once again perfect. (Notice I use the word perfect a lot with Floyd :)) Gilmour starts to run away with it! Again, the progression of the song, impeccable. It's just so strong and smooth, crescendoing too well. Damn! Too good. Then it breaks into a little jam session. It then  returns to it's earlier being an goes out as it came in, slow, patient and very analytically. 

Perfect!

review: Track one is a masterpiece. All else is a bit pretentious but still beautiful. Not as pop-friendly as Dark Side of the Moon and maybe not as ambitious as The Wall. But still part of an amazing trio of albums.

Prog rock is in fashion now. How else could it be that an album that only consists of five songs, most of them heavily overlengthy, is currently rated 4.28 by 36,841 people on RYM (edit: updated 12 October, 2019)? The number would make sense if Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band was rated by even more numerous thousands of RYMers but, as we know, it isn't. And what about the average rating? Do you really think it is absolutely that good? I consider it more like fashion – or a scientific paradigm: as long as it is 'valid' or 'reliable' to hold Wish You Were Here in a high position, thousands of people do so.

1973 was to prog what 1967 was to psych. Beside The Dark Side of the Moon by Pink Floyd, Tubular Bells by Mike Oldfield became a worldwide smash, and as I have noted elsewhere, that was when 'progressive' – as a musical attribute – lost its connection to the socio-spiritual progress. A couple of years later, Pink Floyd released Wish You Were Here, including almost 26 minutes (divided into two tracks) of a giant epic called "Shine on You Crazy Diamond." Listening to the first part, it takes over four minutes before the drums appear, and again more than four minutes before vocals appear. I claim, this was Floyd's own Tubular Bells. And of course this means – I'm sorry, Mike O, you did better with your pop songs! – that the 1975 incarnation of Pink Floyd had lost some of the progress within. This wasn't their first time: Ummagumma and Atom Heart Mother in particular were slightly regressive rock records. Lyrically, "Crazy Diamond" is positive about the mental adventures of Syd Barrett, but at the same time it seals the fact that after Syd's departure, in the circle of Pink Floyd, those adventures were going to be over. Musically, I think the latter part of "Crazy Diamond" may be even better than the former. None of it is bad, anyway.

"Welcome to the Machine" is overlengthy and actually a bit useless. Then comes "Have a Cigar", possibly a link between "Time" and the more rhythmic fragments on The Wall. A nice track. The title song is a worthwhile classic of course. But that is all. Except a few interesting pieces of cover art.

So, Wish You Were Here is not a bad album. Unlike the first two Velvet Underground albums, it is real music. One, two or even four of the five songs could be part of an excellent package like The Dark Side of the Moon, but hopefully not in a pompously overlengthy form. None of the tracks is plain bad, in fact the album is quite good (of course), but come on: is this really modern music at its most essential?

I gave this a 4.5/5 only because, to me, a 5 star album is perfect and can be listened to in any mood. WYWH *is* perfect, but it requires a mellow mood to listen to it.  

There's simply not a bad track on this album.  Shine On You Crazy Diamond is one of the greatest psychedelic/spacey songs ever written. From the opening keyboard section, the mellow Dave Gilmour opening solo, the famous twanging theme (you know what I mean...dun DUN dun DUUUNNN)...the verses are perfect, both lyrically and musically...then a great sax solo peeling off into Welcome to the Machine, a quintessential Floydian dystopic song.  Have a Cigar is the sole rocker of the album, with shades of Money and Young Lust. 

Wish You Were Here is standard issue for anyone who has ever learned how to play acoustic guitar. It's a great sing-a-long for any group of people with good musical taste.  

Then back to the spacey parts 6 - 9 of SOYCD.  You could float away on the last part.  

Bottom line, one of the best albums ever made.

I was pleasantly surprised when I had a re-listen to The Dark Side of the Moon a week ago. I had some preconceptions about it, but I loved it -- it had a great musical flow and balance between experimentation and pop, and it's one of there rare pop albums that really has something to say about the conditio humana. So I was looking forward to this one -- which, however, turned out to be pretty much how I originally feared Pink Floyd would be.

Musically it's all nice and spacey and proggy and complexly layered stuff -- but what else is there to the music? When it's nice and beautiful, it's tends to be overly long and boring and even lounge-y after a while (they got it right on "Wish You Were Here" though). When it starts getting interesting, they kill the song with an extremely annoying 70s synth solo ("Welcome to the Machine"), which they never did on DSotM.

If only the lyrics would have been half as great as on Dark Side! Here, you get two esoteric rants about the music industry, and the rest is supposed to be about the band missing Syd Barrett so much. They did that one on Dark Side too ("Eclipse"), and it feels more genuine there than filling three quarters of an album with that stuff here. Especially considering that, to my knowledge, nobody of the band even went to visit him since he had left.

I know I'm a little harsh here. It's not a bad album per se, and there is some good stuff to be found here. But I just can't understand why this album has the same standing as Dark Side of the Moon. The most interesting thing about this album is the great cover (which I never realized actually is a real photograph) -- and reading up on this weird story when Barrett suddenly turned up in the studio during the final recording of "Shine On You Crazy Diamond", with nobody recognizing him at first (source):

In 1975 a strange reunion took place at EMI Studios, attributable, Jerry Shirley feels, to Syd's uncanny sixth sense of timing. "The last time I saw him was possibly the last time the guys in the Floyd saw him, too. They were putting the finishing touches on Wish You Were Here. Earlier that day Dave Gilmour had gotten married and they had to work that night, so EMI had this roundtable dinner in the canteen for them. Across the table from me was this overweight Hare Krishna-looking chap. I thought maybe it was just someone who somebody knows. I looked at Dave and he smiled, then I realized it was Syd. The guy had to weigh close to 200 pounds and had no hair on his head. It was a bit of a shock, but after a minute I plucked up enough courage to say hello. I introduced my wife and I dunno, I think he just laughed. I asked him what he was doing lately. 'Oh, you know, not much: eating, sleeping. I get up, eat, go for a walk, sleep.'"

That night the band finished the album and were playing back the final mix of "Shine On You Crazy Diamond." "When the song ended Roger Waters turned to Syd and said, 'Well, Syd what do you think of that ?' He said, 'Sounds a bit old.'"

I'm all with Syd on that one.

review:
Reviewed as part of my 'top albums from every year challenge' over on stickaroundpodcast.com/albumchallenge

-------------------

As veterans of these lists by now, Pink Floyd return with their ninth album, recorded at the famous Abbey Road Studios in London, and regarded by keyboardist Richard Wright and guitarist David Gilmour as their best album. It initially received mixed reviews, but has since been hailed a masterpiece, and one of the greatest albums of all time. It reached number 1 in the UK on release, and has sold over 13 million copies since.

The album opens with  Shine on You Crazy Diamond Pt 1-5, the final parts of which close out the album. It begins as an instrumental, Mason’s gentle and basic drumming as crisp and clear as the titular diamond, and Gilmour’s guitar work some of the most gorgeous I’ve ever heard, a master of doing a lot with a little. The song bubbles over in the finale as the vocals come in, when it becomes clear that the song is a tribute to Syd Barrett, who had left the band seven years earlier as I’ve described in a previous post, “Remember when you were young/You shone like the Sun.” The lyrical themes are generally of a life lived too fast, and Dick Parry’s saxophone adds another layer of emotion, without ever approaching cheesiness.

Welcome to the Machine is generally interpreted as a scathing critique of the music industry, the lines “What did you dream? It's alright, we told you what to dream”  summing thins up pretty nicely. Gilmour’s vocals are quite monotone, and rather desperate, like a man who’s been worn down by said machine. The piercing synth spells doom, and wouldn’t be out of place in 2001: A Space Odyssey as it completely obliterates the acoustic guitar backing, like the arrival of a machine-dominated future, crushing that of a more folky past. This music industry vibe continues on Have a Guitar,  a song about a music industry exec creating a monster out of a new prodigy, “And did we tell you the name of the game, boy?/We call it riding the gravy train” Roy Harper howls in a guest appearance - Waters and Gilmour couldn’t get their own vocals to work so asked Harper who was recording next door - as that synth looms in the background. Gilmour’s trying to fight it off with lick after lick, before it eventually seemingly gets sucked into a black hole, announcing the arrival of the album’s title track, one of the band’s many masterpieces.

Wish You Were Here is the album’s shortest, and most instrumentally basic song, largely propelled only by Gilmour’s acoustic guitar and vocal it opens with a timeless acoustic guitar solo and those immortal words, “So you think you can tell/Heaven from Hell?” sung in a melody like the most universal of lullabies. I’m unsure if a song led by acoustic guitars has ever sounded so huge, such a force of nature. Waters’ lyrics and Gilmour’s gentle guitar licks combine as if they’d both drained down from the same mountain. The final chorus is one of the most affecting ever written, expressing an alienation that I’m sure we can all relate to from time to time,

How I wish, how I wish you were here
We're just two lost souls swimming in a fishbowl year after year
Running over the same old ground, what have we found?
The same old fears, wish you were here

The ocean winds bring us the last track, Shine on You Crazy Diamond Pts. 6-9, a 12 minute reprise of the opening track that is more menacing as the lyrics focus more on how Syd has gone somewhere - perhaps to the dark side of the moon - and how Roger Waters will be joining him there. Gilmour’s guitar work dominates the track, as he throws out more dissonant, edgy lines, than the ones in the album’s opening. The vocals appear around halfway and stick around for barely a minute before we’re launched into a 6 minute instrumental outro to the album that is some of the band’s most stunning musical world-building with synths, pedal steels and guitars working in perfect unison to create a sad, and yet infinitely pretty universe. 

Wish You Were Here is the sad deconstruction of the rock ‘n’ roll dream and how it destroyed one of their best friends. It’s hard to imagine a more beautiful musical representation of this, and that, along with its undeniable beauty, is what makes it a masterpiece.

Song Picks: Shine on You Crazy Diamond (all parts), Wish You Were Here

With the exception of "Welcome To The Machine" which is just horrible, this album is a winner from front to back.  The title track has become sing-along cliche but sometimes songs end up that way because they really are great.  That song is great.  "Have A Cigar" has funny, vicious and truthful lyrics and some of Gilmour's finest guitar work.  Roy Harper's bellowing guest lead vocal works perfectly.  The long form tribute to their doomed old founder is moving and shows a heart that this band often took great pains to hide.  With the exception of Gilmour, they don't have the chops that should enable them to pull off so many lengthy instrumental passages but they do pull it off by varying the tempos and the sounds often enough and with enough melody to keep the listener focused.  For my money, their best album.

My first listen of this album completely through and let me tell you I was pleasantly surprised. While I am not too fond of tracks over the 8 minute mark, this album contained two that worked their timestamp to perfection. My favorite tracks on the album are "Have a Cigar" and "Wish You Were Here", but the album is very solid through and through. The build and evolution to start the album off with the first 5 parts of "Shine On You Crazy Diamond" set the tone for the rest of the piece, beautiful orchestra of instruments led by the ferocious strum of the guitar. The album builds in emotion and starts off heavy but fades to soft and light as the tracks progress towards the end, parts 6-9 of "Shine On You Crazy Diamond", which delivers the perfect goodbye to the album. Loved everything about this album, especially the cover art, and can't wait to really dig deeper into Pink Floyd's discography, rather than just scraping the surface as I have been doing for years.

The superior album. Easily Pink Floyd's best, a course correction from every indulgent progressive artistic statement they attempted to make that couldn't be encapsulated in the line "Syd, we miss you." There's five songs in here -- an overlong mess that somehow benefits from being overlong, a dream pop song, the prog interpretation of the Velvets, one of the best acoustic songs of all time, and a Radiohead song.

Will never cease to astonish and perplex how this record has an emotional core most other Pink Floyd doesn't, yet it's also chilly AF. Save for "Welcome to the Machine"-which is a bad song that works in context-the rest of Wish You Were Here is first-rate. If Stanley Kubrick was a musician, this is exactly what his stuff would sound like: cold, clinical, flawlessly played and produced, stunning.

I'll just cut to the chase and say I think this album does many things right. I think the production and effects used still hold up super well today, and don't feel dated at all. The artistic choices such as the transitions between songs are super well thought out, it feels like you are flying from song to song. And overall the crazy diamond series is pretty good. That's what kept this album enjoyable.

When it comes to the things I'm critical of, it's hard to put my finger on. Something about the lyrics and message seem almost naive and surface level to me. I believe Syd Barrets mind was complex and convoluted beyond all hell, and something about even the use of the word crazy, and saying you wish he was there seems a little too surface level for my tastes. Never really liked David Gilmours guitar playing but it's at its best here I suppose. With Pink Floyd I've never really felt their full potential, I see what others adore about them and it's fully clear that it IS there. But it doesn't scratch my itches too much. Also something seems disingenuous with the artistic liberties taken here. I think I'd rather listen to Bowie.

All in all. It's an album that I found interesting, with flaws, but I didn't find it jawdropping.

The production on this album is amazing too, from the amazing guitar solos on "Shine On You Crazy Diamond" to the great displays of organ an synthesizer on "Welcome To The Machine". In March of this year, I lost my grandfather to cancer. I can't even begin to explain how I felt back then, because at that time I didn't know how to describe how I felt. The only time where I felt as if I had any clarity was when the song Wish You Were Here played. At the time, I wasn't a huge fan of the song, even though I regarded this album very highly, but I never put the song on by choice. Especially not at that point in my life. But then one day the song came on in my shuffle. And I really paid attention to what I was hearing. And I broke down and started crying. Between then, and the day my grandfather died, I only heard the song one more time, and nearly cried then as well, but forced myself not to because I was in school. And after his death, after I finally found that clarity I had been looking for back then, the song meant even more to me.

This is a rip-off, right? Another example of EMI milking the cash cow that is Pink Floyd? - Probably. Everyone who wants it has a decent CD copy of "Wish You Were Here" by now? - Again, undoubtedly. And yet this double CD is an essential purchase for what is on the Second CD. 

"Wish You Were Here" came out in 1975 - a very different album from the one we expected. In 1974 I heard a bootleg of their Wembley Concert - it had three tracks "Shine On You Crazy Diamond", "Raving & Drooling" & "You've Got To Be Crazy" - The sound quality was excellent and the tracks were brilliant. I suppose we thought this was how the yet unnamed follow up to "Dark Side Of The Moon" would be - Of course, it wasn't. Anyway, I am sure someone close to Floyd had something to do with the bootleg as here are the same tracks as magnificent as ever and running at 50 mins. In addition there is a track from the ill-fated "Household Objects" album - which is called "Wine glasses" - but sounds more like a Rick Wright organ instrumental. And there are also - "Have A Cigar" with Roger Waters singing rather than Roy Harper and a version of "Wish You Were Here" with violin by Stephane Grappelli - which are both terrific. 

So is this a rip-off? - Well probably but I would recommend this album - particularly the second CD to anyone who has ever liked anything by Pink Floyd.

My wife has never heard a version of "Wish You Were Here" without Stephane Grappelli on violin.  Doesn't that blow your mind a little?  I know it does mine.

It also blows my mind just how much worse a song "You Gotta Be Crazy" is than "Dogs".  I mean, you know, they're pretty much the same song, except that somehow "You Gotta Be Crazy" is wince-inducingly terrible, and "Dogs" is fist-pumpingly awesome.  Score one for sprinkling a little fairy dust on the bastard.

An act of aggression and cynicism, Wish You Were Here is not so much a legitimate statement, but an apology for their fans for being such insensitive assholes.

My ratings for the two vary by 0.5 stars, and really my rating is largely for Shine on You Crazy Diamond, which is pretty impressive and deserves a good deal of merit, although the other tracks are alright (or at least, Welcome to the Machine is pretty good anyway). 

Reading the reviews here is amusing. It seems everyone else pretty much orgasms over stuff like this (I was going to take my sexual analogy a lot further than that, but it was a tad too uncomfortable to type).

I'm happy to keep this as the only Pink Floyd album I possess, although diehard fans can tell me I'm wrong if they so desire.
Can you really hate an album that has Shine On You Crazy Diamond
column8: Welcome to the Machine
column9: Have a Cigar AND Wish You Were Here? It's kind of heartbreaking.

review: Gets half a star for Roy Harper, specifically the bit when he goes, "Every body else is just greeennnnn", which always reminds me of my old mischievous shool friend Billy. A nutcase was Billy, and I often wonder what prison he now resides.

Easily easily easily a superior album to Dark Side of the Moon. So much so that I honestly have to laugh at the status quo of Dark Side being the pinnacle of Floydom. There's no way it can compete here. Remember what I wrote about Layla back in 70'? Remember the rant about what happens when a technical wizard finally taps into the essence of soul? It almost invariably results in pure awesome magic. And Wish You Were Here is that same exact scenario, maybe even a better example. Because instead of unrequited love Roger Waters was tapping into much deeper darker sadder depths of human emotion. This album is a concept album like Dark Side, but instead of bare single noun ideas like MONEY, DEATH, or TIME, this is an album about absence. No....not about absence. A bad concept album is about something. A good one EMBODIES that something. This album IS the sound of absence. It manifests through moody musings about absent friends and lost relatives, as well as looks at the soulless automaton of the record industry which strangely works very well next to the more obviously about absence tracks. They share a sound of emptiness and sadness toward missing something integral in life. The obvious centerpoint of all this of course is Syd Barret, long gone into a private hell of mental illness, it's clear that no matter the track his ghost haunts the precedings. In fact he literally walked in on the studio sessions at one point, looking like a miserable wreck of the man he knew and speaking nonsense he drove his former bandmates to tears. It's not a fun album, easily one of the most sad records I've ever heard. But like many of the best LP's about sad moods it's also achingly beautiful. And shows Floyd finally being economical and playing what needs to be played. Retarded tape experiments ala Ummagumma have no place here. And in fact the most gorgeous sad moment of the whole thing is the simple plaintive guitar lines that open the title track, just a strumming guitar mixed into the back of your ears. Echoing out all that needs to be said or arranged... It's a moment of maturity and true genius to leave in such a thing as is. A motion that stupider prog groups (Kansas) and more juvenile prog groups (Rush) wouldn't think of, even though it's the most basic thing in the world. Prog acolytes may scratch their head wondering why when the Punk revolution swept their genre forever into the underground, why certain bands like Pink Floyd were virtually unaffected. It's obvious to me. It's because the talent and insight shown on a record like this so utterly transcends genre and factionalism. It hits right at the bone and heart even if it is complex in construction and arrangement. And no matter what, people will always respond to that.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
Creo que toda persona tendría que escuchar ''Dark Side of the Moon'' en la soledad de su cuarto a oscuras para saber lo que es la música. 10,0

En realidad podría escribir solo eso y estaría diciendo toda la verdad, pero eso no le haría justicia. ''Dark Side of the Moon'' se puede explicar con muchas, o con pocas palabras. Esa fue la versión corta.

Una de mis pocas convicciones en cuanto a la música es que existe solo un crítico totalmente fiable: El Tiempo. Y la mayor prueba que debe atravesar todo disco es el del paso del tiempo. Es la prueba última. Se pueden decir muchas cosas hoy en día de un disco salido ayer, pero solo el tiempo, despiadado por naturaleza, borra lo inutil y recuerda lo imprescindible, lo importante. Hoy en día, ''Dark Side of the Moon'', con sus casi 35 años (1973), sigue conmoviendo, emocionando y volando las cabezas de las nuevas generaciones en la era del ringtone y los Black Eyed Peas. Hoy en día, Dark Side of the Moon, sigue sonando tan progresivo, tan experimental, tan... cargado de una fuerza única, emotiva, casi mística, trascendental, como fue hace 34 años. Por supuesto, siempre estaran los puristas que diran que Wish You Were Here es un disco mucho mejor o que la mejor etapa de Floyd es la de Barret o la de Meedle. Tal vez tengan razon, pero ¿que importa?. Creo que Dark Side of the Moon es el mejor álbum de la historia simplemente porque condensa en palabras y sonidos mortales el nacimiento, la vida, el amor, la locura, el tiempo y el inevitable final. Todo lo que hace a la vida humana en poco más de 40 minutos. Y de una forma tan hermosa que es dificil describirla y mucho más imitarla. Dark Side of the Moon esta lleno de grandes letras, todos himnos de esos que conmueven el corazón. La música es el resultado obvio de las letras, despues de todo, algo tan complejo como la vida no puede ser retratado de una forma simple. Es en la música, el verdadero alma de este arte, donde yace el corazón (que nace con el comienzo del disco y termina con un último latido en "Eclipse") del álbum. Desde los coros celestiales de "Us & Them" a la psicodelia de "Any Color You Like" el disco atraviesa todos los colores del prisma, simbolo inconfundible del álbum. Y todo está tan cargado de un ''pesimismo'', un sentido de ''trascendencia'' que solo los más fans de Tool dicen encontrar. 

Como dije, creo que toda persona debería escuchar alguna vez ''Dark Side of the Moon'' en la soledad y oscuridad. Tal vez así se podría resumir de forma perfecta la vida.

review:
What could I possibly add that over 1000 reviewers before me haven't already said.
This is the penultimate prog rock Lp.....

It's been nuked a bit in my mind by overfamiliarity but I still put it on now and then and still get a kick out of it.

However I think the original SQ Quad Mix is the best mix of this Lp. I don't like the stereo mixes at all. In fact "Any Colour You Like" is ruined on the stereo mix. A synth track is removed on the Quad mix and it sounds MUCH better and less congested. You can hear all of Dave Gilmours guitar solo clearly, which is buried under a synth, on the stereo mix.

The Australian SQ Quad Lp's are the most common (The were the ONLY one's available through most of the 70's in OZ) and not expensive.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
The music on Dark Side of the Moon is very varied and incorporates several new elements that haven´t been heard on earlier releases by Pink Floyd. Lots of studio tricks, female backing and lead vocals and dominant sections with saxophone soloing are some of the new features on the album. We´re of course also treated with the trademark mellow vocals but also more rocking rough ditto which is great for the variation on the album. All songs seque into each other either by short sound effect interludes or they simply sound like parts of one longer song which for example is the case with Us And Them and Any Colour You Like. Songs like Breathe, Time and Brain Damage have always been favorites of mine but it´s songs like the experimental instrumental On the Run with its extensive use of studio tricks and the female led The Great Gig In the Sky that stand out from the rest as being vastly different from anything else Pink Floyd had released up until then. 



There is a notable increase in the use of synth on the album compared to earlier releases which gives the album a fresh sound. David Gilmour´s guitar soloes on the album also need a mention as they are fantastic. So full of emotion. Many guitarists could learn so much from David Gilmour. Putting equal amounts of great emotion into each note can sometimes be much harder that playing faster than the speed of lightning.



The production on the album is outstanding. The sound gives the album a timeless quality.

Dark Side of the Moon is a classic progressive rock album.

My personal favorites from this album were "On the Run", "Time", and "Money". However, this album is a bit of a departure from Pink Floyd's previous sound. When I think of Pink Floyd I think of songs like "Echoes" and "Atom Heart Mother". Dark Side of the Moon seems to be a bit more radio friendly. The songs are much shorter and they don't incorporate any weird sound effects like they did on Ummagumma.  Nonetheless this is one hell of an album and I can definitely see why its viewed as being one of the greatest albums of all time, even if it is a little less intricate than what I usually hear from this band.
