album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
On the fantastic Radio 4 show 'Desert Island Discs', celebrities pick 5 songs that they would take with them if they found themselves stranded on a desolate island. Since the show started in 1942, 'Dark Side Of The Moon' is the album that has had more tracks chosen from it than any other. 

If I was given the chance to be on the show, I wouldn't  choose any songs from it. In fact if I was stranded on a desert Island armed only with an old record player and I came across this album on Vinyl, I'd simply use it as a hat to block out the sun. As we have a superb variety of hats in the modern world then I don't see the point of me ever needing this album. Fashion is all about taste but walking around with a vinyl of 'Dark Side Of The Moon' on your head would be an unusual step. Your mates would probably call you 'dark side of the loon' or something equally as witty like 'Shithead', which would not only be funny but also entirely accurate. 

Basically this album is something that I would like to go away. Every fucker loves it which probably means that my hatred towards it is about 25% unjustified. This doesn't matter though. For every person you meet who doesn't like this album, there's another 50 who will tell you again and again its the greatest thing ever. This phenomenon doesn't look like ending any time soon as generation after generation go through their early 20's smoking weed and lying to each other that the album goes perfectly with "The Wizard Of Oz".  

'Dark Side Of The Moon' is basically like a shit that won't flush and its only saving grace is that it acts as a fine example of my belief that the world is full of disgustingly boring people. 

Nice sleeve though.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
Look - trying to say anything about "Dark Side of the Moon" is like trying to criticise the Mona Lisa- it is a record that is beyond that and it has sold more copies than almost anything ("Thriller" and "Back In Black" - in case you were wondering) 

So I was at The Zonita Cinema and before the film they played some of "Dark Side of the Moon", so now I am home, I thought I would play all of it. I have a couple of CD versions - the one I am playing is the 2011 "Experience" edition that has a second "live" CD. More of that another time - but this sounds awesome and far superior to any vinyl version that I have heard.

And yet I think "Dark Side of the Moon" is flawed. What was Side 2 in this tale of madness is damn near perfect - "Us & Them" is quite brilliant (Written by Roger Waters and the great Richard Wright) but what was Side 1 is one track short, I think.

I do not think this is the best Pink Floyd album, either. Besides the Syd Barrett inspired "Piper At The Gates of Dawn" and my favourite, "Saucerful of Secrets", "Animals" and "Wish You Were Here" are better, But that really doesn't matter because "Dark Side of the moon" is loved by many so what I think is totally irrelevant. But I tell you what - on a night like this, there really is nothing better,

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
7.3



One of the times where doing this massive list has really forced me to revisit and ultimately adjust my opinion on a lot of lauded albums that I may have blindly rated highly in the past(my old score was a 9.3). But coming back to this outside of the songs I actually love(Time, Money, Brain Damage), I'm decidedly disappointed with how samey(wether it's supposed to be or not) so much of what's here is and incredibly innocuous in both the compositions and the lyrics. First off, I have always thought "Great Gig" is one of the most overrated songs of all time. The melodramatic and almost absurd female vocals are so obviously intended to be as gripping and emotional as possible, but I've always found it to be just too over the top and really an eye-roller. The other song I have beef with is "Us and Them" which takes almost the same sonic palette that like 4 other songs on this album have used, and peppers in some really mediocre contrasts that I just don't find nearly as awakening as their meant to be. 



That being said, there are great songs here and it's easy to see why this record was so groundbreaking on release. "Time" is among my favorites from the 70's and is really the only time on the album that my mind is actually blown with terrifying lyrics about the sun racing around to come up behind you as every day passes and you age without being able to control it. And "Money" is a classic as well with that iconic riff and some great analogies to the root of all evil. It's tough because there's big highs here but I feel like a 1/4 of the record is just kind of monotonous, which leaves me by the end of "Eclipse" feeling extremely mixed. This is a decidedly good album but Pink Floyd most definitely went on to and had already done bigger and better things, creatively and otherwise.

“There is no dark side of the moon, really. Matter of fact, it’s all dark.” –Gerry O’Driscoll, Abbey Road Studios doorman
- - - - - - - - - -
As I grow older, I find myself thinking more frequently about my existence, my future, and my overall purpose. Who am I? What am I here for? What will I be like 10 years from now? 20? Will I be (more) insane 30 years from now? How will I die? When will I die? Is death something to be afraid of? Is it every man for himself in this world? What path will I choose? What path should I choose? These are just a few of my unanswered questions.

The Dark Side of The Moon  doesn’t do much to answer those questions, but it makes me feel as if my concerns are justified in a way. Thankfully, there’s a lot more to this record than the philosophy. It excels in practically every aspect musically. There are so many noteworthy characteristics of this record to point out, I don’t even know where to begin. The production, the songwriting, and the instrumentation are all highly appreciated elements of this fantastic record, and for very good reason. But, I think my favorite aspect about Dark Side is that it presents a fascinating outlook on human life.
- - - - - - - - - -
March 1st, 2016 marks the 43rd Anniversary of the US release of The Dark Side of The Moon. It’s been nearly half a century since its release, yet Dark Side’s reputation seems to only grow stronger with time rather than diminish. There's actually a simple explanation for this - Pink Floyd towered above their contemporaries. They were undeniably so much more than the average rock band. I think a big flaw of 1970’s rock music – one that I believe should be pointed out much more often – is that a considerable amount of the more commercial releases tend to be  overambitious to the point where the songs and albums are unlistenable because they’re so pompous and pretentious, and, rather than gather significant critical acclaim, are  left behind and dismissed as products of their time.

Dark Side isn’t a product of its time. It’s not even close to being one, and for a couple of reasons. For one, it actually balances ambition with high-quality lyrics, instrumentation, and songwriting. Yes, the band did try to make a grand, monumental statement – which is probably what ruins the record for some people – but Pink Floyd actually had the talent to back up their ambitions. 

But, here’s the biggest reason why tens of millions of copies of Dark Side have been sold. Here’s the biggest reason why Dark Side is still considered by thousands and thousands of people to be the holy grail of rock music, the be-all end-all. Here’s the reason why the 5 star ratings for this record outnumber the 4.5 ratings by a more than 2 to 1 margin. 
It’s because – and as silly as it may sound to the naysayers and even some of the fans – The Dark Side of The Moon is phenomenally relatable. It seems unlikely that anyone – much less the nearly 25,000 people on this website alone that gave this record a rating of 4 or above – could relate to four cynical, pessimistic, philosophical, and arguably pretentious Englishmen. Or, at least, it would be difficult to understand this album if it weren’t for the ingenious lyrics, and the perspective this record offers on our world. Our lives.

Us.

I get chills every time I listen to “Time” and Wright sings the lyrics, “you are young and life is long and there is time to kill today / and then one day you find then years have got behind you / no one told you when to run, you missed the starting gun”. The line, “every year is getting shorter”, is universally relatable.

“All you touch and all you see is all your life will ever be”. A fascinating point of view, from what is possibly my favorite track on this record, “Breathe”. Hell, even the insanity-themed lyrics on “Brain Damage” are partially understandable. Everyone’s at least a little insane.

I could go on and on. To word it simply, Dark Side contains the best lyrics I’ve ever heard on a rock record. Even when ignoring the lyrics, there’s still a lot I could talk about. I could discuss the first time I heard Dark Side of The Moon (about 2 years ago), how it completely changed my perception of music at the time, and how I would do practically anything to erase whatever memories of the album I have just so I could listen to the record for the first time again. I could talk about the wonderfully vivid soundscapes, the jazzy, psychedelic world of Pink Floyd. I could talk about Alan Parsons’s amazing production. I could talk about the interview excerpts scattered throughout the album, an undeservedly under-appreciated element. I could talk about how all of these components help give The Dark Side of the Moon a sense of completeness, probably the most of any rock record. But, I think my favorite thing to talk about when it comes to this record is what it means to me, and my interpretation of this record can be summarized in five words.

Life is pretty damn strange.

9/10

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
I honestly have no idea what to add about this album that hasn't already been said, as the iconic color prism that dons the front cover has loomed over all music discussion for nearly the last 50 years. Oftentimes regarding as the best album ever by mainstream music publications and its greatness constantly touted by music lovers all over, it can be easy to just get sick of hearing about this thing.

But "Dark Side of the Moon" really is a phenomenal album. There is not a single lull in the tracklist. Nothing overstays its welcome. And yet, the album allows itself to be wildly expansive, completely ignoring typical 3-minute long pop song structures. I have a distinct memory when I was 13, just starting to get into music: My parents were sharing their CD collection with me in the family room, and my dad pulled out the immediately recognizable cover of this album, and played "Money." I was instantly grabbed with the groovy bassline rolling along to the odd jump of a 7/4 beat, complete with raucous guitar solos and a saxophone solo. Oddly enough, it took me years to actually listen to the album in full for the first time, as the constant praise and discussion of it made it feel like an insurmountable beast that I just wouldn't "get."

That's the thing, though. For as out there and experimental as "Dark Side of the Moon" is, it is incredibly accessible. There's a reason it has the stereotype of being the album that showed people through multiple generations that music could be more than simple pop songs. It has a tight runtime of 42 minutes, and it packs so many interesting musical ideas in without feeling obtuse or overly simplistic. The songs are all distinct, and yet flow into each other so well. "Breathe (In the Air)" is a chilled-out, spacey opener whose musical themes and motifs get loosely reflected throughout the whole album, notably in the expansive and explosive "Time," and the triumphant "The Great Gig in the Sky," boasting an incredible wordless vocal solo that no doubt is an ascension to musical heaven.

Meanwhile, "Us and Them" in 8 minutes takes you through so many interesting changes it never gets stale, despite its simple construction. Of course, the chorus is absolutely grand. And to top it off, it flows seamlessly into "Any Colour You Like," which takes the previous track into space with a glorious night sky of synthesizer. Even here, you can here the echoes of the base musical motif from "Breathe (In the Air)" floating around. And to the very end of the closing track "Eclipse" it just flows naturally.

I know this album can feel imposing and terrifying to actually listen to given how much cultural baggage is wrapped up in it, but it really is easy to get into, and easy to love.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
The most interesting thing about The Dark Side of the Moon is how obvious it becomes that Floyd used to be a psych-rock band - I mean, listen to it, it's all cyclical rhythms and gliding guitars and pithy half-formed pseudo-philosophical "poetry". Made me feel right in my comfort zone, at least in that regard. But the album itself is not amazing, and that concept (of Dark Side of the Moon sitting up there with Sgt. Pepper and Blonde On Blonde) seems to be more of a general 'willing into existence' than an objective, measurable truth. The songwriting is, you know, fine, but not even at Syd Barrett solo work level most of the time, and the sin of expelling him will haunt this album forever as their first worthy work post-Piper, at least for those who lean more psych than prog. Anyway, anyway, anyway. That's what this is, it's psych-prog, and often unholy in that regard. The production is the real star here, and that was all done by the band themselves, so thank god that at least it's not a Visconti/Bowie/Iggy situation. Undoubtedly the LP is successful, it just doesn't go to the moon and back. More like to Pentangle's Basket of Light and back, or some other such half-psych half-whatever work that were so deeply critically popular in '68-69, when this album probably should have come out. Unbeknownst to most at the time, psych and prog were a back to back deal - I mean, re: prog, has anything beat Crimson King? No? Thought not. Knew not. Anyway, it's too American and much too commercial for a band like Floyd, who came onto the scene with 'Interstellar Overdrive', 'The Gnome' and 'Bike'. I swear I heard David Gilmour say, "don't give me that goody-good bullshit," as if he were some sort of cowboy ... um, excuse me, boys, but what happened to Grimble Crumble?

5 February 2021.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
Side A is pretty much dope, and that includes "On the Run". It has that sci-fi feel, like a dude being chased by robots while trying to escape dystopian city.

I know "Money" is the overplayed one, but it kicks off Side B on the right foot. The solo-section is snazzy. Then I get issues. "Us and Them" is well-loved, but I always found it a bit of a slog that goes on for too long, and that's followed by a jammy instrumental that initially feels like a breathe of fresh air but soon becomes tiresome too. At least "Brain Damage" eventually shows up to get my attention back into full-focus and the finale is a banger.

After decades, my opinion somehow has never changed. It's also neat that Dark Side of the Moon remains one of the key albums to assess the latest in sound equipment. Amazing how different this sounds from their prior output. Almost uncanny.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
When I was a kid, I would listen to the classic rock station of my city whenever I had access. This album would get most of those picks throughout the the time period, and I would never really get sick of any hits from this album. The tracks individually are loosely nostalgic to me, especially "Money", "Great Gig in the Sky" and "Any Colour You Like". 



Only had a chance to listen from beginning to end later in my life in High school when I understood how I could expose myself to a lot of music from the internet and moved out of my mom's house. You can definitely find better rock according to your taste, but Pink Floyd is definitely a rite of passage in a way although they didn't invent much or innovate all too much. It's just an understood solid classic rock album, that's all.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
I've read somewhere an interesting opinion on this album - "the greatest album of all time and not even the best album by Pink Floyd". Every time I listen to The Dark Side of the Moon, I think of that sentence and if I agree or not. Well, you can't deny the album's greatness, it's sublime, grand and the concept is great, the tracks smoothly transition one into another, the instrumentals are transcendental - especially the guitars - and both the singing and instruments quite strongly affect your emotions.

 

What could be more universal for everyone than life itself? Or the fact that we all have a limited time in our lives and that we all have goals and try to achieve them? orY that we work all our lives and at some point we realise that many years have flown by?... The album is timeless, because no matter who you are, you can relate to it - the only exception I can think of would be very young people and children. I think that's why the album was and continues to be so successful and that's why some people consider it to be the greatest album of all time. 



Just like life, the album starts and ends with a heartbeat. It's a perfect way to tell the audience what the album is going to be about. We get a lot of world building done through many different sounds that speak for themselves - breathing, running, clocks ticking and the bells ringing as if they were screaming at you to remind you that time is constantly running and at one point you won't be here anymore - a sound as terrifying as the thought. 



The first half of the album is about how short lives are, the second half is about having a choice to either go with the flow or against it - you could lead a simple and average life or do everything that you can to have the opposite - either way it leads you to insanity, as both ways have their difficulties along them. 



My favourite song here is Money, a song about chasing a rich life, but as I've said before, it leads you to insanity either way. It's written in 7/4 time signature, but it's so smooth that people who wouldn't focus on that detail will probably miss it. The melody flows so effortlessly that it feels like a song in 4/4 and the singing is incredibly catchy. 



Everyone has seen this album cover, many people have listened to it, but to those who haven't - please do, it's an amazing experience and once you get more familiar with the tracks, the whole project is great for contemplating. I would often listen to it and think about my life, where I am right now and whether I am happy with the course of my life or not. I'm sure I'd have it on loop before making a hard decision that would have a major influence on my future.



Is it the GREATEST album of all time? For me, no, not really, there's a lot of amazing music out there, but hell is the album fantastic. I would definitely mention it if I were to make a list of greatest albums of all time.



Is it the best of Pink Floyd's discography? Well, to me it all comes to the question what am I in the mood for - because Wish You Were Here is their best for its sound, Animals is their best for the concept told through three tracks, The Wall is their best for the storytelling and it's a whole play that I have in my mind when I listen to it and Dark Side of the Moon is their best for the universal message and how relatable it is. Each of these four are amazing and deserve praise, so in the end - who am I to tell if it's their best? It definitely is a must-listen classic and one of the best four Pink Floyd albums, but I struggle each time I'm trying to tell which of them is my favourite.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
Fifty long years ago, on September 4, 1973, I started my sophomore year of high school. My freshman year was spent finishing junior high school, and during March of '73, four important rock albums were released, serving as a reminder of what an epic era it was for rock music, as the high flying Led Zeppelin dropped down Houses of the Holy, Alice Cooper minted Billion Dollar Babies, The Who shipped the double LP Quadrophenia and Pink Floyd issued the ambitious The Dark Side of the Moon. That is serious vinyl output for a single month!



Pink Floyd's The Dark Side of the Moon first charted in America shortly after its release, debuting at number 80 on the Billboard chart. The ambitious concept album topped the Billboard album chart for just one week, reaching the number one position on April 28 of '73. By the time I started High School, six months following the release of The Dark Side of the Moon, the album was firmly entrenched in the top ten on a twenty-five-week run. 



While twenty-five weeks is a solid representation for virtually any album, that was just the start for The Dark Side of the Moon, with the LP's constant presence redefined album chart staying power.



Remaining a fixture on the Billboard album chart week-after-week, the album sold consistently throughout my high school and college years, and well into my early resume-building career post college. That speaks volumes for the ten-song album meticulously crafted and produced by guitarist David Gilmour, bassist Roger Waters, keyboardist Richard Wright and drummer Nick Mason. 



PINK FLOYD GOLD!

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
"the dark side of the moon" deals with the subject of madness, as well as the band's own experiences with personal pressures in their lives. through this, they put together their most concise piece of work yet as well as merely creating one of the most iconic, easily recognizable and highly regarded albums of all time.



how does it hold up in 2023? well, it's still a classic.



i've had this one in my rotation for nearly a decade, and i can still remember how big of an impact it had on myself years ago, starting high school.



as a result, some of these tracks have collected a bit of wear due to countless repeated listens. and yet there are many which remain as impressive to me today as they did then - 'money', 'us and them', the entire climactic medley of 'any colour you like' / 'brain damage' / 'eclipse' are a fantastic, all-time side B run.



no disrespect to side A though, 'time' and 'on the run' are exceptional. 'speak to me' is a really impressively produced introduction which leads into the excellent, psychedelic 'breathe' - where the album first spreads its legs a bit. finally 'the great gig in the sky' is another gem, i've heard countless people say they want this played at their funeral. it doesn't touch me to that extent, but there isn't a single track here that stands out as being even slightly weak.



i don't have too much to say about this remaster as i noticed very little change overall - my review and score are based on the original album itself.



'the dark side of the moon' is one of those albums that's recommended to everyone to listen to at least once, and while it might not change every listener's life, this classic is well worth hearing whether it's your first listen or your thirtieth. the whole thing flows so well and feels so mechanically efficient that it makes this collection particularly listenable.



hitch a ride on the next rocket, i'll see ya there!



9.3/10

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
Big themes often demand big music and here Pink Floyd treat with both on this their massively successful album from 1973 with the iconic cover, which still pops up on album charts coming up for fifty years since it was first released.



It's taken me about as long myself to delve into the band's rich vein of albums from the mid-70's on, featuring the line-up of Waters, Gilmour, Wright and Mason but I have to admit that from what I've heard already, it's been worth the wait.



I do admire them for engaging with such heavyweight themes as birth, life and death just for starters, plus references to work, mental illness and of course, filthy lucre. In addition it's almost impossible not to pick up on the continuing influence, indeed almost presence of their original member and principal writer Syd Barrett, a subject which would come to dominate their follow-up album.



Here, after a sort of mini-overture which previews some of what follows, the band settle into the expanse of what is a powerful suite of serious music. Don't expect any ballads or singalong pop tunes, these guys instead are all about their subject matter and heavy-duty music-making.



I can't quite connect with everything here however - some tracks are over-indulgently long, they can't help but drift into what sounds to me like instrumental navel-gazing, I could have done without the enigmatic drop-ins of spoken phrases and there's just too much saxophone for my liking.



But when they get it right, they certainly hit home, like on the vocal performances of  "Breathe", "Time" "Us and Them", "Money", "Brain Damage" and the closing "Eclipse".



Of the largely instrumental pieces, I don't have much use for "Any Colour You Like" and while it's an aural extravaganza, one might say the same of "On The Run". Richard Wright's major track "The Great Gig In The Sky", now correctly co-credited with the lady vocalist who overlaid her haunting wordless singing culminates the journey-of-life passage on the album.



It goes without saying that the production is pristine and the group's playing, lyricism and sense of musical dynamics have clearly plateaued on this record. 



It may not be an altogether easy listen either musically or lyrically but ultimately I found it a rich, rewarding album to get to know. 



Getting there, I can now much more easily appreciate the massive global success and high critical acclaim it enjoys, even today.

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
The original vinyl pressing of Pink Floyd's The Dark Side of the Moon was released in America on March 1, 1973, with the U.K. distribution following three weeks later. The ambitious concept album topped the Billboard album chart for one week, going number one on April 28th of '73.

Despite the fact that The Dark Side of the Moon was limited to one lonely week at the peak of the Billboard LP chart, the album's legacy is confirmed by the incredible staying-power that the inspired recording commanded.

The Dark Side of the Moon was issued as the final quarter of my Freshman year of high school was underway. In a monumental year of album releases, that included Led Zeppelin's Houses of the Holy, Alice Cooper's Billion Dollar Babies and the double-LP Quadrophenia by The Who, there was no shortage of era-defining rock recordings for long hair teens cruisin' 'n' boozin' action. 

Once The Dark Side of the Moon hit the Billboard chart, the album went on to spend a mind-numbing 741 consecutive weeks in the top 200. Throughout my high school and college daze, and into my early years of career resume-building, The Dark Side of the Moon was a constant on the Billboard chart. 

Remastered in 1992, the compact disc release of The Dark Side of the Moon is no competition for the original LP, which featured a pair of foldout posters that were a dorm room staple during the high times '70s. The iconic cover art remains the same, but there is no substitute for the original '73 release of The Dark Side of the Moon in the Hipgnosis designed gatefold sleeve.

I dont think you can argue against this albums brilliance , against its state of the art production ,its superb playing ,its deep philosophical musings . So i wont bother.

Our music teacher at primary school played this to my class and it was the first time i had heard it . It has hardly been an ever present in my musical life though but yet still i retain a respect for it. It catapulted floyd to a whole new level of success and acclaim .

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review:
Every now and then a thought crosses my mind: that the so-called Rock Canon is just one big put-on, a plot against my own comprehension of what good (no, not merely good - excellent) music should be and what should it stand for.

And then a record such as The Dark Side of the Moon comes along and completely reassures me in my believes. Because, as someone already pointed out, this is easy listening, an immaculately produced record by a bunch of talented but uninspired individuals, a music that takes no real chances, that does not challenge the listener, offering him instead nicely recorded glimpses of beauty (and boredom) throughout the whole thing. I kind of shiver at the thought that most people would perceive this album as one the pinnacles of Rock, without ever having a chance to hear, say, The Marble Index, The Modern Dance or even Pink Floyd's own The Piper at the Gates of Dawn (although, admittedly, they were completely different band with Syd Barrett on board).

The Dark Side of the Moon is one of all-time biggest bestsellers, but its musical merits are, to say at least, questionable. It was not the music people were paying for, but the masterful production and arrangements. This is an album for lazy "intellectuals" and rich snobs who prefer their music to be lulling and "backgroundish" rather than fresh and inventive. I know I'm purposefully generalizing here, but I cold never relate to either. So, please, stop this nightmare.     6,5/10

album: The Dark Side of the Moon
review: While The Dark Side of the Moon is a masterfully produced album (thanks in part to the sound engineering of Alan Parsons), it's never really made much of an impression on me. The individual songs are more like sketches, with only "Time" and "Us and Them" featuring any real development. "Money," the album's ostensible hit, is just corny, its jazz-fusion style (and supposedly noteworthy 7/4 time signature) completely undercutting its anti-capitalistic lyric. Because the album insists on stitching each track together, they end up being breezier than they should be, completely neutering the "dark" musical elements on the record (which only manifest themselves in the throwaway segue "On the Run" and the album's best cut, "Time"). Furthermore, the attempts to parody gospel and soul music in "Any Colour You Like," "Brain Damage," and "Eclipse" are stilted and goofy, compromising the album's "spacey" qualities. While it is impressive that the one-time sodden act, mired in the post-1960s psychedelic rock ghetto, managed to synthesize their approaches (and musical breadth) into an album as accessible (and commercially successful) as this one, it lacks the focus of its follow-up, Wish You Were Here, or the sonic adventurousness of earlier efforts The Piper at the Gates of Dawn or Meddle.

review:
Well, Nick Mason thinks this is better than Dark Side of the Moon. Of course, Martin Barre's favorite Jethro Tull album is Under Wraps, which just goes to show that band members don't understand their own music. This is certainly a good album, and it features David Gilmour's last lyrical contribution to the band until AMLOR (another horrible album by a great band, while we're on the subject). This was Childhood's End, one of the best tracks on the album. 

On to the tracks: Obscured by Clouds is a nice instrumental, but nothing special. When You're In is even better, and is one of the highlights. My only complaint is that it starts to fade out with about 50 seconds left in the song. Burning Bridges is an interesting song that's hard to interpret, but it could be about divorce (or Hiroshima/Nagasaki, take your pick). Either way, it's a nice song, worth having. 

The Gold It's In The... is a rocker (along the lines of The Nile Song and Ibiza Bar off More), but not so extreme. It's good, but nothing to write home about. Wots... Uh the Deal, aside from having a great title, sounds most un-Floydish. Yet it works, so long as you can stand to listen to a song about half as fast as the opener to Shine On You Crazy Diamond, except that this song remains that way the whole way through. 

Mudmen is a reprise of Burning Bridges, and not nearly as interesting. Childhood's End is one of the few tracks off this album that I still listen to. It's well, done, interesting, and one of Gilmour's best lyrical contributions (because he wasn't a great lyricist). Free Four is my favorite (but Childhood's End is close), because it's Waters's first death song. Think Maxwell's Silver Hammer, by the Beatles. This song has the same feel, except this one is worse musically and better lyrically ("life is a short warm moment/and death is a long cold rest/you'll get your chance to try/in the twinkling of an eye/80 years with luck or even less") 

Unfortunately, Stay and Absolutely Curtains are weak tracks, with littel to recommend them. And with that, the album ends. 3.5 stars musically/lyrically, but I can only recommend it to fans of Pink Floyd.

Just like More this album is criminally underrated. You just have to compare how many ratings/reviews there are of the surrounding albums to see that not many care about Obscured by Clouds. This is even better than More with all the songs standing alone without the movie (except 'Absolutely Curtains' I suppose). I saw the film (La Vallée I think it was called) quite a long time ago and I can't remember how the music fitted with it but anyway...



The title track features squealing guitars over an incessantly driving beat with crisp drums leading straight into the faster but stylitically similar 'When You're In'. 'Burning Bridges' is the first change-up and is a little dreary. 'Mudmen' is another great instrumental with a fantastic squealing guitar solo, 'Childhood's End' would be the best song with lyrics and 'Free Four' is a very funny country song - contrast the disturbing lyrics with the upbeat rhythm and melody. 'The Gold it's in the...' is a complete garbage rocker, while I have never understand why so many hardcore Floyd fans normally only like 'Wots... uh the Deal'. The album finishes a bit meekly with the dreary 'Stay' and the puzzling 'Absolutely Curtains'. Presumably important to the film, the early part of the track is all about atmosphere while the end features the native chanting from the film. It is simply way too long and unnecessary.

While everyone stood around asking "How the hell did Pink Floyd make the jump from the Meddle to Dark Side?", this genial little recording was waiting on the sidelines to take a stab at answering, if only someone would listen. That someone is me, I have listened and I can inform you that Obscured by Clouds will get you about a third of the way from the one album to the other. It's definitely nearer in spirit to Meddle, but it's qualitatively different in some important ways, probably from being a soundtrack. There's no progressive rock, no real epic rock, no wigging out, and no layers of sound effects or other audio conceits. The fab four didn't have any time to apply their usual polish and varnish (they only had a couple of weeks to record) and that makes for a refreshing break from the usual Pink Floyd meticulousness.

In fact, this is their most grounded and normal release by the standards of when it came out. It never cultivates any anger (there is some rock, though), because Gilmour isn't ripping the shit out of his guitar and Waters isn't scratching out page after page of dead dad librettos. Everything is kept sane and accessible, and a few song parts may well remind you of other bands active around the same time. Here's mine: "Wot's...Uh the Deal" sounds as if Led Zeppelin extracted their idea for "The Rain Song" from it. Of course, Pink Floyd had three distinctive vocalists who could work together to make some Beatles-quality vocal harmonies, and Led Zeppelin only had...Plant. I think the architecture students win that round.

My attention is wandering, so instead of describing all the tracks I'll simply list other bits and bobs you'll hear: the same synthesiser that was on "Save the Life of My Child", acoustic guitar, piano on rockers and ballads, instrumentals, Wright organ, slow and trippy Gilmour mannerisms on electric guitar, and gentle singing. That oughta get you started.

review:
Even if this album is primarily a soundtrack, there’s no other Pink Floyd record in the classis era which is more song based. There are some instrumentals, we can say fillers that bring in some soundtrack feel: ‘Obscured By Clouds’ definitely, ‘When You’re In’ is more like an unfinished solid rocker, a very good one, ‘Mudmen’ is my favourite, a real Gilmour shine, the closing track reminds me to More for a while but the tribal chanting makes special.



And the real songs that are also good: ‘Burning Bridges’ is a Pink Floyd essence in 3 minutes, Gilmour’s psychedelic vocals with soft slide guitars. ‘The Gold It’s In The…’ is a very groovy track, just like ‘Wot’s… Uh The Deal’, a bit different than other Floyd songs, but both remarkable. ‘Childhood’s End’ is one of the best songs of Pink Floyd ever, great build up, but no progressive pathos, simply strong songcraft. ‘Free Four’ is a typical Waters song, a strong one, but not my favourite here, a bit odd. ‘Stay’ is another mellow Gilmour track.



I always liked, but fell in love with Obscured By Clouds during a long trip last year to Crete visiting hidden places settled by hippies back in teh '70s. I am really curious about the movie ‘La Vallée’, I hope I will see once.

review: Pink Floyd were asked to do another soundtrack in 1972 and it was for another French movie. The movie, La Vallee, is the movie and this is the soundtrack to the album. This album is a real surprise for me. The album sounds both psychedelic and progressive. "Burning Bridges" is wonderful and there's a surprising rocking song on here ("The Gold It's in the..."). "Wots...Uh the Deal" is another fantastic song as is the very psychedelic "Mudman". The whole album is very good but the album doesn't seem like the kind of album that one would remember listening to. So, I have to give it 3.5 stars. Still, a very good Floyd album!

I think it'll make great background music though.

Coming off from a relative high and progression forward with Meddle, Obscured by Clouds is something of an oddity in the Floyd cannon in that it serves as a brief intermission between two vital albums. Instead, this one offers some decent songs and developments that lead into the monster that follows it, but on its own, it's not the greatest. With that being said, as far as an album goes, Obscured can be seen as the band's most cohesive work since A Saucerful of Secrets--indeed, perhaps the greatest sign that Dark Side was coming is in this album's sense of flow and intrinsic sense of wholeness. Obscured by Clouds is larger than the sum of its parts, not the greatest songs the band had composed, but a fine album nevertheless.

One of the Floyd's more underrated records because it's a soundtrack and not a proper studio album. Not amazing but definitely worth having. The band recorded this really quickly and it shows -- it's a tight, muscular, straightforward rock n' roll record that has a little bit of the spacey, proggy textures ("Obscured by Clouds", "Mudmen") but not much. "Free Four" and "Childhood's End" are probably the best two pieces here, although "The Gold It's in The..." gets the adrenaline flowing thanks to a fantastic Gilmour solo and "Wot's... Uh the Deal" is a Beatles-esque laid-back ballad. Weird to think they went to Dark Side of the Moon right after this.

Non ho mai capito che cosa ci fosse di così tanto orribile in "Obscured by Clouds"...sì, d'accordo: è ovviamente un disco minore, un po' manieristico, troppo diligente, un disco che in un paio di punti strizza persino l'occhio a certe sonorità di facile consumo (pop e hard-rock)...ma, oggettivamente, non riesco a trovarlo abbastanza brutto o noioso per potergli rifilare in buona fede un votaccio e augurargli buonanotte. A mixed bag...some pleasant tracks, a "meh" easy listening track and a little rock. 72.5/100


   Not that keen on the first two tracks of this album and the last one. It's tracks like these which lower my score. On first listen I scored the album 65?/100, but was feeling slightly more generous on second listen. I've got the 1995 edition of this album (I hear that there will be remastered versions of all Pink Floyd albums later this year...2011). Album runs to 40:31 minutes long.


The best tracks:


Burning bridges - ">(.)". Got more out of this track on second listen...no songs really stuck out first time around. 3:30 minutes long. A pleasant, sort of Beatle-esque kind of track (perhaps with regard to the vocals)...also in keeping with the sound of Pink Floyd's debut album. Features drums, cymbals, organ and electric lead guitar...the latter having a country twang to it, at times, or sort of Hawaiian sounding.

Mudmen - ">[.]". 4:18. An instrumental. With a pleasant piano, organ, bass and beat (interesting beats). Maybe there's a xylophone type instrument in the mix too. Sharp, bluesy electric lead guitar notes...and synth too, perhaps. Organ plays a sort of familiar melody. 

Childhood's end - ">>[.]". 4:33. Gave this track a third listen, for some reason, and got more out of it that time. Has a quiet, synth start (a touch of U2's "Where the streets have no name" to it). Melissa Etheridge was also brought to mind, perhaps with regard to the lyric "We are stardust" (my notes aren't the best here...maybe I'm saying that the song with that lyric, whoever it is by, was brought to mind by this Pink Floyd song). Track has a synth beat...bringing to mind John Cougar's "Crumblin' down". Track also features organ and steel string guitar. Lead guitar is bluesy. 


The rest (in track order):


Obscured by clouds - 3:05. Instrumental. Synthy, with an electro beat...I'm assuming that a synth is being used instead of drums. Synth notes are fuzzy too. A slow-medium tempo song. Electric lead guitar used too.

When you're in - '2:31' (perhaps more like 2:22, as my CD player didn't give full value to the time it displayed on the LCD display). Instrumental. Rock track. Sort of familiar melodies...bringing to mind songs by King Crimson or Metallica. Drumming is solid. Organ brings to mind Deep Purple. Electric lead guitar and synth also feature.

The gold, it's in the... - '3:08'/3:01. Mid-tempo, riffing rock song with lead guitar. Points of reference...sort of Beatle-esque? Hard go-go type music? A touch of their "More" album to this too? 

Wot's...uh the deal - 5:09. Pleasant, steel string (semi-acoustic?) guitar. A slow-medium tempo song, which sounds like it could have come off The Beatles' 'White' album. Features guitar notes, harmonies and the piano...the latter playing a lick which reminds me of Billy Joel's "Scenes from an Italian restaurant". 

Free four - '4:16'/4:11. Bit of a laddish intro, heavy synth chords, a la glam rock, acoustic steel string guitar, some boogie riffing at times and electric lead guitar solos. Lyrics mention death and tourning America...has a sort of a whimsical tone to it. 

Stay - 4:07. Easy listening song with piano, bass, cymbals and a country style electric lead guitar sound. This is one of the songs which drag my score for this album down...i.e. my score would improve if this song wasn't on it. The lyrics are a bit meh and naff...the vocal melody is a bit naff too. I wonder if the vocal melody is similar to some of "Mudmen" too...maybe not, though the melody in that other song still strikes me as being familiar, somehow. 

Absolutely curtains - 5:51. Synthy track...that part could have made for a good Pink Floyd style instrumental, but the outro veers into different territory...it sounds like the voices of an Australian Aboroginal male elder and children are used...the children sing their native language, presumably. That outro is incongruous for the album and maybe the album would have been better minus that incongruity. Track does feature keyboard, symphonic drums and some kind of steel string instrument. 


Pink Floyd recommendations - 


The Wall - 90+/100. Comes across as a movie soundtrack featuring excerpts from the movie...which it isn't...some call this a "concept" album. A great rock album in any case.

The dark side of the moon - 87.5/100. Works well as a mellow album, but I can't say I really want to hear these songs on the radio...apart from "Us and them". 

Meddle - 82.5/100. A nice, mellow album with a sprinkling of American styles on it.

Wish you were here - 82.5/100. 

"More" soundtrack - 4/5. Mellow, but also with their two heaviest rock songs on it.

The piper at the gates of dawn. 75+/100. Strangely alluring debut album. Repeat listen value.

review: This is an extremely underrated (almost forgotten) album by Pink Floyd. It has some really nice songs on it, great musically and lyrically. This album was a soundtrack album to a hippies in New Guinea themed french film called "The Valley" and was recorded between "Meddle" and "Dark side of the moon". I have personally never seen the movie, but I've enjoyed listening to  "Obscured by clouds" many times. My favorite tracks are: "Burning Bridges", "Wots...uh the deal", "Mudmen", "Childhood's end", and "Stay". Those songs are so melodic and powerful, they really mean a lot to me. David Gilmour's guitar playing and tone sound awesome and Richard Wright's vocals on "Stay" are also very good. I've always liked Richard Wright's vocals, he should of sang more often. He was just as good as Gilmour, I think. More people should go back and give this album a chance, because it's a great early Pink Floyd experience.

A soundtrack album released between Meddle and Dark Side of the Moon, Obscured by Clouds is overlooked by many.  Don't be one of them!  It is Pink Floyd without the polishing that comes on the later albums.  It's a group just playing with a minimum of effects.  Richard Wright and his keyboards get plenty of space on this album.  Don't worry, there is plenty of David Gilmour's guitar too.  These two get more writing credits than any other PF album before Roger Waters left the band.  Don't worry Waters still has the lion's share of writing credits.   This album like their other soundtrack More, are notable for having only relatively short tracks on it.  



This closes the era of Pink Floyd before their music world domination begins but don't miss this overlooked gem!

Probably their most song based and most accessible album at the time of release, ironic since it is their least popular album released in the '70s. Originally the lack of extended compositions on this made it hard for me to get into this album, but once you get used to the songs format you start realizing how good these songs really are. Childhood's End in particular is probably the best "song" they had written up to that point, and evokes later anthems such as Time and Wish You Were Here. The instrumentation is quite similar to Dark Side of the Moon, and comes as more modest cousin.

Also the first 30 seconds of the title track could be an MGMT song.

review:
Obscured by Clouds is not a very appreciated album. This is actually quite bizarre, because the album is a kind of cross between The Piper at the Gates of Dawn and The Dark Side of the Moon. It lacks the special, somehow 'esoteric' quality of the latter, but it more than equals the former, which has been my favourite of the pre-Dark Side Floyd albums. The two opening instrumentals create some of the magic feel of Dark Side and the vocal number that follows, "Burning Bridges", is even quite excellent. Another one that I like very much is "Wot's... Uh the Deal." Instead, "Free Four" is not that much my kind of song (surrealistic country?!), but still, to me it feels a little reminiscent of Piper.



Pink Floyd is known as a progressive rock band. Obscured by Clouds is not Floyd at its most progressive, so for the prog fans, that may easily mean 'not at its best.' On the other hand, fans of psychedelia may like Syd Barrett a lot, but this is not Barrett, not the 1960s, and not even very psychedelic. So, extra-musical factors seal the underappreciation of the album. I am not considering Obscured by Clouds a real masterpiece, but while I am not very much a 'prog' person, I like this one well over Ummagumma, Atom Heart Mother, Meddle, Animals and even Wish You Were Here.

When I was much younger, my favorite Floyd albums were DSOTM, Wish and Animals (in that order). I really didn't pay much attention to the older things. But as I have aged, I find myself disregarding anything that came after this album. Or to put it another way, this is the last of the "Great Ones".

Now to frame this appropriately, my current favorite PF albums are Ummagumma (especially the live disc), Atom Heart, Meddle, More and this one. It is very organic, with a psychedelic spontaneity that would later disappear. I find this to be an extremely likable album and I truly wish that the band had toured it more. Bootlegs of this material are very slim pickin's.

This probably won't rock your world if your favorite PF is The Wall (sorry... I positively hate that album), or the Barrett years. However, if you're into some of the spacier stuff, then this one is right in your wheelhouse.
All of the overindulgent soft-psych from their following 3 albums with none of the highlights

As many other reviewers will say, this happens to be one of Pink Floyd's most tragically overlooked albums, and with good reason. Despite the fact that it's one of their soundtrack albums- and therefore objectively shitty- it still remains listenable and enjoyable. Sure, it's not nearly as grandiose as Dark Side of The Moon or Wish You Were Here, but listen close enough and you can see how the album contains elements from both, and foreshadows them as well. Mudmen and Burning Bridges brings to mind the slow beats and droning guitar/keyboard that ends up featured on DSOTM. The "good ol' " rock n' roll in The Gold It's in the... is nice, but what really makes itself known is Childhood's End. Its existential lyrics and perfect blend of 'spacey' keyboard and guitar make it seem like the framework for Time off of DSOTM, and Wot's... Uh The Deal is rather similar to Wish You Were Here, if you squint hard enough.

Maybe not the same quality as some of their later works, but you can still enjoy the hell out of Obscured by Clouds. I, for one, think that Mudmen and Childhood's End (and even Wot's... Uh The Deal) are classic Floyd tunes that represent them rather well.

This is an album that I feel is often overlooked by a lot of people, mainly due to being released between two critically-acclaimed albums, Meddle and Dark Side of the Moon. While Obscured by Clouds may not be as iconic as either of these albums, there is still a lot of fun to be had with this album. 

Obscured by Clouds, as others who have reviewed the album have said, as a much different vibe compared to other Pink Floyd albums from the era it was made. The songs on this album have a much more laid-back feeling to them with some rocking tunes added to the mix. 

There are tunes such as "Wots...Uh the Deal," which is very mellow and almost feels like a track could have appeared on Meddle. Tracks such as "The Gold It's in the..." and "When You're In" show off Pink Floyd's more rock-focused side. I especially love "The Gold It's in the..." it's such a fun and energetic song with great rhythm. However, my personal favorite off of Obscured by Clouds would have to be "Childhood's End," which is apparently loosely based off a science-fiction novel of the same name. It's difficult to not get sucked into this song with the eerie, yet nostalgic sounding synthesizers luring the listener in until the song breaks into a rock song with introspective lyrics. People often say that this particular track feels like a prototype of "Time" from Dark Side of the Moon and I can definitely understand the comparison. 

 I feel that Obscured by Clouds is an album that takes some time to fully appreciate. Pink Floyd has been one of my favorite bands for years, yet I always overlooked this album. It wasn't until recently that I gave it various listens before I got hooked. While it may not be Pink Floyd's best albums, it is an album that I think most fans of the band would enjoy.

Pink Floyd's second soundtrack album is miles better than the first. It stands alone a lot better and has one absolutely incredible song (The Gold It's in the...) The atmosphere is dreamy and laid-back. I don't think it's entirely fair to review soundtrack albums without seeing the context behind it, but luckily this doesn't really need it to be "good".

"Dreamy, very pretty, and nearly lifeless, Obscured by Clouds was Pink Floyd's warm-up for The Dark Side of the Moon. The one track with an actual tempo ("The Gold It's in the ....") is an intriguing glimpse of what the band could have been if it wanted to be"-original review (08/12/2011).

Upon revisit this is better and way less boring than I remember. "The Gold It's in the..." still kills (I can't think of another post-Syd Barrett Floyd track with such energy). It captures the band dipping their toes in the water, but not yet becoming the musical equivalent of Stanley Kubrick (I love the director but don't intend that as a compliment). If you don't have the sound up too loud you might even mistake "Stay" for Steely Dan. And "Free Four" is actually-gasp!-fun.

Obscured by Cloud is the seventh studio album by Pink Floyd. It serves as the soundtrack for the film La Vallee. During this time the band was in the process of recording their magnus opus « Dark Side of the Moon ». So they choose to be more free wheeling with this project. And it shows, the songs are shorter (for Pink Floyd) and not necessarily tied to a recurring theme. There are 4 instrumental tunes, but its the vocal ones that show a different side of Pink Floyd. Songs like « The Gold it’s in the… » is a straight up rock tune, and a very fun one at that. A song that if you had never told me it was Pink Floyd, I would have never guess it was them. « Wot’s …. Uh the Deal » is the other Gilmour/Walters composition from the album and what a sweet tune. After Syd Barret left the band, some of the early Pink Floyd uncomplicated fun went with him.  So it was nice seeing these guys could still make fun music when they weren’t trying too hard.

Now I’m not saying this is a top tier album within the vast Pink Floyd discography. But it is a must listen, if anything to see what these guys were able to produce when thery weren’t trying to be deep and instead just decided to let loose.

 

Footnote : the drum pattern from « Childhood’s End » was recycled for « Time ». I was expecting Gilmour to start singing « Ticking away the moments that make up a dull day » when he started singing on that song.

This is easily Pink Floyd's most overlooked album, seeing as how it's sandwiched between Meddle and Dark Side. Much like their third studio album More, Obscured By Clouds was written as a soundtrack to a film by Barbet Schroeder, and consists of a series of lighter, more acoustic tracks. However, Obscured By Clouds is entirely unlike More in that it doesn’t bore me to tears every time I listen to it.

Obscured By Clouds employs a combination of tracks with electric guitars (e.g. the first two tracks, "The Gold It's In The…") and tracks with acoustic guitars (e.g. "Burning Bridges," "Wot’s… Uh The Deal"). The songs all sound similar in terms of atmosphere, but are varied enough from an instrumental perspective to keep the listener interested.

That being said, there's not much in the way of standout tracks. All of these tracks are good, but they're not lyrically or musically developed in the same way that the songs on Meddle or the band's later albums are, nor are they particularly experimental. Obscured By Clouds is just kind of… there. Still, it is very well-performed and well-produced, which is impressive considering it was written and recorded in less than a month.

If you have 40 minutes, and it's a nice day out, take this record outside, lay back, and hit the play button. It's a very relaxing album that doesn’t get the respect it deserves.

Could this be the most fun Pink Floyd album? It may very well be. It lacks the cohesive statement their best albums have but so many of these songs (especially “Free Four” and the wildly overlooked “The Gold It's in the...”) are way more fun than you might expect them to be.

More like obscured by better albums, amirite?
Despite coming right before their big breakthrough, OBC is probably Pink Floyd’s least known album, with even keen fans of the band liable to forget it exists. In this it’s similar to More and it’s probably not a coincidence that like More, OBC is the soundtrack to a Barbet Schroeder film, this time La Vallee. Floyd are a band who love to conceive and develop concepts so maybe the same inspiration isn’t there when they’re delivering someone else’s vision – in any case there’s not much to say about this album. It isn’t bad, in fact it’s decent but it never climbs high enough to be anything like an essential listen.
Tracks worth saving are The Gold It’s In The, a strange yet catchy glimpse of Floyd impersonating a conventional 70’s radio rock band, and Childhood’s End is also pretty great, moving from a dreamy ambient opening to a slow yet groovy rock number with a great vocal from Gilmour. Free Four is interesting, being the last confirmed sighting of Roger Waters’ sense of humour, a sarcastically bouncy number that is almost Eagle Rock, as Waters cheerfully sings a blackly comic lyric about death and the futility of life. One wonders if Eric Idle was listening.
The rest of the material is fine – nothing is awful, it just lacks the intensity and cohesion many Floyd fans will expect. One cool thing about OBC is it’s the last time we’ll see the band just hanging out and playing some songs for fifteen years as every album after this until then is Big And Serious. Skippable but a pleasant enough listen, OBC may appeal to those looking for the lighter side of Floyd.

This is a weird one. Caught between two gamechanging Pink Floyd albums this is an anomaly but while you can't connect it to the main thread of Pink Floyd developing their main style, it's still fascinating.

It's often said that this record was PF trying to make the closest thing to mainstream rock they'd make, and that's true but there is still a lot of creativity here with their use of kosmische electronic elements, that heavy blues influence, and psychedelic aspects.

After listening to these songs a dozen times, I think this album is pretty good and something special.

Y'know, some people may say that this is an odd precursor to Dark Side of the Moon. But in all honesty, it fits perfectly in this stage of Pink Floyd's existence. People just don't like it because it sums up all the flaws of DSotM. It's a lenient, near-ambient pop record with nothing to do with the Space Rock epic that is the latter, however, it shows the same lazy concepts and songwriting that I come to expect from Atom Heart Mother-Dark Side. Maybe I'm being too complain-y. Both are very fine records, Dark Side obviously more than just fine, they just...reveal each other's vulnerabilities so well. 

Absolutely Curtains is a blast though.

Before listening to this album, I think one has to understand that it is very easy to gloss over this record and not think twice. I mean, to be between one of the greatest prog-rock songs of all time, "Echoes," and possibly the greatest album of all time, "The Dark Side of the Moon," the standards are immense for this album, yet after spinning it, it isn't awful in any way. I believe it isn't Pink Floyd's greatest work and that is completely understandable. 



The album itself is a solid four out of five stars for me. Overall, the album sounds great for a movie soundtrack when compared to other soundtracks that Pink Floyd has done. You still have a sense of, "Oh, this is for a movie," with the ending of Absolutely Curtains and the opening song Obscured By Clouds (which I think would go great with any movie title screen). But other than that, the album's prog-rock nature still is prolific in most of its songs, with maybe the exception of The Gold It's in the... which sounded like a classic Floyd song with an infusion of the Beatles. Wots... Uh the Deal is one of my favorite Pink Floyd songs ever and Mudmen + Childhood's End are fantastic songs that shouldn't be overlooked.



Main point: this song is easy to overlook, and isn't the band's best work, but still is extremely great and important to the genre of progressive rock itself. 100% deserves a listen. Plus, who doesn't like an album cover by Hipgnosis :)

I liked the other ones as well, but a lot of the other songs were a bit too slow for me, and the lack of lyrics kind of led me to feel like something was missing. Lyrics are really important to me because I want to relate to what the artist is feeling, and I think "Mudmen" conveys that even without any words and Pink Floyd is generally able to communicate so much through the instrumentals alone, but lyrics are still my favourite part of a track generally speaking. I think that's why I genuinely cannot get into hype and mainstream pop music just because the words often mean nothing and don't relate to anything sentimental or meaningful !! Just my opinion. 



Anyway really thought this album was super interesting and it was super enjoyable to listen to. Points deducted just cuz of my personal tastes, but I don't think this album should be overlooked and it definitely deserves a listen and some more recognition.

Ahhh good ole’ _Meddle_ , the calm before the super stardom, the last Floyd hippie record, call it what ya will but in my eyes it’s a classic. One of the most fluid multi genre inspired albums ever managing to experiment yet remain Pink Floyd overall.



“One Of These Days” is the Kraut Rockiest song Floyd ever did, one could almost say it foreshadows Industrial. Truly a tune ahead of it’s time. “Pillow of Winds” is a fairly pleasant hippie song sounding a bit like a _More_ outtake and, though not a standout track, a lovely addition to this set. It is “Fearless” that makes you shuffle in your seat and pay attention, typed words cannot express my love for this song. Let’s just say this album gets the rating it does mainly because this song is on the album. 



“San Tropez” shows a lighter side of the band and has a very pleasant loungey groove to it. You almost feel as though you should be sitting in the backyard drinking something made in a blender and served in a curvaceous glass (sunglasses and flowery shirt are optional). Those who know Pink Floyd know they’ve always been obsessed with Southern Blues and that they’ve done their fair share of drugs, so a short Delta Blues inspired song based around a dog that howls at Blues music should be no surprise to anyone. “Seamus” is not a great tune but it’s short enough to be harmless.  



“Echoes”, how the hell do you explain Echoes. It’s such a  dark, psychedelic masterpiece that you don’t even realize twenty three and a half minutes have passed when it’s over. One of my top five favorite Floyd tunes easily. 



This album may sound very early 70’s and may be a bit Hodge Podgeish , but when the mood strikes it is one fantastic listen. I still can’t figure out if that’s a nose or an ear on the cover. Guess I’ll have to wait for the day I run into Roger Waters.

review:
_Meddle_ was the first pink Floyd album I listened to in totality for myself after being subjected to constant plays of _Dark Side Of The Moon_ over and over at strange and dirty apartments and flats that had a party raging on into the very small hours. Of course this  would only come on when everybody was completely zoned out and would annoy the fuck out of me no end. Watching the sun come up while my eyes are several shades of purple and my mouth feels like an ashtray is no comfort when "Money" is thrown in the equation and my wallet seems to be lost and my liver has abandoned me due to neglect. Now, I have never been to keen on _DSOTM_ (you don't say?) and it's commercial leanings, mainly because over time I have noticed that the majority  people who own _DSOTM_ have little else by Pink Floyd except for perhaps _The Wall_ or _The New York Philharmonic does DSOTM_ or something to that effect in the most extreme of cases. You know the people who might have _Harvest_, _Hysteria_, _Thriller_, _Celine Dion's Greatest Hits_ and what ever mullet man Michael Bolton did in their collections. Basically the safe multi platinum options. I spoke to a guy about _Meddle_ one time and claiming to a HUGE Pink Floyd album had never ever heard _Meddle_! And gave me an odd look. I dared not mention Syd Barrett's name.



I do enjoy the _Meddle_ album even though it's inconsistent and the sound mix is actually very low, which is a pity as this lessens the sonic blast of "One Of These Days" which should have been ripping. Floyd have never attempted to recreate a song with the ambition and creative yet stoned quality of a song like "One Of These Days", but remember, this is coming from a _Dark Side.._ and _ The Wall_ hater so please forgive me. "Pillow Of Winds" is decidedly dreamy with a very flowing rhythm and though "Seamus" sounds barking mad , it's worth it's place but the highlight and obvious center piece of the whole affair has to be the schizoid mood of "Echoes", which starts out melancholic and emotive which for a second threatens to be sweet but builds big walls (ahem) of brooding darkness and freakiness that only druggies could come up with but is a song of epic proportions. I have a friend who works as a mortician and he tells me that there is nothing he likes better than to have a spliff, go in to work play "Echoes" and fuck around with a few corpses for the day. Needless to say, I don't envy him.

Two things.  First off, whenever I refer to Pink Floyd in this article I'm referring to Pink Floyd post 1967, comparing this album to Piper at the Gates of Dawn is a very different and less relevant exercise than comparing it to Dark Side of The Moon.

Secondly, this album has two pretty cool Easter eggs, both of which I've experienced personally.



First if you have a record or a tape and play it backwards(or if you know how to do it digitally) the satanic voice on One of These Days is actually telling you to send money to Pink Floyd at such an such an address (think about the time they must have wasted working that one out!) 

Second, Echoes is an alternative sound track to the Jupiter segment of 2001 a Space Odyssey, the space bird segment actually makes some sense in this context, and unlike Dark Side of the Oz it actually works when your not stoned, with all of Echoes’ and the Jupiter segment’s numerous changes lining up perfectly.

 



This kind of playfulness is exactly what makes Meddle stand out among Pink Floyd's Album, and prog rock in general, it doesn't take itself very seriously even when it has every right too.  



Meddle finds Pink Floyd finally giving up on creating abstract modern music and crafting their first album in which every track is firmly grounded in tonality.  It documents the bands discovery that in their failed attempts to make long passages of crazy noise make sense(several other bands suceeded at this end at this point) they had acquired a structural discipline that made them really really good at composing normalish tonal music.  It also found them at a time when Roger Water was still rather unsure of himself, confused and weary of experimental rock and all those bad attempts at atonality.  As a result we get a unique Pink Floyd album, not a bunch of wildly uneven puesdo- avant-garde efforts to keep pace with Barrett’s genius, or a reluctant Gilmore and Wright doing a really good job of backing up Waters on a pretentious but musically accomplished social realist crusade.  Instead, we find Roger Water's subtly and wittily sabotaging Gilmore and Wright's gorgeously melodic prog rock.  We get the majestic compositional sweep of Echoes, and really all the prog tracks, contrasted against intentionally overblown but gorgeous sounding prog-pysch lyrics, goofy easter eggs, and wild stylistic shifts towards a planet way outside of the Pink Floyd universe, Earth. 

A gently post-modern masterpiece, possibly Pink Floyd's best nonBarrett album, certainly their smartest.

review:
Meddle is my favorite Pink Floyd album in much the same way that Miller's Crossing is my favorite gangster movie.  Sure, The Godfather and Scarface are more epic and quotable, but I find them to be a bit drawn out and dull at times.  Miller's Crossing is just perfectly cast and the Coen brothers tell their story perfectly.

Pink Floyd don't have any overarching theme here like they do on later albums.  Everything just seems understated and executed flawlessly.  There's a breezy nature here that I haven't found on any of their releases (although I haven't heard all of them).  OK, "Seamus" is kind of lightweight and "Echoes", awesome as it is, doesn't really have to be 23 minutes long.  Still, "One of These Days", "Fearless", and "San Tropez" are some of the best songs Pink Floyd ever recorded.

(Then I go back and wonder how I could possibly like it more than The Dark Side of the Moon)

This album reminds me very strongly of Rush's 2112: One great twenty minute sound surrounded by a bunch of filler. One of These Days is a strong song, but the other four songs are completely forgettable. They are boring and, worse yet, bland.

Echoes is, of course, exceptional. I think the early sections of the song - say, the first eight minutes or so - are great, combining eerie lyrics with haunting, atmospheric, and textured music. I don't mind the breakdown during the middle of the song, but I can't stand the 'whale song' section, which is piercing and annoying. The end return of the song is as quality as the first. While I wouldn't say it's as good as the song 2112, it is still very good.

A mixed bag, really: One strong medium length song, four pieces of filler, and then a very good, somewhat uneven twenty-three minute cut. Overall, Meddle is only an average Pink Floyd album.

I could write two reviews of this album. Having bought it in the seventies under the influence of errr... blotted like paper and various like substances it actually scared the hell out of me a few times...lol.. so I could write the delusional, bordering on paranoid tripped out schitzophrenic review which back then we believed was a requirement for listening to Pink Floyd. However now living in the real world as opposed to the Syd Barrett(who of course is not on this album)world I can say there is nothing too horrific here. I even get a chuckle out of the "One of these days I am going to chop you into little pieces" line towards the end of of "One of these Days", which I consider along with "Echoes" the meat of the album. The four songs in between these two are pleasant tunes, with the exception of "Seamus" where the music is too low and the dogs too loud(my hound loves this tune) and yes is a little irritating. This was really the last of it's kind from Floyd as there direction would change after this release.  Good stuff, get out the strobe lights man!

P F  always sounded like a band on the verge of becoming ahead of it's time before Meddle.  This is the first P F album that actually sounds like seasoned musicians with musical integrity and something to say.   Whereas everything before this tended to rely more on trying to be avante garde than it did strong musical abilities, this album sounds like guys that know how to write and play music and could do so without technology if they needed to.   
  The actual recording itself is VERY professional sounding.  The acoustic guitars sound very crisp and in your face.    The drums sound much like they do on later P F albums ,  as does the electric guitars and various keyboard sounds, and the effects.   P F found their sound on this album.  
   This in many ways sounds like the Dark Side practice album.   As with later PF albums,  this one is about the music and production as compared to their early stuff which was almost entirely about the songs.   The early stuff sounded like P F was experimenting in an attempt to find a proper base for the songs.   This album sounds like the atmosphere for the songs was part of the composition process.    
   You hear bits and pieces of the old PF combined with snippets of what came later on DSOTM,  Animals,  WYWH  and MLOR.     
 Also, unlike earlier albums which sounded like a bunch of unrelated songs grouped together to form an album, this one sounds like one cohesive unit.  
  I think much of this is because by the time this album was made David Gilmour was a solid enough member of the band to have a commanding influence on their sound.    
  If you like Dark Side, Animals, and MLOR  then this is a must have.
People who say this is PF's best album are really full of it.  Now I don't fall for all of Dark Side of the Moon's cheap tricks

review: This is what I consider to be the first "Traditional" Pink Foyd album. The first where the classic line-up finally cut the looking for a way concept and found it. "One of these days" is an anarchic Pink Floyd classic. "A pilow of Winds" sets the mellow mood. "Fearless" is a happy song, and "Echoes" is a soundscaping 23-minute jewel which to this day is timeless. The Pompeii film is one of the most underatted rock films of the time.

This is one of the few albums in which I would consider every song to be average or above. I love the diversity of this album due to it's diversity from blues-rock (with the "rock" almost out of the picture) to ballads such as "Echoes" (easily the best song on the album) with a trippy and almost frightful (erm..."instrumental?) break in the middle. Wow, such an amazing piece of music, lost under the Piper at the Gates of Dawn and DSOTM and later releases. The first half of the album (that is, the first 5 songs), is pretty good. "One of These Days" is one of the best instrumental pieces I've ever heard, ranking up there with "The Great Gig in the Sky" and Metallica's "Orion". "A Pillow of Winds" is an OK number but nowhere near Floyd's best. "Fearless" is probably the #3 song on the album, its catchy, has a bit of a country twang to it. "San Tropez" is very tropical, Pink Floyd does a great job portraying the setting through music. "Seamus" is kind of an annoying song. Humorous but unnecessary. The barking at the beginning is too loud, it drowns out the rest of the music. "Seamus" is the worst song here.

Now for the fun stuff. What can I say about "Echoes"? This is quite an amazing song, and accounts for the entire second half of the album, clocking in at about 23:30. It is the best song here, and accounts for at least 3 of the 4.5 stars I am giving this album. This song truly shows what Pink Floyd can do (or could do) when they all pull(ed) together as a team. The lyrics to the song are prevalent in the beginning, but about 8 minutes in, the song takes on almost a whole new form, echoing mechanical screams after a few well played solos. Then, at the end, the lyrics come back as the song comes to a close. This song is truly something special and is why every Floyd fan should own this album.

One of two Floyd CD's I own (the other being good ol' Dark Side).  This one is lots more progressive seeming at first, what with the side-long epic in Echoes, but the rest is based on the same kind of poppy/psychedelia of that disk.

One of These Days is a charging opener with some fast bass-picking and riveting Guitar/Synth stuff.  The one vocal line is pretty much hilarious, but in a good way.  I love it, it's perfect driving music.

A Pillow of Winds is a nice ballad to cool things down.  Not in the same level of some of the other progressive ballads I've heard, but it's pleasant enough.

Fearless is pretty thrilling... until the stupid choir ending that doesn't even really fit with the rest of the song.  At around 4:50 the song might as well just end, I'm skipping the rest of it anyways.  But other than that complaint, I absolutely love this song, from the simple lyrics to the awesome (if not repetitive) instrumental chorus.

I honestly skip San Tropez and Seamus every single time.  They're just throwaways to me.  San Tropez is especially out of place.

And Echoes.  Well, it's got a lot of fluff (the first 1:40-ish, the middle section of random screaming), but other than that I love it.  The Phantom of the Opera theme is really neat, and I really like the build after the screamy section.  A bit inconsistent, but great overall.

Which is really how I rate Meddle as a whole.  The great parts are... well, great.  But there's certainly a decent helping of filler as well.
