review:
So Meddle, one could liken this album to someone coming off one substance and starting to abuse another one. After the Syd influence era, Floyd decided to try and give the whole song writing thing ago themselves and so here is Meddle. The album screams of a band reaching maturity, nothing, besides Echoes, shows any type of coherence and so most of the songs come off as filler for track 6. One of these days starts off quiet enough but slowly starts to build into a good opener, however like the rest of the songs comes off as random in context of the entire thing. A Pillow of Winds is a smoothing song, near enough ballad form, quite disarming and has you drifting off in no time. Fearless is also good, albiet discomforting with the chior following in through-out the entire thing, as if to shy away from itself and draw your attention to something else. San Tropez and Seamus are in pretty much the same boat, both forgettable and nothing remarkable about either of them. 



Now it's onto Echoes, is it overrated? probably but I don't think that stops the song from eclipsing everything that Pink Floyd have ever done. Yeah I love the song to bits and nothing, well Crazy Diamond comes close, that they did before and after seems to reach the level that Echoes climbed to. It's raw, full of energy and seem abit disjointed at time and you know that's ok with me. The entire song has this grand spacey feel, as if it's not so much a song as it is a soundtrack to travelling across the stars to some distant planet. The melding of Gilmour and Wright on this song is just perfection, adding an almost other worldy feel to the already alien song. Unlike Shine on you crazy diamond, Echoes isn't a song of multiple suites running in and out of the song, it's one complete song. Both a strength and a weakness to it, some of it just sounds weird and gives the feeling that the guys had no idea what to add in and so just put in random sounds to make the song longer. So the entire song isn't overflowing with grand sounds, progressing time signatures, complex lyrics and a over arching feel that it's towards something even bigger or some message that needs to be told. It can be both under and overwhelming at times, there isn't a real middle ground through-out that gives you some type of rest to just absorb it all. So I mean Shine on you crazy diamond is a better song, production wise and quite frankly so are most of the other classic Floyd songs that you will listen to or not listen to. But all in all this is still my favorite Pink Floyd song evar, the fact that the song was a show of what was to come and the many problems it does indeed have only add to the greater love I have for it. 



So that's Meddle, messed up, not all there and certainly not the epic grand journey of albums like Piper, Dark Side and Wish You Where Here. But it didn't need to be, all it needed to be was the stepping stone on which they could build the foundations for Dark Side and then destroy it with The Wall.

Pink Floyd has always been one of my favorite bands, and so this is going to be a weird album for me to review, based on the fact that this contains arguably Pink Floyd's strongest song along with one of their weakest. For the most part this album is wonderful, as this was when the band started to jump into more progressive tendencies and slowly began to move away from their pure psychedelic sound of the earlier Barret era. With that said, the first four songs are all great to varying degrees. Then we come to "Seamus", which involves lead singer David Gilmour singing over a nice acoustic guitar for about 2 minutes. And this wouldn't be a problem if there wasn't a dog howling and yelping in the background for half of the song. It is completely distracting, annoying, and unnecessary. But that all disappears when we get to the epic closing song "Echoes", which takes the audience on a musical journey through what feels like time and space. Echoes is a masterpiece of a song in my opinion, and completely washes away all the negative feelings I had towards Seamus, which I'd like to make clear is not a terrible song, just a relatively mediocre one for this otherwise great album.

8.9 out of 10

Favourite tracks: One of these days / Echoes

Meddle, released in late 1971, saw Pink Floyd’s best-known sound taking shape. The instrumental “One of These Days” opens the album. The powerful, reverberating bassline propels the song as stabs of organ, twisting guitar, and the odd strike of percussion continue to build. The unusual effect on the bass acts as an instrument unto itself, particularly in the slower midsection. The song’s final two minutes explode with searing guitar and warbling organ.



“A Pillow of Winds” follows this striking opener. While I’d argue that this song fits into the overall flow of the record, its slow, folky composition pales in comparison to the highlights on Meddle. “Fearless” maintains a similar sound palette, but the melody is more engaging, and the inclusion of drumming keeps the momentum up.



Side 1 ends on a pair of less-than-impressive tracks. “San Tropez” is a lightweight, jazzy piece, and “Seamus” is a brief, forgettable bit of acoustic blues with a howling dog in the background.



What everyone knows Meddle for, though, is the closing opus: “Echoes”. Covering all of side 2 and clocking in at over 23 minutes, this is one of Pink Floyd’s indisputable masterpieces. From the opening pinging piano notes, which evoke a satellite calling out from the icy void of space, the song gradually builds while Nick Mason’s drums complement the grand-sounding organ.



Gilmour and Wright harmonize their vocals on this song, giving a gentle, dreamy atmosphere. The chorus swells gracefully before dissolving into a dramatic, downward guitar riff. Following the second verse, the song enters an extended instrumental period. At first, it’s a natural extension of the post-chorus guitar solo, but the structure shifts, subtly at first, as the solo builds in intensity.



Around the 7-minute mark, “Echoes” enters a funkier, groovier movement. The irregular stabs of Hammond organ add depth behind Gilmour’s bend-filled, bluesy soloing. After a few minutes, the song enters its well-known “whale song” section. Amid a sparse and ominous backdrop, Gilmour’s guitar wails and squeals sharply.



These few minutes of eerie atmospherics gradually give way to an extended crescendo. Farfisa organ and a few more piano pings reestablish the opening atmosphere. Light cymbals and muted guitar and bass propel the movement as Rick Wright plays an organ solo over top of it. At 18 minutes, this section reaches its climax. David Gilmour unleashes a magnificent, glimmering arpeggio before dissolving into a third verse. The final few minutes feature one more masterful guitar solo before the song quietly fades out.
This album is all about 'Echoes'. But that's okay as it more than makes up for a pretty forgettable Side A.
"Echoes" is just about everything I'll ever want in Progressive/Space Rock.

Side A has never done a whole lot for me. "A Pillow of Winds" is mostly beautiful and "Fearless" is mostly epic — but unchangingly so. There are no dynamics. Otherwise, "One of These Days" is a no-brainer classic and surely one of the most iconic album-openers of all time, "San Tropez" is a breezy, jazzy ditty with a piano solo that I once saw my good friend Max air-piano note-for-note, and "Seamus" is a throwaway mood-setter (sorta like "Horizons" before "Supper's Ready" — although of course "Horizons" is a beautiful little song in its own right).



And then there's "Echoes," about which I could easily write 10,000 words and not be done, and for that very reason I won't.
Their best album together with Piper at the Gates of Dawn and the Dark Side...and the idea of Seamus closes the first side before you enter the world of Echoes...

It's so refreshing going back to earlier Pink Floyd works. I mean, I like their more famous albums as much as the next guy, but post-Dark Side Floyd just starts feeling so suffocating after a while. There's this constant attempt to be epic and sweep you off your feet that must have come from the massive success of their '73 release and the pressures to match it. Even if they managed to arguably maintain their high quality throughout the 70s, it still feels like they're stressed as fuck, culminating in the massively neurotic atmosphere of The Wall.

These earlier albums just feel so relaxed in comparison. I love how chill they sound in the first part of Meddle, aside from the more anxious opening track (and even that doesn't come close to The Wall levels of crazy). I also had this idea in my head of Pink Floyd as somewhat detached from the blues rock tradition, being more baroque and progressive, but, man, were they bluesy on this record. A lot of the tracks in the first half just put a smile on my face, like the jazzy groove of San Tropez and the anguished dog howls in the background of Seamus. It's my understanding that this is considered one of the worst Pink Floyd songs, and although I can see where that comes from (it's, at best, light fare, and, at worst, cheesy and a bit embarrassing), I also believe some of the hate comes from fans of their monolithic, self-aggrandizing later albums discovering this different side of the band and being weirded out by it. I wouldn't call any of this a masterwork, but I had fun with this version of a Pink Floyd with nothing to prove.

Echoes, which comprises the lot of the second half, is more reminiscent of later Floyd. It's an awesome track, slowly evolving into a funky jam, then disintegrating into interstellar chaos, and finally reshaping itself again in time for a grand finish. It doesn't fit in with the first half of the record at all, feeling like the band just threw this long suite and the rest of the songs together and called it an album, but that's part of the unstressed approach I mentioned earlier, and didn't really bother me. Overall, I say check this out if you’re a Pink Floyd fan that still hasn’t ventured into the treasure cove of their earlier work, or if you just want some cool psychedelic music.

review:
Pink Floyd had an amazing start with The Piper at the Gates of Dawn. To this day, that record is hailed as one of the shining achievements of psychedelic pop, and for good reason (as most or all of the material on it is fucking great). It was the first and last record written by Syd Barrett, and to be frank, the band might as well had broken up after Barrett's departure and been one of those  United States of America-tier one-off psych classics of the 60s seldom discussed from boomer to boomer, a la "hey, remember Pink Floyd? that record was good". It was clear that the band struggled to find their footing in the coming years, pining to achieve more material evocative of their earlier long-form meditative masterpieces.  Saucerful was a decent attempt, while Ummagumma (or, the studio works on it) and Atom Heart Mother are essentially experimental albums parallel with simply fucking around and hoping it sounds good. Meddle, on the other hand, was the band's return to their former glory, not in psychedelic pop, but rather entering the foray of space rock and expansive atmospheres. 

Side A is a collection of songs absolutely worthy of praise, even in the presence of this record's b-side. One of These Days, despite its fair amount of criticism, is a wildly inventive way of opening the record and serves as a chaotic introduction to an otherworldly experience. Don't believe the critics, it's an absolute highlight. The next track, A Pillow of Winds isn't as brilliant, but serves as an eye-in-the-storm (per ce) between the previous and following tracks, working as an absolutely heavenly departure from the record's pre-established violence. Fearless follows up with possibly one of Pink Floyd's most iconic and catchy guitar lines, as well as earning the title of "best on this side". This three-track run, from the opener to Fearless, is absolutely solid and makes for one of Pink Floyd's best, even when compared to their later material post-DSOTM. The unfortunate truth of the record lies in the next two tracks which end out side A, those being San Tropez and Seamus. The former is a nice enough departure, serving as a fun tune enjoyable in the right setting. The latter, though, is just flat out annoying. Why either track was included on the record is beyond me, especially when considering the general atmosphere of the remainder of the music present throughout. Serves as an annoying speedbump in the track list, which is thankfully skipped by simply stopping the record and flipping it over, pretending Fearless is the true closer to this side (it deserves the title).

Side B only contains one song: Echoes. Similar in fashion to Kraftwerk's breakout Autobahn, this track takes up the entire side as it weighs in at a staggering 23 minutes long. I'm already reminded of visions of Pink Floyd's earlier Atom Heart Mother, what with the title track being a decent-enough (yet scattered) long-form piece that likes getting on my nerves at times. At this point, I'm just hoping not to have my time wasted by this thing.

Echoes is a rock opus like no other. I would call it unique if not for the similarly-formatted LONG SEASON, released a few decades later, or perhaps Close to the Edge's title track matches up in scope. The disparity lies in execution: instead of focusing elsewhere, Echoes instead focuses its efforts on creating a brilliant, evocative piece sending the listener to the outer cosmos upon its stellar heights and dark, oppressive midsection. Simply put: Echoes is a masterpiece, and would live on as not only Pink Floyd's greatest work, but furthermore as one of the best rock songs composed to this day. Remember that great run on side A? The sheer magnitude of Echoes' quality stalls any memory of the record's former half until revisiting it. Despite drawing comparisons to other works of the time, I can't come up with many other pieces that really match the scope and execution of this piece. It's absolutely mind-bending in every way possible. I cannot praise this song enough, the experiences created by simply turning the lights off and letting this blast over your headphones is absolutely surreal and cement the track as a must-have in anyone's repertoire of musical knowledge. 

Despite a pretty rough pothole in the album's tracklist, Meddle is Pink Floyd finally back at their best. They've found their footing going forward for their next few records, which is intense, meditative passages evocative of deep space. No wonder their next real effort, Dark Side of the Moon carried with it such ethereal focus on the realm beyond our atmosphere. As I said, Echoes is a fucking masterpiece in every respect, if you hear anything from this album, make it Echoes. An absolutely brilliant exploration of the mind, from a band that more than deserves the immense popularity they've caught in the public conscious. Despite not being their first, Meddle was Pink Floyd's spiritual beginning and defined their intent moving forward. A stunning experience.

That iconic "ping" from Echoes sends shivers down my spine every time.

I could write a book about this album. It is so perfect, so fantastically written and played. Every time I get somewhere near the end of Fearless and hear the Liverpool fans singing proudly You'll Never Walk Alone my neurotransmitters are already stuffed with positive energy. Hearing this album even influenced such details as interest in football in my case. 

On the subject of Echoes, I will say this much: it is my favorite composition in the history of strictly rock music. I idolize both the studio version and the live version from Pompeii. Can you fucking hear what they're doing before that funky entrance? The marriage of guitar, bass, drums and hammonds in the final resolution of the basic moment is so infernally dense and psychedelic, I think they must have eaten a triple dose of LSD to write it. Intensity on the level of Effigy of The Forgotten by Suffocation but these albums are separated by 20 years of musical evolution in wreaking havoc. 

Everything here is perfect and fully deserves the maximum rating. 

This is definitely my favorite album of theirs. Their best release along with Dark Side of The Moon.

A transitional album, Meddle sees Pink Floyd at an experimental peak with it's wide range of moods and styles hinting at an era that would go on to define their career just a few years later with the release of The Dark Side of the Moon. A Pillow of Winds is a quiet, acoustic piece, San Tropez: a jazz-embellished, pop song, Seamus: a bluesy, "joke track" featuring howls from a dog guitarist David Gilmour happened to be taking care of at the time. The album's closer, Echoes, is not only one of Pink Floyd's greatest achievements but one of the finest examples of progressive rock. Echoes' use of slow guitar ripples and variety of sonic textures perfectly encapsulate that underwater ear on the album cover.



Favorite Tracks: Echoes, A Pillow of Winds

Pink Floyd is a band I've always felt guilty for not being head-over-heels in love with. Sure, music is mostly subjective but I know this is supposed to be an objectively great group. Their influence on pop, rock and experimental music is unquestionable, and David Gilmour is rightfully revered as a guitar virtuoso, but there's often been something missing from their most renowned records for me, a sense of passion and joy. Instead of sounding like musicians having fun, making things up as they go and imbuing their ever-loving soul into their art, albums like The Wall felt like soulless noodling and pretentious fuckery I found hard to enjoy, let alone appreciate as an outsider. Being surrounded by friends who constantly sang their praises, coupled with the band's legendarily obnoxious fanbase who criticize every artist afterwards as beneath Floyd and fly into a rage at the mildest of criticisms (I realize this isn't a problem exclusive to Floyd heads, but it's pretty damn common), was just icing on the metaphorical sawdust cake. I almost ended up swearing off prog entirely because of it (*chuckle*, that would've been awful)

Now, what made me change my opinion on the band? Well, you see it was primarily through the realize that the majority of Floyd's output I didn't enjoy stemmed primarily from the mind of Roger Waters. While Gilmour is undoubtly a genius, In my eyes Waters is a hack siphoning the talents of David and riding on the coattails of Syd Barrett, twisting the band ever further into an ostentatious parody. His machinations culminated in The Wall, an epic album of grandiose proportions. I understand why The Wall is so well liked by others, but I for one can't stand it. It feels needlessly lugubrious, somehow both rudimentary and overwritten, and just plain boring (the worst sin for an album to exhibit). The fact that it's a double album is even worse, making the ordeal far longer and more bloated than it already is. So many themes and song motifs are revisited and retread, and if you've heard my thoughts on The Glow Part 2, I loathe it when artists try to beat you over the head with the same leitmotif or audiological sample, particularly on long albums. Sure, I've heard the conceptual argument a million times, but some of my favourite albums (TDS, Zen Arcade, 69 Love Songs etc.) are conceptual, and well regarded ones at that. The only differences they possess is that those albums are varied and eclectic. The best concept and double albums often exhibit a variety of sounds throughout their runtime, but with The Wall you pretty much know exactly what you're gonna get from the first few tracks. Anyways, back on topic.

Upon realizing my beef was with Waters and not the band itself, I decided to look deeper into the catalogue to see if I could confirm my hypothesis. This is what leads me into Meddle. A few weeks ago I elected to listen to Meddle for a combination of reasons, mainly the cover was cool and Echoes was the name of Koichi's stand from Jojo. I wasn't sure what I was expecting, but upon my first listen I was transporting into another universe, a veritable stream of tranquility flowing into me as I waded through waves of lush sound. Seriously, the combination of Folk, Psychedelia and Prog Rock is unearthly and unlike anything I've had the chance of hearing before or since. Sure, the record does sound a little samey and sedate but who cares? The "samey" sound is fucking gorgeous. The first half goes by, and I'm treated to one of the greatest feats in music: the pocket-rock symphony that is Echoes. Boy, is it wonderful. The stream of conscious lyrics, interstellar atmosphere, aquatic ambience and neoclassical instrumentation is utterly gorgeous, and goes by far faster than you'd expect for a 23 minute song. After my listening session, and apart from the first side being ever so slightly fluffy, I was utterly floored and in the coming weeks I decided to reevaluate some of the band's catalogue and sure enough, it's pretty solid. They'll never be one of my Top 10, or even Top 20 bands but are alright in my books.



Best Tracks: Echoes, Seamus, Fearless

This psychedelic and prog rock album starts off with a droning instrumental building layers upon layers of different sounds with a galloping bass distorted guitar riffs. The album then leads into soft upbeat and summery songs with beautiful chord progression that compliment the soft and optimistic vocals that make you feel care free and at peace. The album then ends with a 23 minute masterpiece of experimental prog rock with woozy vocals, funky bass and keyboard riffs along with shredding guitar solos that build up on each other to form a complex and meditative sound that transports you, taking you on a journey into another world that the music creates.

Fave songs: One of these days, Fearless, San Tropez, Echoes.

The era of Pink Floyd nestled between The Piper at the Gates of Dawn and The Dark Side of the Moon has been my favourite for a while, and I do not see that changing. It's the only Pink Floyd era with more than one principle songwriter, Roger, David - hell, even Richard and Nick get a few tracks - are each competent songwriters and their skills and ideas contrast well to make a comfy and always enjoyable fusion of folk, psychedelia, rock, and this amazingly nostalgic, quaint and homey atmosphere I just can't get enough of.

Meddle is probably the conceptual epicenter of this era. The A-Side is a collection of great songs with the aforementioned vibe - Fearless is undoubtedly my favourite here but I love them all for their different reasons.

Echoes comprises the entirety of the B-side. It's utterly bizarre that these two sides are on the same record, it makes the album hard to talk about and say, rank the tracks on it as Echoes is so radically different in goal and method than anything else here. However, I can say without a doubt that it is forward-pushing, filled to the brim with great sections both vocal and instrumental, and amazingly foreshadowed what Pink Floyd would be up to in the 1970s.
Other than Echoes (which I knew would be great) I don't think I'll ever remember this album existed. Shame

review:
This album sits in a strange middle ground to me. I haven't yet heard Atom Heart Mother so I do not know if this is where this middle ground begins but this album is quite literally separated into two. If you look at the track listing here on RateYourMusic you can see that side A is comprised of 5 tracks. 5 tracks for a side is fairly standard practice for something like a pop album or something of that ilk, not a Pink Floyd album. Side B on the other hand is quite literally one track, Echoes. The way I see it is that the first side is a last hurrah of their 60s style when Syd Barrett was at the helm, but side B represents the direction they wanted to head in, the evolution they pursued.

The first side is pleasant with some great guitar work as expected, amazing organs and driving drums throughout. Nothing gets too weird on side A, it is all short form prog rock songs essentially, and as you'd expect from a class act such as Pink Floyd they're all great songs. Seamus is very nice, just wanna give special mention to the last track on side A because it is very sweet.

Side B is this one 23 minute prog monster that is ever evolving, opening with some strange tones, the first half is rapturous, exhilarating, transcendent, you get the idea. It has undeniable groove and hits some unbelievable highs with the guitar work. By the halfway point it gets weirder, with shrieks and woozy tones, it gets creepy at this point and it's brilliant. We get back to that intro tone that keeps stabbing through and then a build begins, it's palpable. This last leg of the track is really quite something, with excellent tension building. It's subtle enough to not be overwhelming yet just after 18 minutes this feels like everything this album, scratch that, Pink Floyds entire career had been leading up to. 

Looking at the track listings to previous albums just after writing all of this I feel like a bit of a plonker, long tracks from the very beginning, I'm sure they're all as proggy, if not proggier, than this one. Regardless, Echoes seems to be cited as a fan favourite quite often so I'll leave what I've said as a testament to my feelings prior to further inquest of their discography.

Anyway, pretty good album.

review:
For me, this is the first 'required' Pink Floyd album. Not to say the previous albums are lacking, there's a lot of solid material to go back to, but this just feels like a mastery on all levels. While I might slightly prefer a few of their other albums, this remains one of my top picks and is a bit of a hidden gem in their catalogue amongst more casual listeners.



From the atmospheric and menacing opener to the absolute classic of a closing epic, this is just a solid album through and through, even the 'middling' tracks are beautiful, and while they don't hit the absolute heights of its bookends, they still stand ~mostly~ on their own. Tat said, 'Seamus' fills like the band was just filling space, and 'San Tropez' feels a little misguided. 



That said, 'One of These Days,' feels almost like a farewell to the band's psychedelic era, and 'Echoes" feels like an amazing step into a new and perfected prog-rock world, while still retaining all the lessons the band had learned and applied up to this point. Considering the track length, it never feels like its bloated or drags on. It's a gem and so is this entire album.

Everyone knows the classic Pink Floyd albums like The Wall, Wish You Were Here, and The Dark Side of The Moon. Meddle, however, is kind of odd. It almost is what Rubber Soul was for The Beatles. An album that served as a transition between two phases. On the A side of the record, we mostly hear what is known as "Psych Floyd". The songs are great and worth the listen. But as soon as you flip the record, you get the first glimpse into the future vision of Pink Floyd. "Echoes" is a prog rock masterpiece. It is a behemoth of a song clocking in at over 20 minutes. However, it is structured in a way that makes it seem like there's a fresh new song every couple of minutes. Its atmosphere is also very vivid and is laid out perfectly in the beginning of the song. The song captures the cold and quiet of drowning under the turbulent waves of the ocean, consciousness fading in and out with each change of music. It is definitely one of my favorite Pink Floyd songs. As for the album, I feel like "Echoes" just shrouds every other song making the other songs almost obsolete. Nevertheless I still enjoy the album.

My mother is a big Pink Floyd fan. Naturally, once I got my first “car” when I was 20 (a pre-Defender-era Land Rover 90, of all vehicles!) I “borrowed” a bunch of their cassettes, and this beaten copy of Meddle was one of the ones that got the most plays.



I’m no expert in the world of the Floyd, but the early 70s were of course the main portion of their astronomical rise to fame. Wether that had anything to directly do with Meddle I don’t know, but even today, after replacing a faulty drive belt on an old Sony Walkman, am I blown away by the sound on this record.



Yesterday my son was born so maybe I’m feeling a bit emotional, but playing Meddle today took me back not only to 2010, where I drove no doubt reckless over and under things in my first car, but to the early 2000s when I listened to Pink Floyd and tried to understand why my mother liked them so much. This is such a beautiful record.

I guess "One of These Days" isn't so bad, certainly predicts elements of their later sound, but I hate the electronic wails in the middle of Echoes, why does prog rock get so full of itself.

I think if there is one album where I spent most time with of any album of the Floyd or maybe of any album of all time it will probably be Meddle (or perhaps Animals). This is the one album I can put on at any given time of any given day. 
For me personally, Meddle especially reminds me of the day I had my second Mushroom trip which at first went terribly wrong when I putted on Ummagumma. I have a Pink Floyd poster in my room and during "Be Careful with that Axe Eugene" that poster fell down, I went absolutely nuts. When Roger Waters started screaming during the song I got so spooked that I ran out of my room to just eat some sugar (I was told that it would help go get you normal again) and I eventually ended up in my bed alone after sending away my friends in total fear. Still tripping I started to calm down again and decided I wanted to put on some music that really made me happy. So Queens of the Stone Age’s “Songs for the Deaf” was put in my CD-player and I was dancing for about an hour, alone in my room. Next up where the Desert Sessions 9 & 10 and it had such a groove in it that I couldn’t even lay still in my bed anymore. I just had to groove all over the place even when lying in my bad I was snapping my fingers. My bad trip had made a complete 180 degrees turn and it was only getting better when I decided to put on Meddle. “One of these days” was a bit scary I remember but then “A Pillow of winds” showed its face and I was simply in heaven, just floating on the music. “Fearless” was even better and now appeared to be the perfect song. But it could get even better with the next song, namely “San Tropez”. I always found this is nice song but not since that day in January I discovered the true nature of it. The song grooved so inevitably heavy I didn’t even know what to do anymore. I was just putting it on repeat over and over again to just groove along that godly rhythm. “Seamus’ was again a neat song and nothing more special than under normal circumstances but “Echoes” was really quite something. 

The thing is, normally Echoes is my all time favourite song in every way, but this time the whole Echoes experience freaked me out so much that I didn’t even know what to think of it. When the song opened I was of course in heaven. I mean how could I resist such wonderful melodies and such magical trippy sounds? The solo was beautiful and freak out part with the underlying blues tune was cool but when things slowly started to fade out I got scared by it. The whale noises where absolutely freaking me out and the room went dark. I wasn’t fading into darkness but the music was, so much that Echoes actually represented death for me at that time. Luckily I got over it in time since the track started building up again. It actually felt like a year had gone bye somehow, opening in spring, flourishing in climaxing in summer, having some last impulses in autumn and dying in the dark days of winter, only to be reborn again in the next spring. Echoes represented that for me, building and building again into another summer where it eventually faded out ending the album. 

So this is my ultimate experience with this album which I still consider my favourite Pink Floyd album merely for its mood and my experience with it. It is laidback music at its finest but it grooves like no other in San Tropez and shines like no other in Fearless and especially “Echoes” which after that weird trip became my favourite song of all time even more so.

review: I love this album.  It is not a concept album like Pink Floyd's widely considered greatest work, _The Wall_, it is just a collection of excellent songs.  "Fearless" is an incredible lesson in songwriting.  "A Pillow of Winds" is gorgeousness and gorgeousity.  "Echoes" is classic Floyd at their instrumental best.  My personal favorite, and one of my favorite tracks of all time, is "San Tropez".  Don't ask me why.  It makes me giddy every time I hear it, which is appoximately once a day.

After Syd Barrett left Pink Floyd had a bit of trouble regaining firm stylistic ground. Barrett being the wellspring of material, the rest of the band, plus Gilmour had to learn to pick up the slack. By their fourth album _Ummagumma_(1969) it was apparent that they finally got their act together, well judging by the follow up _Atom Heart Mother_, they still had a way to go. The title track, a collaborative effort with Ron Geesin (who worked with Floyd bassist Roger Waters on his wretched _Music From The Body_ soundtrack) is an absolute art rock mess. It's great that they include a choir and horn symphony on this particular work, although some clearly worked out ideas is really what this piece needed. In essence the title track is not a total loss, although refinement was desperately needed. Fortunately this album is saved by such wonderful tracks as "Summer 68" and "Fat Old Sun," which alone make this album a worthy purchase for any Pink Floyd fan. Unfortunately this album ends on a more tedious and absurd note than it began. "Alan's Psychadelic Breakfast" (named for Alan Parsons, the album's engineer and eventual leader of the Alan Parsons Project) is a thrown together, vinyl filling piece of crap. Pink Floyd followed up their attempt at avant-garde experimentalism on the studio half of _Ummagumma_ with another ambitious effort in the symphonic rock of _Atom Heart Mother_. 



With the side-long 20+ minute title track, they begin a series of slowly evolving '70's epics. With a large horn section and choir in tow, they plod along with slow tempos, melodramatic effects, and slow investigation of musical ideas (it's all instrumental, by the way...). This isn't going to be to everyone's liking, but those were the days when Deep Purple, Keith Emerson, and Yes were trying to combine hard rock with classical ambitions and orchestration. However, Pink Floyd had been at the center of English artsy rock longer than the rest of 'em, and they did manage to give the piece their repetitive ambient charge that separates it from the others. I like the strident main theme, and the avant-garde electronic part near the end is alright, but the section with the choir sounds a little dated and overlong. There's plenty of soloing/noodling in between.



The second half is comprised of 3 songs in the 5 minute range and a long one at the end. The first 3 are good concise statements of where the band was at and gave each band member (except for drummer Nick Mason) another chance to write their own song as on _Ummagumma_, but are more straightforward this time around, perhaps having gotten their ambitions out of the way in the title suite. This works to their advantage. "If" is a folksy song in which Roger Waters begins to really show his future direction with simple melodicism and self-absorbed lyrics with references to insanity. "Fat Old Sun" shows guitarist David Gilmour to be the most mainstream of the band, and finds a nice setting between delicacy and rock music. However, the crown jewel here is keyboardist Rick Wright's "Summer '68", which utilizes the horn section from the title suite more concisely and powerfully. This song captures _both_ the breezy whimsy and darker psychedelic intensity of the era nicely as the sections unfold. "Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast" wraps things up with the echoing sounds of someone making breakfast interspersed between pleasant but unfocused melodic noodling. 



To be honest, the experimentation here is not altogether successful - this is non-essential for non-followers. But what I like so much about 60's and 70's Pink Floyd is that they (slowly) evolved from release-to-release as true creative artists usually do, which makes this a worthwhile piece of their puzzle for followers.

review:
Finally shed of Syd Barrett, Pink Floyd continues their promising upward trend with each successive album since their debut. Of this trilogy of albums, Atom Heart Mother is clearly the strongest. The title track is the best extended track the band had dones by this point in their career even if it comes well short of the lofty standard set by "Echoes". The shorter tracks in the middle of the album are all fine, at times they can glide by without sticking with a memorable hook but "Summer '68" is possibly the best song of any kind they'd done up to this point. The closing track "Alan's Psychadelic Breakfast prevents me from going a half star higher as it reminds the listener of the faults of Pink Floyd's previous two albums. Strange background noises (of someone actually preparing a breakfast how novel!) are distracting from anything else of note going on. Best case scenario I don't pay attention to the closing track but in coming back to review this album I was forced to come face to face with how uninspired a track it is. Even with a lackluster closer, this is the best album Pink Floyd had done up to this point edging out Saucerful of Secrets. The band wouldn't really get things going until the excellent Meddle.

review: Minimal whining from Waters makes this better than many post Barrett albums. Particularly good is Summer '68. Wright is always awesome!

I think I am an exceptional listener of Pink Floyd. For me, the 'longs' are always the least interesting tracks on their albums. On the famously cow-covered Atom Heart Mother, the symphonic, one LP side covering title track and the closing number "Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast" (nothing really psychedelic on it) sound like second rate instrumental filler to me. Instead, "If" and "Summer '68" are really good, while "Fat Old Sun" is also more than decent. I first tried to make friends with Atom Heart Mother back in the early 1990s, and I couldn't even notice that the shortish tracks were good because the lengthier ones were so dominant. Listening to the album now, I am still about to get the same feeling. In fact, by the way, Atom Heart Mother is Wish You Were Here five years before the latter was released.

Just pips "Wish You Were Here" as my favourite Floyd album.

Admittedly, wouln't be the universal choice of Floyd fans, but I feel this highly overlooked and under rated album represents Floyd's finest hour.

Pink Floyd have unfortunately developed quite a label in many people's eyes as being out-dated 'Dad' music that deserve a place only in the world's 'classic rock' radio playlists. Obviously we know that they were so much more. This album is a wonderful example of the breadth of their sound and the boundless sense of adventure in their experimentation.



The album opens with the 24 minute epic title track that travels through several different movements, including a massive climactic final reprise with a full choir and celebratory horn line. The main theme is so much fun. Like Floyd doing an epic spaghetti western. It’s glorious and psychedelic and euphoric and I bloody love it. The track moves into some great cello stuff and a stunning slide guitar solo from Dave Gilmore. It then goes some really strange places, introducing wonderfully peculiar wordless choral stuff that feels like a sprawling, playful precursor to ‘Great Gig In The Sky’. In the middle of it, Dave Gilmour let’s rip with another one of his earliest classic solos. The track also delves into some of the band's sound experiments with tape loops and field recordings creating a nice sound collage before the final climax.



The Waters penned ‘If’ is a simple folk number neatly fleshed out with bass, piano and slide guitar. It that shows Roger in an unusually sensitive mode. Wright’s track has a big and breezy sunshine psych pop feel to it that shifts into a tense horn section. It leaps from acoustic verses to Beach Boys harmonies to dramatic horns. Great fun, well written and a nice way to reintroduce the horns and give the album some cohesion (a tough thing to do when the entire first side is one very idiosyncratic psychedelic epic). Gilmour’s ‘Fat Old Sun’ is an absolute classic. Slow, spacious, dreamy, pastoral psychedelica. These three tracks perfectly capture everything that was great about early 70s Floyd. That pastoral psychedelic sound that they absolutely nailed during this period is an absolute joy to listen to. Warm bass, breezy guitars and pianos, gentle vocals and lots of space and beauty.



The closer is another psychedelic epic, this time of a much more mellow nature. It focuses on sound collage and sweet movements of gentle instrumental bliss. It’s a light and humourous end to possibly Pink Floyd’s most unusual and endearing record.



‘Atom Heart Mother’ is a wonderful exploration of Pink Floyd’s diversity and potential in their early years. Totally underrated.

The stupid cover and title are a good tip-off to the below average material inside. The title track has its moments (mostly supplied by David Gilmour's soloing) but mostly amounts to a silly self-indulgence (Spinal Tap surely take some inspiration from that level of goofy pretensions). "Summer '68" is pretty good. "If" is not. And "Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast" is a complete dud.

As the years pass, I'm close to claiming that Pink Floyd have seven certain masterpieces (Piper, Meddle, Dark Side, WYWH, Animals, The Wall, and this), as opposed to just six.

Atom Heart Mother has been ridiculed by David Gilmour and Roger Waters, but I think that's because both artists lost the plot after The Wall. It's *certainly* better than any Water-less Pink Floyd record, or any solo record associated with the band. It's easily better than The Final Cut, too.

There's a sense of adventure here and experimentation that has an earnest, organic quality. There's a countryside, mushroom-esque feel to the proceedings. You can hear how it influenced bands like Godspeed You Black Emperor and Radiohead (pastoral influence on King of Limbs).

And, applause for no politics (even though I agree with Roger's for the most part). It's just cool sounding music. Nice texture, interesting arrangements. A band being a band. Nothing is really forced. It feels exactly like what the band had in them at the time. No grandstanding, no "look at me" golden lab puppy pleas for attention and affection like Arcade Fire.

Roger's singing is great. So is David's. So is Rick's. Like the Beatles, Pink Floyd had three good vocalists, and it helps their music to never really get old. So talented.

And here's the thing: Rick Wright is unbelievable on this. Listen to his playing. Listen to the fat, organic and timeless tone he uses, and how it interplays with David, Roger and Nick. IMHO he's the star of this show. And when you really, truly pay attention to what this wizard is doing, with his complete understanding that less is more, Atom Heart Mother will click into place for you.

Each year Atom Heart Mother grows weirder and better. It never seems to lose anything, which is remarkable.

A joy.

This album is an underrated gem in the Pink Floyd catalog. It's another one of my favorite Pink Floyd albums. The title track is a trippy psychedelic/progressive instrumental masterpiece. "If" is a great Progressive/Psychedelic Folk song with beautifully sad lyrics. "Summer '68" is probably one of my least favorite tracks on the album, but it's still a great upbeat song. "Fat Old Sun" is a great Folk Rock Song with a little Psych mixed in at some points, and the song is the inspiration for the later Pink Floyd song of "High Hopes". "Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast" is a great Trippy Experimental song. Overall this album is a great mixer of Psych, Prog, Folk, and Experimental Music, very underrated, and overall great album.

Pink Floyd's Atom Heart Mother is wildly uneven, but has its moments. The 24 minute title track doesn't always work, but at its best looks forward to mid-1970s soundtrack music (I'm thinking of that brainwashing scene in Alan Pakula's Warren Beatty vehicle The Parallax View). "Summer '68" shows that the group could've succeeded as a baroque pop in the style of The Beach Boys. The remainder of the album, especially the tepid "If," never gathers any momentum. They would sort of figure it out by their next album, Meddle.

Atom Heart Mother is an interesting album in that the album cover has become such a quintessential piece of Pink Floyd iconography, yet the album itself is passed over by most casual fans, as well as the band themselves. Despite being Pink Floyd's first album to chart #1 in the UK, none of the songs from Atom Heart Mother appear on any later compilation albums, and the band never performed any of the songs past the Meddle Tour. In postmortem interviews with members of the band, they tend to regard it as one of their worst albums. 

"Atom Heart Mother is a good case, I think, for being thrown into the dustbin and never listened to by anyone ever again!"

                                                        -Roger Waters, 1985

As a whole, Atom Heart Mother is aimless, confused, and forgettable. The entirety of Side 1 is occupied by the titular song, Atom Heart Mother, a whopping 23-minute-long "epic" of six movements pieced together by an orchestral and choral arrangement by Ron Geesin. Nothing contained in this song is interesting or memorable, save for a few of the orchestral motifs. It would be more appropriate to call this song by its original title, The Amazing Pudding, since the indiscernible slosh presented in this song pretty amazing.

The second side of the album is organized in the style of Ummagumma, in which the first three songs are written and sung by an individual member of the band (except Nick Mason... Sorry Nick! We gotta make room for  "Roadie Eats Breakfast"...).

Waters' song, If, is the first of many "syd went insane i miss syd" songs that Pink Floyd would write throughout their career. In comparison to songs like Wish You Were Here and Shine on You Crazy Diamond, this song is pretty vapid, both lyrically and musically. This is best represented by Richard Wright on this song, who's boring piano arpeggios seem to indicate that there wasn't really anything for him to do on this. 

Wright's song, Summer '68, is okay. In the beginning, the organ brings a familiar and uniquely Floyd feeling to the song, but this feeling is quickly drowned out by a Pet Sounds-esque baroque pop arrangement. Everything from the horn arrangement to the harmonized "ba baba baas" qualifies the song as an uninspired pastiche of The Beach Boys. An ironic interpretation of this song, a happy, Wouldn't-It-Be-Nice sounding song about the misery of one night stands with groupies, is compelling but feels a little too generous. The novel quality of the song is negated by its glaring lack of originality. 

The theme of pastiche continues with Gilmour's song, Fat Old Sun. Everything about this song just screams Paul McCartney, from the pace, Ringo-esque drum fills, ending guitar solo, and the vocals. Especially the vocals. If I had listened to this song blind, I would've honestly told you it was McCartney singing this. The reason why this song just doesn't work is not because the song is bad in and of itself, but because this glaring lack of originality makes it so uninteresting. 

The final song on the album, Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast, is probably the best song on the album, but not by much. The field recording samples of roadie Alan Styles talking about and eating breakfast doesn't add much to the song beyond a sense of weirdness that tries to convince the listener that it's "psychedelic". The last section of the song, titled "Morning Glory" in the liner notes, has an epic sense of finality to it in the same vein as Eclipse on Dark Side of the Moon. But what's funny is that this finality seems to imply a kind of intention to the song and the album as a whole, an album that stitches together bottom-of-the-barrel ideas to imply some "psychedelic" statement. 

Atom Heart Mother marks the nadir of Pink Floyd's post-Barrett, pre-DSOTM career, a period defined largely by artistic bankruptcy. There's not many good reasons to listen to this album, even if you are a fan of Pink Floyd, given that you wouldn't miss out on anything original or interesting. I wouldn't say that I regret listening to this album only because listening to an artist's discography can give one an understanding of the progression of the artist's career, and this album certainly exemplifies one of the lowest points in Pink Floyd's career.

3/10

Album Of The Day: Pink Floyd – Atom Heart Mother (1970)

David Gilmour is on record as calling this “a load of rubbish”- just goes to show you can’t necessarily trust someone’s take on their work as I think this is brilliant.
Similar to Ummagumma, each band member was given a song to be in charge of but the others stayed in the room this time. Waters’ If is a pretty, intimate acoustic number with surprisingly dark lyrics about his failures and fears, given flavour by delicious slide guitar work from Gilmour. Wright’s Summer 68 moves from sleepily gorgeous piano led verses to a Beach Boys-esque chorus before unexpectedly cracking out dramatic horns, a song that is both inventive and appealing. Gilmour’s Fat Old Sun is a favourite of mine, a beautifully simple song with swoon-inducing vocals. It climaxes in a guitar solo at once epic and comforting, an early example of Gilmour’s talent for playing in emotional sympathy with a song. And finally Nick Mason cooks up Alan’s Psychedelic Breakfast, a 13 minute instrumental themed around the legendary fry-ups roadie Alan Styles would make on tour. An opening section of delightfully naïve keyboards from Wright makes way for a warm and folky acoustic passage from Gilmour before the whole band convenes for an uplifting piano-led finale. All the while Styles can be heard making and discussing his breakfast – the whole thing is so silly and cute it’s sure to leave a smile.
Alan’s Psychedelic Breakfast also serves as a cleansing counterweight to the darkness and turmoil of the other instrumental on this album – the titanic 23 minute title track. Among the best things the band ever did, Atom Heart Mother is a compelling and absorbing listen right from the ominous Hammond note that opens it to the overwhelming brass-augmented choral finish. Along the way there is heart-breakingly beautiful cello, hilarious mass scat-singing, spooky electronic passages and some of the best guitar Gilmour has done so far as he discovers his signature soaring soulful technique that blues and hard rock styles. And learning their lessons from Ummagumma, this time everything is cohesive, making no sense and all the sense at once as these disparate parts all flow together seamlessly. It’s just fantastic.
A mind-blowing album which remains one of my Floyd favourites.

I wish I could enjoy this album more than I did, I truly do, but after 3 listens, I simply cannot. The title track, in my opinion, is boring and best for background noise. "If" and "Summer '68" aren't bad tracks by any means, they just weren't my cup of tea on this particular day. "Fat Old Sun" is my favorite track from the album, and Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast is better than the rep it's given. Overall, there are streaks of brilliance and the clear beginnings of their 70's reign of classic songwriting, but it's mainly a swing and a miss here.



weak 6/10

Sometimes you gotta experiment to set a foot off in the right direction. I see a lot of general negativity towards both of the lengthier pieces on here, and while I also can't stand the sound of people eating, I feel the title track was the sort of experiment in structure and themes the band needed in order to more fully succeed on pretty much every following album, and 'Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast' feels like the same kind of experiment, but whereas the title track feels much more rigid and refined, the band is playing a little more loose on here while still taking steps towards more fully realized lengthier pieces.



This album would have easily gotten a lower ranking were the middle tracks of a throwaway quality, but I really like both 'If,' and 'Fat old sun.' While 'Summer' is just sort of meh, I really like the more soft, haunting, and delicate approach taken on the other two.



This certainly isn't a highlight in the Pink Floyd catalogue, and I don't think I'd want to classify it quite as a hidden gem in the same way I would for Meddle, it's still worth checking out for those fans of experimental and psych rock who haven't ventured into this transitionary period of the band.

Oh if it isn't that cow album. What's this? It all starts with a brass instrument combo, with mixed with the psychedelic floydian riffs. Jazz infused, if you will. It's like the Pink Floyd Symphony here. After the title track, each band member gets their own stab at a song. If has Waters' wonderful riffs mixed with his somewhat eerie vocals. Summer '68 greets us with Rick's psychedelic keyboarding skills. Fat Old Sun is Gilmour's sleepy song, soft and quiet, but one of the highlights. Alan's Breakfast is a bit silly, but entertaining -- Alan's thoughts while he mixes together his breakfast. Not quite the closer song you'd expect, but it's enjoyable to say the least, if only once. Combined together, you've got one strange album. One record that casual fans may skip (but I wouldn't recommend it!), but any "real" fan must give this a listen. Grab a chair, 'cause you'll have to sit down for this one.

"Atom Heart Mother" is often cited as Pink Floyd's lowest point artistically, but I completely disagree. Though half of this album is terrible, the first side is amazing (I mean it is "The Amazing Pudding"). The six part suite is experimental (not the most cohesive genre), but this is the best experimental track I've ever heard. It's a little rough around the edges, but nearly as epic as its successor, "Echoes" (and edges it out by 15 seconds). Unfortunately, the rest of the record is pretty much the usual terrible early Floyd stuff and without Syd's pop flair. "Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast" may be my least favorite Floyd tune.

Got milk?

Atom Heart Mother is another hit-or-miss collection from Pink Floyd's wandering pre-Dark Side period, dominated by the side-long orchestrally augmented title track. "Atom Heart Mother" plays like a patchwork nod to the band's occasional forays into soundtrack work, with it's triumphant main theme and alternately funky, spacey, and hypnotic sidebars. While it's got plenty going for it, the song does feel sorta cobbled together and tends to wear out its welcome before all 23 minutes are up. Flipping Atom Heart Mother unearths a series of shorter tunes, highlighted by Gilmour's idyllic "Fat Old Sun" and Wright's psychedelic pop charmer, "Summer '68." Waters' delicate acoustic entry, "If," is at least notable for it's semi-disturbing lyrics, but "Alan's Psychedelic Breakfast" comes across as filler, more concerned with sound effect gimmicks than memorable music, even if in a roundabout way it fits in to the pastoral feeling that runs through the album, cow cover and all.

con questo disco ho semplicemente capito il senso della musica...

To my ears the first epic almost neo-classical piece is the highlight to this rarely talked about Floyd album. The second half has some unique pieces but seems to drag a bit to my ears with some moments which seem to almost have a same era Beatles kind of influence. Regardless it's a unique work that certainly wouldn't disappoint the Floyd fan.

It's actually kind of amazing to listen to these mid-period Pink Floyd albums. They are so widely seen as the most prominent and premier among Prog bands....but it's clear from this and Ummagumma that they were nothing of the sort at first. Remember who we're dealing with, this was a fantastic psychedelic band. That being over they have to readjust. The ill advised Ummagumma is a marked step the fuck down from their good days, and here they crawl up a little farther. The problem is I still don't see what they're really going for, because I don't think they know either. A lot of this comes off as sort of awkward attempts to follow along contemporary British music pattersn, mainly the obligatory long epic song thing, the title track. It's there, it's cool I guess, but it almost seems like it was built to make a long song and not with much else in mind. Like Pink Floyd is trying hard to follow King Crimson's lead. Yes, following. They were followers! The obsession with english folk music has to be seen in that way too. And they really aren't so super with it. I'll say it straight up, this is a pretty dull album. Mostly strumming, and not a lot of great melody to sink your teeth into. There are the occasional moments that work a lot, namely the pretty great Summer 68' which benefits from some really pretty instrumentation plus good songwriting. Elsewhere though? Well hey, I've never been impressed at all by Floyd's obsession with sound design. They seem to think recording non-musical sounds and stuff is the key to cool, and mostly they're mistaken. Ok, so I'm hearing a man have breakfast, alright, why do I care about this? Is it funny? No. Is it interesting? Well it makes me hungry I guess, but not interested. It's like they think....no I don't know what they think. It doesn't do a damn thing for me. They use it much better later on Dark Side of the Moon, so at least it ended up getting them somewhere. But as is? Shit, what a pretentious waste of time. Not even new really, the Beatles even used stuff like this on Yellow Submarine! Anti-Prog to say the least! So still Floyd is in their awkward times...nothing to see here. Move along.

review:
Interstellar space folk-rock...and plonking noises. 75/100


   This is a review of the 1994 digital remaster. I like the sound quality on this version although I don't have any points of comparison for it. On first listen I would have given this album 72.5/100 but on second listen bumped it up to 75/100. I'd describe the music on the album as interstellar space folk-rock, experimental, soundscapes, genteel, plonking noises, fx. It's mostly instrumental. Has a relaxing atmosphere which is occasionally broken by grating noises. The studio album runs to 46:57 minutes long on one cd and the live album, which is mostly relaxing versions of their earlier material comes in at 39:21 minutes.


The studio album -


Sisyphus:

Part 1 - I've marked it down this part as being 1:08 though Wikipedia gives it a different run time. A symphonic instrumental. An Emperor march type track (I'm not even sure that phrase exists...if it does, hopefully my use of the term is apt!). Has big drum sounds, which makes me think of something imperial in any case. Might have a synthesiser. Violins and Cymbals.

Part 2 - mostly a piano piece. My notes say that this follows on from the first part without a gap, which may explain why my time length differs from Wikipedia's entry for this album. Percussion does appear later on this track...cymbals and drums. Piano has a slow-medium tempo. Track becomes more violent towards the end with the percussion and chords on the piano.

Part 3 - experimental instrumental...plucked strings, percussion on metal objects and drums, sort of animal noises (or weird human ones...sometimes it sounds like someone has inhaled helium gas and started singing!), piano?

Part 4 - symphonic, slow tempo song. Sombre tone but with some ambient noises, a la meditation music. Has sounds of twittering birds but also a jolting transition from peaceful to loud music. Maybe you could describe this piece has having three movements (?). What I take to be the third movement is relaxing.

Grantchester Meadows - has ambient nature sounds...birds twiitering, insects. The ending has an amusing bookend to this use. Dual acoustic guitars playing genteel notes.

Several species of small furry animals gathered together in a cave and grooving with a pict - if they released this song as a single, they'd just have had to sing the title once for the needle to have reached the end of the vinyl! Anyway, it sounds like ambient jungle sounds from another planet. Early on you get weird tapping percussion. Track is a bit schizophrenic at times. Sounds like a Scottish explorer or something at the end of the track. You also get experimental scat sounds and what sounds like UFO noises...presumably. I have never heard UFO noises myself.

The narrow way:

Part 1 - jarring bookends but pleasant dual acoustic guitar notes in-between. Some squeaky noises recalls their debut album. UFO sounds reappear for the intro and the outro to this track.

Part 2 - sort of Black Sabbath type proto-metal then mixed with spacey sounds. Bongo percussion.

Part 3 - a bagpipe sounding synth (presumably). Dave Gilmour sings, I think. Slow-medium tempo. Piano, cello? twangy guitar, woodwind type instruments, electric guitar at the end, bass and some solid drumming.

The Grand Vizier's garden party:

Part 1: Entrance - 1:00 minute long. Dual woodwind type instruments. Jarring military style drum/drum roll.

Part 2: Entertainment - Experimental. Like the drum intro and the tone of the drum...watery! Drums bookend this track. A candidate for my drum songs list, especially for the drum outro, which is good. Small passage in the middle of the track which has a grating, fingernails down the blackboard sound. A more experimental soundscape later. There are other ringing type noises in the track.

Part 3: Exit - 0:38 minutes long. Peaceful track with dual woodwind type instruments...recorder?


Live album:


Astronomy domine - 8:29. Seems pretty faithful to the original. Studio quality sound. Relaxing track. A percussion strong piece...cymbals etc. You only hear the crowd at the end of the track.

Careful with that axe, Eugene - 8:50. Crowd sound faint at the start. Mostly an instrumental, with just a few words and a little scat. The scream in this track prefigures that used on their "The wall" album. Sombre. Violins, organ and cymbals. Crowd sound louder at the end of the track. I heard a pop on my cd at 0:24 minutes in, I think.

Set the controls for the heart of the sun - 9:12. No crowd noises on this one. Genteel track, especially the vocals. Keyboard, hand percussion on interesting sounding drums. Big drums are used too.

A saucerful of secrets - 12:48. Got a note questioning if this is a new track. They released an album by this name right after their debut...can't remember if this was on it or not. Probably not, going on memory. Another contender for my drum songs list...this track has a bit of a "Dance with the devil" vibe to it (Cozy Powell drum solo track) as far as the rhythm goes. An instrumental pretty much, but has some vocalising at the end. Organ, bassy, cymbals. Genteel for the most part but gets noisier later. Surf Guitar sound at times and maybe they have a sort of Jimi Hendrix vibe to them as well.


Pink Floyd recommendations:


More. 4/5. Experimental album at times, but a couple of their heaviest rock songs thrown into the mix as well. Some genteel tracks too.

The wall. 9+/10. A concept album with an actual concept. Polished double cd release.

The piper at the gates of dawn. 7.5+/10. Strangely compelling debut album. Whimsical. 

The dark side of the moon. 8.75/10. Works as an album, but I'm not that keen on listening on the radio to the songs that get radio play...apart from "Us and them".

Meddle - 8.25/10. Mellow album with a bit of a lost classic on it ("One of these days").

review: An extra star for Gilmour's wonderful Narrow Way. Half a star deducted respectively for Mason's percussive drivel solo effort & Waters' scottish rantings.

It is hard to think of this album as one cohesive whole.  The live album is a great record of one of their early live shows.  "Saucerful of Secrets" sounds better here than it does on the original album, while "Careful With That Axe, Eugene" is also much better than the original single version--Roger's scream is amazing.  "Set the Controls..." and "Astronomy Domine" are also superbly performed.

  The studio album is another proposition.  It's an album with some interesting results.  The best songs are "Grantchester Meadows," which is a very lovely nature song, and "Sisyphus" which is a haunting and almost terrifying Rick Wright "keyboard extravaganza."  It could almost fit in with something by Stravinsky or Schoenberg.  David Gilmour's first solo credit is "The Narrow Way."  It is not a bad song but it sounds almost too forced--as if David Gilmour were trying to hard to be weird.  "Several Species..." is kind of fun but can get tiresome.  As for "The Grand Vizier's Garden Party" by Nick Mason, it could probably do better if someone else had helped create it.

Often I am too busy thinking about the pretty poor studio "experiments" that I forget about the quite good live disc. The first three tracks on the live disc are all ones that I really enjoyed from the studio albums. And they are performed and recorded very well. In particular 'Astronomy Domine' is wonderfully fleshed out, with some nice organ links between main phrases. 'A Saucerful of Secrets' sounds almost exactly like the studio version except for the erm... off-key chanting at the end (which makes it even more haunting!). As for the studio disc - well 'Several Species' is an absolute laugh, 'Grantchester Meadows' is quite a pleasant Waters acoustic while 'The Narrow Way' is probably the best studio piece. 'Sysyphus' has a great intro and coda but the main themes are idiotic - once described as Rick Wright using kitchen utensils and gardening tools instead of instruments. And Nick Mason has been described as bouncing tennis balls off his skins to create the main theme ('Entertainment') of the 'The Grand Vizier's...' which ruins the rather entertaining flute intro and coda.

Ummagumma seems to puzzle most people. I'm not sure why. Perhaps they see Pink Floyd only as the band that did The Piper at the Gates of Dawn or as the band that did The Dark Side of the Moon and yes, this album doesn't really sound like either. As far as I'm concerned, it sounds much better than either, but there you go.

Ummagumma falls very neatly into two parts: record one (or CD 1) is a live recording, record two (or CD 2) is a studio recording, and the ground covered here is very, very different.

The live album is extremely tight, compact and heavier than their preceding records. Bear in mind that this was 1969, the Seventies were approaching fast, and Led Zeppelin and other prog rock bands were out there, and Pink Floyd seemed to think about joining the movement. The live performances here are reworkings of earlier material in a surprisingly bluesy style, interspersed with rather spaced-out moments. In that, they are remarkably similar to the performances captured on the Live at Pompeii video, for which this album is probably the closest that exists of a soundtrack. For me, the versions of "Careful with That Axe, Eugene", "Set the Controls for the Heart of the Sun" and "A Saucerful of Secrets" come pretty close to being the definitive versions of these tracks.

After the live tracks, Ummagumma continues with a disc full of solo recordings by each of the band members, a sprawling antithesis to the tight first album. Richard Wright's "Sysyphus" is a puzzling keyboard workout charting the Sysyphus myth complete with falling rock and everything, a strange avantgardish tone poem.

Roger Waters' "Several Species of Small Furry Animals..." can be seen either as a throwaway track or as a spaced-out drug experience, at any rate, it's just what the title promises -- the Pict at the end is particularly impressive. "Grantchester Meadows" seems to be about little more than sitting in a meadow, strumming your acoustic guitar and getting stoned. Fair enough.

David Gilmour's "The Narrow Way" is the closest this second record comes to a standard Pink Floyd song, if again a bit more bluesy than what you might expect, whereas Nick Mason's "Grand Vizier" is a mixture of flutes and weird percussion instruments.

That doesn't sound like a particularly exciting album, I know, but for me it is still one of the most varied, strangest and oddly enough, persistently interesting Pink Floyd albums out there, even though, and perhaps especially because it doesn't sound all that much like The Piper at the Gates of Dawn or The Dark Side of the Moon.

Pink Floyd’s second album of 1969 was even more acutely aimless. Ummagumma is a sprawling double album of immense contrasts. Disc 1—the live disc—is absolutely fantastic. It features reworkings of four songs: “Astronomy Domine”, “Set the Controls for the Heart of the Sun”, “Careful with That Axe, Eugene”, and “A Saucerful of Secrets”. That fourth song, in particular, is one of Pink Floyd’s top live moments, and that is a title with stiff competition.



Disc 2—the studio disc—in contrast, is an absolute mess. The four members of the band split up song writing duties, so each would get between 9 and 14 minutes of time. The band members had to play all the instruments on their tracks, much to the record’s detriment. This disc is a trudge, but it’s also the weirdest studio material they ever put out.



This disc opens with Richard Wright’s four-part “Sisyphus” suite. Part 1 has a promising opening, full of looming Mellotron and dramatic tympani and cymbals. Part 2 is piano-focused with a lot of jazzy dissonance which reminds me of the weakest parts of the studio version of “A Saucerful of Secrets”. Part 3 dives even deeper down the disjointed, atonal weirdness hole, to the point that it is almost unlistenable, especially with all its high-pitched squealing. Part 4 is as long as the other three parts combined and thankfully is the best of the bunch. It opens on a quiet and meditative note before some brief bombast leads into dull improv. It closes by revisiting Part 1’s theme. With the help of the rest of the band and a bit of workshopping, this piece may have been salvageable.



The same cannot be said of Roger Waters’s two compositions. “Grantchester Meadows” is an abusively dull seven-and-a-half minutes of floundering folk. Live renditions would be improved slightly, but not by much. The annoyingly-titled and entirely unlistenable “Several Species Of Small Furry Animals Gathered Together In A Cave And Grooving With A Pict” follows. Only Roger Waters’s voice is featured on this track, often manipulated in some way.



Side 2 of Disc 2 starts with David Gilmour’s three-part “The Narrow Way”, which is hands-down the best composition on the studio disc. Part 1 is a folky idyll, and Part 2 is centered around an off-kilter, slithering guitar riff. Part 3 is the best of the three parts and feels the most like an actual song, though Gilmour’s unsteadiness on the piano and drums is evident. (But kudos to him for branching out.)



In order to prepare for this review, this was the first time I’d listened to Nick Mason’s three-part “The Grand Vizier’s Garden Party” since high school. It’s not as awful as I remembered, but it’s mostly unfocused percussion and weird tape effects. If you’re into musique concrète, you may like this. Otherwise, skip it.

Let's say the live disc is a 4/5 and the studio disc is a 1/5. As for the latter: the few melodies that are discernible are hopelessly overblown and impotent, and only ever feel secondary. "Grantchester Meadows" is at least a moment of calm, and "Several Species" is interesting if only from a 'studio trickery' standpoint. If you like experimental, have at it. This may be your  Dark Side, you warped fuck.

Ummagumma the word may or may not be a slang term for sex, but Ummagumma the album is the sound of a band hedging its bets, split as it is between a live album of older material and a studio album of what are essentially solo compositions. The live album starts off well with a version of the Barrett-era track Astronomy Domine, which introduces a strange little quiet interlude towards the end of the song which doesn't quite work but otherwise succeeds at matching, though not quite exceeding, the thunderous, apocalyptic majesty of the studio version of the track. However, the versions of Careful With That Axe, Eugene, Set The Controls For the Heart of the Sun are actually better than the studio versions, to my mind, whilst the version of A Saucerful of Secrets turns the pure celestial merging of organ and choir at the end of the studio track into an opportunity for a triumphant instrumental workout, making the live disc of Ummagumma the best representative of the 1968-1969 post-Syd Pink Floyd sound which tended towards spacey instrumentals/almost-instrumentals rather than particularly tight or structured songwriting.



The studio album is a bit more of a mixed bag - inevitably, given the way it's given over to solo compositions. Richard Wright's Sysyphus has a bombastic opening that gives way to extended piano and organ noodling that makes for decent enough background mood music but doesn't elevate above that level, it's the sort of thing you might imagine the band turning out for a movie soundtrack.



Waters was a somewhat better songwriter at this point, but only somewhat, his two contributions to the studio album just aren't up to his usual standards. Grantchester Meadows consists of a not-quite-folk knockoff with birdsong effects obscuring the acoustic guitar and repetitive lyrics, continuing for some seven minutes, it again works best as background music rather than as something to actively listen to. Several Species of Small Furry Animals... comes across as a joke track which rather outstays its welcome.



The second side of the studio album is also somewhat undercooked: we are presented with David Gilmour's The Narrow Way, which consists mainly of Gilmour noodling away on his guitar with some synth effects added here and there over the top and some singing towards the end (actually, this is probably my favourite of the studio tracks and the only one which really is compelling as more than background music, the closing song actually being pretty good) and Nick Mason's The Grand Vizier's Garden Party, which eventually degenerates into a musique concrete drum solo.



The album as a whole might have been improved if the studio tracks were tightened up a bit and presented in the context of the "Man and the Journey" suite that the band were performing live at the time, rather than being sliced up and presented as solo tracks, but even so this studio material just isn't up to the band's usual standards. Ummagumma averages out at four stars or so - there's a great five-star live album there, and a three-star studio album which at points almost dips down into two-star territory.
If you're expecting to hear Dark Side of the Moon or Wish You Were Here

review: Apparently, I'm one of the few who appreciates this album more for the studio disc than the live one. Don't get me wrong, I think the live disc is pretty good, but I think it suffers due to the short set list. I understand there's a lot of great jamming going on, and it probably represents live Pink Floyd from that era pretty accurately, but I think with a couple minutes a song chopped off, they could have at least left room for "Interstellar Overdrive". Anyway, these guys were still cutting their teeth on what would become the best era of Pink Floyd (post-Barrett with Waters). The studio disc admittedly does get redundant at times, notably on Sisyphus and Garden Party. However, Gilmour and Waters' sections on this album are great, and judging from many reviews I've encountered, under-rated.

It's interesting that a release of such monstrosity would come immediately after the band parted ways with Syd. But then again Pink Floyd were never ones to settle in their sound: they were progressive, even for the genre of progressive rock, which is what would ultimately contribute to their reign as one of the greatest rock bands to ever live. They were versatile, unique, and most of all transcendent in their style. However, Ummagumma is a reminder that Pink Floyd didn't always have 20/20 vision for the artistic direction of their craft.

This album is sporadic. So much so that it becomes intolerable, and exhausting, given the 80 minute duration. It baffles one to ponder why Floyd would be so quick to become nostalgic, throwing together a medley of admittedly great tracks onto a live disc what felt like immediately after their sophomore release to most, while offering little to no ambiance of reality that would normally accompany such a release. Be that as it may, the live disc, music-wise, is the highlight of the double album. The chanting timpani mallets on "Set the Controls For The Heart of the Sun" provide a disorienting atmosphere, over which guitrars emphasize the aesthetic with roaring melismatic moans. "Astronomy Domine" utilizes a cascading guitar progression that presents a mental image of a comet falling to Earth. It's a beautiful opener to an album that ultimately implode under its own ideals.

The studio disc, which compiles some of Floyd's easily most experimental tracks, constantly leaps between vexatious psychedelia and arpeggio-based acoustic soothsayers like a man on hot coals, and with a similar gusto. Sysyphus, interestingly the longest song here at a much too long-winded 13 minutes, is an atrocity of indecision, dwindling about petty minimal keyboards like a 12 year old who got into his dad's private music room. It's a dirge so indulgent even Wright himself would later label it pretentious. However there are saving graces to the second album: "Several Species" creates a haunting naturalistic soundscape that intensifies like cicada calls in the night. It makes a good representation of the darker side of psychedelia, something rarely explored before for fear of literally scaring away the acid users that constituted a large majority of psychedelic music listeners of the time. Its eerie nature makes it all the better that the pleasant "Grantchester Meadows" would be its precursor, which echoes soft acoustic guitar while Waters serenades the audience with quiet lyrics of a bird flying over water(with the sounds of its ascension of course). It makes for a relaxing listen while maintaining its psychedelic integrity with its long but repetitive guitar melody and roundabout nature of repeating its chorus. 

Overall, this release is a mixed bag of epic proportions, but at the end of the day suffers too much from noisome inclusions like the earsore irish vocals on "The Grand Vizier's Garden Party", and an alarming sense of carelessness to be perceived as anything more than a playful, young Pink Floyd, who had experienced a horrible loss and seeked comfort in its blanket before returning to reality.
The live LP is awesome but the studio LP is totally uninteresting.
