review:
Il disco live fotografa la band in uno stato di forma che possiamo definire ottimale, considerato il livello dei bootleg in circolazione.

Nell'album studio ogni membro della band fa sfoggio della propria creatività e sensibilità artistica. 
Tutti i brani - da "Sysyphus" di Wright, all'esperimento rumoristico di "Several Species of Small Furry Animals Gathered Together in a Cave and Grooving With a Pict " di Waters, fino all'avvilente prova compositiva di Mason - denotano una tonitruante presunzione, che stona con l'effettiva capacità dei quattro di comporre brani progressive credibili, o comunque di fare musica all'altezza dei loro tempi.

Da lì a qualche anno, Waters e Co. avrebbero affinato notevolmente le proprie capacità compositive, dando alla luce i capolavori che tutti conosciamo. Ma prima che tale maturazione giungesse a termine, i Pink Floyd furono un gruppo ondivago e goffamente pretenzioso, ben rappresentato dalla tronfia inconsistenza di Ummagumma.

For me, everyone's review of this album is valid. I have my opinion of the album and I read other people's reviews and even if they differ from mine, I can see their point. Opinions of the studio portion (disc 2) ranges from a hard listen to outright sucking and I'm not about to try to defend against any of them.

My recent experience with this record in my iTunes collection is noticing there were songs missing. I did a search for the missing files and, indeed, I had removed the Rick Wright and Nick Mason . . . sequences. 

And no wonder, because I pretty much listen to my entire collection on shuffle these days, and breaking up Wright's "Sysphus" and Mason's "The Grand Vizier's Garden Party" and listening to pieces individually on shuffle play is intolerable.

Since I removed those files, I'd found a program that can stitch files together, so having re-found the files, I stitched them together and gave them a whirl. Still a hard sell.

Nothing brilliant, not even necessarily listenable, but they appealed to my appreciation for experimental music. A prior reviewer mentioned being able to tolerate the record only because of an early open mind towards experimental music. 

I think that's key to assess this record, even for those who don't like it. I'm not trying to convince anyone to like it or to not trash it. It's experimental. It's challenging. It's soundscapes. It ain't rock and roll. If you call it Pink Floyd, it's one thing, if it was by friends of Andy Warhol for some art exhibition/experiment, it'd be another thing.

I've read that the band members themselves don't stand behind this record. Again, perfectly valid.

But I've stitched Wright and Mason's files into single pieces and put them back in my iTunes collection (along with Gilmour's, which also should be one piece), and to my experimental sensibilities, they're more than tolerable, but interesting. I have far weirder and more challenging genres and music in my collection, most recently an inexplicable addiction to K-pop girl groups (I guarantee most people who have even heard of Ummagumma would rather listen to it than K-pop).

I think one of the members considered the record a "disaster". I think it's relative. Even with an appreciation for weird shit, there are days that I wouldn't be able to listen to Ummagumma, but other days when I can be taken in by it. Pink Floyd was a band I grew up with and developed a deep respect for. Why wouldn't I give them, in their deepest darkest creative moment, a chance to wonder what they were going for? (that's a rhetorical question, "because it sucks" being a valid response).

I'm talking about the studio portion, but the 3 1/2 rating includes the live portion.

"Ummagumma" is the 4th full-length studio album (partially live) by UK psychadelic/progressive rock act Pink Floyd. The album was released through Harvest Records in October 1969. The original release was a double LP. The first LP features 4 tracks recorded live and the second LP features new original studio compositions by the band. Or maybe more correctly each band member contributed their own songs to the project. "Ummagumma" is Pink Floyd´s second 1969 album, as they had already released "More" in July of 1969.

The 4 tracks featured on the live part of "Ummagumma" are great quality live tracks performed by an inspired and probably tripped out young band, featuring a pretty good sound quality (for the time) and a great atmosphere. The live versions of "Astronomy Domine" and "Set the Controls for the Heart of the Sun" are quite brilliant and the psychadelic trips "Careful With That Axe, Eugene" and "Saucerful of Secrets" are stunning too.

Unfortunately the studio part of the album can´t match the brilliance of it´s live counterpart. The Roger Waters´s penned "Grantchester Meadows" and the David Gilmour penned three part suite "Narrow Way" are decent contributions, but both the Nick Mason penned "Several Species of Small Furry Animals Gathered Together in a Cave and Grooving with a Pict" and the three part "The Grand Vizier's Garden Party (Part 1: Entrance, Part 2: Entertainment, Part 3: Exit)", and the Richard Wright penned four part "Sysyphus (Parts 1-4)", are pretty redundant to my ears.

The high quality of the live recordings on the first LP, are worth the price of admission alone, and they are the reason why "Ummagumma" is a worth while release even though the studio part of the album is decent at best and redundant at worst. A 3 - 3.5 star (70%) rating is deserved.

review:
This was my first Pink Floyd album after getting the excellent "Relics". Back when I was a teenager I was much more open and receptive to experimental music, so the studio album of this two LP set was right up my street. Had I first heard it 30 years later, my views may well be very different about the avant garde nature of some of the music. So, on that basis, I consider myself to be fortunate in that I heard it first when I was at my most open and receptive. I do believe that people hearing this for the first time in recent years are at an immediate disadvantage because their ears are not attuned to experimentation and/or music that does not take the traditional form of verse, verse, chorus, bridge chorus etc etc. Back in the mid to late sixties and early seventies, this pushing of the boundaries was, whilst not quite the norm, fairly common. Even now, with the passage of time and having developed a less tolerant ear, there is nothing on the studio set that I don't like and to cap it all off, there is the wonderful, pastoral beauty of Grantchester Meadows. This song, surely Roger Waters' finest, evokes the very essence of Summer and remains to this day, not only my favourite Pink Floyd track but is undoubtedly in my all time top ten songs.
The live content is, in my opinion, indispensable to all who love this spacey period of Floyd's output and all four songs are amongst the very best versions to be found anywhere.
I loved this album growing up and I love it still.

I am sometimes dropped into a limbo when listening to this album. I knew for a fact that Pink Floyd had this bizarre, experimental, incohesive period of songwriting (if that's what we can call them), though it is still puzzling whether or not they were purposefully producing inaudible noise music. 

Regarding the live side of Ummagumma, isn't really appealing to me since all the songs are from PATGOD, ASOS or Relics, and to me, studio version is superior than their respective live performances in terms of Pink Floyd's music.

The true problem however, arises in its B-side, the infamous "studio side" of Ummagumma. Other than the masterpiece Grandchester Meadows, a sole ray of hope in this hellish musical experience, this album is impossible to listen to unfortunately.

La prima parte dell'album è un live di pregevole fattura con brani già presenti nelle prime uscite registrate in studio, con l'aggiunta dell'interessante "Careful With That Axe, Eugene".

La seconda, invece, è equamente (purtroppo) diviso tra i vari componenti del gruppo. E' il loro lavoro più sperimentale, il che è decisamente un'arma a doppio taglio.
Il risultato finale è un cocktail dal buon sapore, ma dal retrogusto decisamente amaro. Anzi, a pensarci meglio, è uno spritz un po' annacquato ma con un prezzo onesto, di quelli che si possono trovate nel "Bar Centrale" di un comune di piccole dimensioni delle Prealpi Venete:

 -Il prosecco è della miglior qualità: le influenze classiche e l'inventiva di 
  Wright emergono prepotentemente, creando una suite bellissima e 
  travolgente. "Sysyphus" è una delle cose migliori prodotte dai Pink 
  Floyd, anche se sembra più dei King Crimson.
  E' un peccato che Richard non abbia mai avuto lo spazio è il 
  riconoscimento che avrebbe meritato, ma "con i se e i ma, la storia nun 
  se fa".

 -Buono anche l'Aperol Waters, anche se "Several bla bla bla..." trova il 
  tempo che trova

 -Ci pensano Mason e Gilmour ad annacquare la bevanda

Mason lo perdono, anche perché non mi ha mai convinto troppo come batterista, e non mi aspettavo nulla di che come compositore, però il Visir si fa ascoltare.

---Capitolo Gilmour---
Gilmour da solo è intollerabile e noioso. Ci vuole Waters a tenerlo al guinzaglio, sennò scappa e si perde (lo dimostrano "A Momentary Lapse of Treason" e "The Delusion Bell", l'ultimo non lo voglio neanche ascoltare).
Quando Gilmour ha carta bianca, sarebbe meglio che ci si pulisse il culo (citazioni importanti).

Nella sua parte, Gilmour dimostra di essere l'Anti-Wagner per eccellenza: ma non come l'ultimo Nietzsche o Stravinsky, magari!!
E' Anti-wagneriano nel senso che le opere in 3 Atti del vecchio Rick durano quattro ore, ma sembra che passino 15 minuti, la nenia pseudopsichedelica in 3 parti di David dura un quarto d'ora, ma sembra che ne passino quattro.
Consiglio a chi si cimenta nell'avventura dell'ascolto di "The Narrow Way" di posizionare un catino sotto la pelvi, così da ritrovare -una volta terminata- i propri gingilli senza troppi affanni.

I have a feeling that my take on Ummagumma is going to mirror that of many other reviewers. In short, the live disc is pretty great, and the studio disc is pretty poor.

The idea of a double-album with one live and one studio LP predated Ummagumma in the form of Wheels of Fire, the 1968 Cream release which spawned “White Room.” Similarly-formatted albums over the next few years included (Untitled) (the Byrds, 1970) Eat a Peach (Allman Brothers, 1972), and Some Time In New York City (John Lennon, Yoko Ono, Elephant’s Memory, 1972). There was also Live-Evil, a 1971 Miles Davis double album on which live and studio recordings were intermixed.

But whereas the Allman Brothers, Byrds, and Cream albums are still considered classics today, the reputation of Ummagumma is closer to that of Some Time in New York City.

My complaint is not that Ummagumma should’ve been condensed into a single album. Choosing and reassembling the best 50% of Tales from Topographic Oceans might have made for a much better album, but most of the best 50% of Ummagumma is already on one LP. Who knows, maybe with a magician like Teo Macero in charge, something like a Live-Evil might have been possible with Ummagumma, in fact, earlier in 1969, on Uncle Meat, Frank Zappa had already demonstrated some of the possibilities of using tape editing to assemble a single musical work from both live and studio sources. But short of that, my solution to the Ummagumma problem would be to decouple the live album from the studio album.

The live half of Ummagumma would be a four-star album, in my opinion. It’s a fine example of the kind of prog excess that would inspire Spinal Tap, but I enjoy it anyway. Or most of it—I could do without the screaming on “Careful With That Axe.” Although I acknowledge that guitarist David Gilmour was probably the only seriously talented instrumentalist on stage, both the drumming and the keyboard-guitar interplay are very good. The bass lines are also good when they’re audible.

The best track on the live album is “Astronomy Domine,” although both songs on Side Two are nearly as good. On the studio album, “Narrow Way” stands out as the closest to the quality of the live material. But each of the five studio songs comes across as half-baked experimentation for the sake of experimentation. The band and/or the record label must have realized that tens of thousands of consumers would purchase this either the live or studio LPs immediately and ask questions later, but to play it safe, they bundled them to make sure anyone who bought one had to pay for both. 

But it’s art, and it’s what Pink Floyd wanted to be in the fall of 1969. 

I’ve never been much of a Floyd fan. I enjoyed their 1980s singles, from “Another Brick in the Wall” to “Learning to Fly.” I also appreciate Animals and Wish You Were Here. Ummagumma is really nothing like any of that, but the live disk is every bit as good as anything I’ve heard from this group.

review:
I think this is great for what it was all the way back in 1969. Most psychedelic albums back then complimented psychedelics rather than tried to completely emulate them, and this album is a great interpretation of an acid trip. It makes me feel like I'm on drugs when I'm completely sober, which is what the Neo-Psych wave was more known for. It might not be great rock music (because it isn't rock music, which is a big reason why this is so poorly received), but it was a great step forward in the experimentation of Sound Pink Floyd would explore more in their later LP's.

Yeah they sacrificed musicality for blatant experimentation on this LP, but I'm still glad PF made it.

(not to mention there's still a great live recording attached to this thing, so it wasn't all in "Vain")

Careful with that axe, Eugene. Ummagumma is the 3rd studio album by Pink Floyd released in 1969. After they finished their work on the soundtrack for "More" Pink Floyd continued creating for the next album. This album is structured differently than the bands other albums as the first disc is essentially a live album with some favorites that they played live frequently, while the second disc is the studio album with the band members creating music without any input or influence from the rest of the band. Richard Wright created the piano suite "Sysyphus". Roger Waters created "Grantchester Meadows" and the Musique concrète piece "Several Species of Small Furry Animals Gathered Together in a Cave and Grooving With a Pict". David Gilmour made "The Narrow Way" and Nick Mason made "The Grand Vizier's Garden Party".                                                                         As you can probably tell if you heard this album its probably their most experimental overall with each member doing a different thing. review:
I think that of all the good Pink Floyd albums, not many are going to like Ummagumma. From the perspective of many, even myself included at one point, this is just a compilation of some okay music, and some pretentious garbage, oh and a live set too. And you know what? I get it, but after hearing so much of the Floyd, I get this album too. Ummagumma is the peak of Pink Floyd's psychedelic era. While the music they make after this point becomes more and more sophisticated and artistic, I think it truly drifts away from the real psychedelic sound they present here as this album is what I think of when I think of peak psychedelia.



I guess I'll first talk about the live side. Astronomy Domine and Set the Controls are both good. Not crazy but they present a pretty streamlined vision and are played well. Saucerful was a better version than the one on the album, that's for sure. While Gilmour's ahhhhhhhh in at the end was a little weaker than it should have been, it's a fine performance. What's awesome on this side? Careful with that Axe Eugene! What a subtle intro with the bass, what a masterful build up, and what a god damn amazing thing that is the climax! Waters' screeching at the end is so fitting, so powerful! The live side represents this awesome era of the Floyd that they captured so well in the compilations.



I guess first I can describe the Wright and Mason contributions. Sysyphus is a pretty cool composition. The piano is nice and the intro and outro are really cool. Same idea goes for the Garden Party, nothing special but more than gets the job done.



Waters, the asshole he is, of course supplies us with Grantchester Meadows, a really nice guitar duet with him and Gilmour, and then makes you listen to the looping experimental garbage that is Several Species of Small Furry Animals Gathered Together in a Cave and Grooving With a Pict. While I can definitely be glad that this experimentation would lead him to do great things (you can even hear how he would go on to make On the Run) but this is just not a fun listen.



Finally I can mention The Narrow Way. If there are highlights on this album, it's Careful with that Axe and then the Narrow Way. My my, what a theme, what chord choices what playing! Every part of this composition, from the beautiful intro, the mysterious middle section, and the swirling outro encapsulate the very sound that Pink Floyd would do perfectly for the next decade or so.



This album will always be a little sad to me. It's the first album in which Syd Barrett had nothing to do with the band and while his influence is very much still intact, his singing, vocals, and writing are all dearly missed. To be honest, after losing an important bandmate, this is as graceful as a comeback is it could get, and set them on track to really just be one of the greatest bands ever. So you know what, I swore I would never like Ummagumma, I thought it was pretentious random sound at one point, but I've grown, I've adapted, and I love this album just like the rest. Good Job Pink.

Pink Floyd’s last foray into psychedelic music and, while it is their most focused and inconsistent, it is still an excellent effort. Half of the album was recorded live, and the other half in the studio. It is undoubtedly Pink Floyd’s most divisive record and is easy to see why. There is a clear disparity in quality between the two halves of the record, which I hope to express accordingly in this review.



The live portion of the album could have been the ultimate masterpiece of Pink Floyd’ entire career. The only major omission was “Interstellar Overdrive” which gets replaced instead by “Careful With That Axe, Eugene” In fact, the more dramatic, thriller take on this track would propel it to become a new classic for Pink Floyd fans. The somber opening instrumental is shattered abruptly in the 3 minute mark by Roger Waters’ bloodcurdling scream as the group launch into a frenzied hard rock jam. Interestingly, Roger achieved this inhuman scream by breathing in while screaming, vocalizing  and chaining his tortured voice to the rhythm itself. The other live tracks are also spectacular. “Set the Controls For the Heart of the Sun” is far more oriental and neoclassical while also taking off into a psychedelic jam, lasting over twice as long as the studio recording. The version of “A Saucerful of Secrets” is also no slouch. A romantic take on the sonic landscape of the original, with a poignant hymn-like coda performed by David Gilmour to close out the live portion of the album.



The studio part of the album is where the songwriting and direction clearly falls apart. All members of the band are responsible for at least one track on the album. Richard Wright’s contribution is the four part “Sysyphus”, a bloated medieval fanfare / ballad executed in harmonic freedom and perfection. Richard Wright is clearly an expert in crafting austere and experimental piano structures that jut out from the depths of his depraved creative mind. His piano playing here is certainly reminiscent of the second part of “A Saucerful of Secrets” yet I get the impression that he’s simply trying too hard to sound experimental, so some of his piano passages in the second and fourth part feel cumbersome and pompous. Roger Water’s compositions are “Grantchester Meadows” which captures soft rock acoustic balladry ala Leonard Cohen and electronic bird chirping and “Several Species of Small Furry Animals Gathered Together in a Cave and Grooving With a Pict” which attempts to simulate an intense collection of woodland creatures living in harmony with electronic voice modulations, and percussion that sounds like several animals chirping, tapping, and scampering about. It is possibly, the most embarrassing moment of early Pink Floyd, with its obnoxious looped electronic voices and irritatingly high-pitched animal soundalikes. 



David Gilmour pens “The Narrow Way”. A track that moves between all styles of contemporary rock music. The guitar is at first folk, then heavy-metal, and then psychedelic in each of the three parts. It is the most uncertain music of the studio recordings. The whole atmosphere is neurotic and delirious. The electronic soundscape is desperately trying to create mini vortexes of psychedelic sound effects while Gilmour repeatedly experiments with guitar counterpoints before settling on the psych rock formula of most of the material from “A Saucerful of Secrets”. Lastly, Nick Mason contributes “The Grand Visiter’s Garden Party” which is mostly centered around the second part with an entrance and exit for thematic effect. It is a fascinating piece inspired by the early electroacoustic work of Karlheinz Stockhausen (“Kontakte”) that is hardly psych rock anymore. Percussion comes from all sorts of sources, from timpani, to xylophone, and even the Mellotron is used for percussive and atmospheric elements. It is self-indulgent, but not in a negative way. More akin to exploring every possible avenue of sound on a guitar or piano but stretched out to fill an entire performance.



While “Ummagumma” reaches the same heights as the first two albums, the overall sporadic level of quality of the studio efforts bog the album down and ruin its reputation. Most people view this album as the band jumping the shark on their experimental roots. The group feared that their self-indulgence and pretention was received quite harshly when the album was released. Thus, they completely changed the direction and sound of their future output which appealed to more people, but alienated the fans who wanted more psychedelic experiments and twisted musical landscapes.

review:
This album starts with the recording of a live. Pink Floyd is known for their legendary concerts, so you expect that a live with their early work, which is fine, should be good, well its kind of a mess, honestly.  I think the first two recordings are pretty good. However, the other two songs, which are way longer, are way less captivating, and not as rewarding. This first part is a bit disappointing. (2.5/5)



After that, you get to the actual studio album. I actually like the concept here, a record where each band member gets their part and does what they want. Of course, that will come with some pacing issues, but it helps someone listen to the whole record, because instead of having to listen to the whole thing in one sit, so you can follow the album's narrative, you can take your time with each part.



This starts with Richard Wright's "Sysyphus", which is divided in 4 parts. I think his part of the record is fine, nothing particularly annoying or bad about it, and its not bland, but at the end, I felt a bit underwhelmed by it, and I can't really grasp why, the instrumentation is huge and cinematic, unfortunately, there is still that feeling of emptiness. (3/5)



Next are the two songs by Roger Waters, let's analyse each one. The first song is "Grantchester Meadows", truly a great track, in my opinion. The song is pretty simple, the only instrumentation is a guitar, but that's the beauty of it. The bird sound in the background make this feel like a warm summer morning, when you wake up and you see the sun shining through the window, truly amazing. The track ends with someone killing a fly, and for me, that works pretty well, with the summer feeling of the rest of the song. Waters' next track is "Several Species of Small Furry Animals Gathered Together In A Cave And Grooving With A Pict". I think most people don't like this song for its weird animal sounds, that could come across as irritating. For me, this is a really disturbing song, a great one. The animals work together, of course it is weird, but they intended for it to be weird. The fact that this song is addicting says something about its nature, it feels like idk a Sophie beat or something, and it is impressive that Pink Floyd was able to do something that reminds me of 2010's production in 1969. With that, the only complaint I have about these two tracks is that they don't really work together. One reminds me of a summer day and the other of a Sophie beat, you can see the difference there. (4/5)



Next is David Gilmour with "The Narrow Way". This song is divided in three parts, and I do think there is a difference in quality between these parts. The first two are kind of whatever, they passed by quickly and there was nothing memorable to take from it. The last part, though, is so far my favorite song on the record, not really sure why, it is a straightforward song, not that psychedelic, but I love it. So that is what I have to say about "The Narrow Way" it takes half of the song to get to the good part, but at least you get there. (3.5/5)



To finish the record, you have Nick Mason's "The Grand Vizier's Garden Party ". Again, it is divided in three parts, ig now they have names, but I don't really care. I'm not going to focus on the first and last parts since they aren't even 1 minute long. The second part, called entertainment, is the main event here, and it was a let down. You have a cool drum segment toward the end of the track, and I think he should have explored more of his drumming skills. Unfortunately, he doesn't do that, most of this track is just noise, it is like him messing with different percussion instruments, without making music out of them. It is borderline ASMR, which is nice ig, except for the fact that it isn't. Disappointing, could have been way better. (1.5/5)



To conclude, this record was a bit all over the place, I thought the Roger Water, David Gilmour and Richard Wright's segments were enjoyable, with the low points being the live recording, which was average, and Nick Mason. The live is half of the album, so to decide my final grade I'm going to do the average of the different segments of the studio album (the final grade for the studio album is 3/5), and know this with the live recording grade, it rounds up to a 3/5.

5 stars for the album cover and the live side is a nice bonus and deduct 2.5 starts for the self indulgent mess that is basically the rest. The only real dint in the incredible lead up to the stratospheric albums that would follow…

live part is easily a 9/10. such a dark, atmospheric live recording. the most live sounding of all live albums i've heard, to be honest. something about it really puts me in that room, something about it really makes me see the four of them on that stage guiding us through the lengthy psychedelic-era songs, recovering from their loss of syd. a very great example of pink floyd's excellent shows as a live band. there's a dark edge to it, a haunting quality to it, especially due to the richard wright's keys, which carry a really powerful sound throughout, especially on 'set the controls for the heart of the sun'. it reminds me of what people say about Wish You Were Here, the spirit of syd in their music despite him only existing in pink floyd's history for such a brief period of time. definitely worth checking out for anyone.



studio part is a 7/10 for me, which is probably pretty controversial. obviously most of it is very abstract, and that's probably why most people rate it so lowly. it is not really like any other pink floyd release. the splitting into parts idea i think works quite well, it means that these bizarre ideas can be split into sections. 

sysyphus has an erratic jumpiness to it, twists and turns that shake and throw you around. the outro part is very textural and quite peaceful at points, and also very harsh and biting at others. a really unique piece of music.

grantchester meadows is beautiful, an easily accessible peaceful song that shows a pretty part of pink floyd everyone loves. 

several species of small furry animals... is honestly a favourite of mine. it builds and builds from the start of a bird call with such interesting textures to it, it has elements of music despite being produced to be such an avant-garde piece. there's repetition, there's percussion. it's almost like a collection of samples arranged into a song, as is seen far later in electronic music. it is pretty disturbing when you listen to it past the humourous aspect of it. very Nurse With Wound, to be honest, with the sound collage elements to form one piece.

the narrow way is a pretty conventional piece of music. nothing incredibly inaccessible on it. nice little acoustic part, followed by a very progressive electric guitar part and finishing up with a pretty conventional song as pink floyd goes. nice chord progression and melody, a contrast with what is around it on this studio part. 

the grand vizier's garden party is a very sparse percussive section that really tones things down to close out the album. lots of strange warping of sounds to contrast the drums. again, as with the other abstract parts of the album it relies on texture and rhythm to carry you through. 



overall definitely overhated, as i think it attracts two different audiences with the live/studio, meaning it's rare for someone to be into both parts to give it a good rating. furthermore, people who would typically be into pink floyd are not likely to be into the studio side, and people who would like the studio side would not typically be into pink floyd, i believe. just my guess

How does Pink Floyd evolve without Syd Barrett and make a case that they can be just as compelling a listen without the mad creativity of their former frontman? Ummagumma  is a pretty good attempt at rekindling the freaky psychedelic spirit that the group initially had – in fact, it’s even trippier than Piper and quite the experimental endeavour from a group that is willing to tap into their creative selves and take risks. Keyboardist Richard Wright was adamant to record new music rather than simply releasing live recordings with the suggestion that every member come up with their own material, isolated from one another’s influences. A bold move and one that I’d say ultimately paid off, even if the band has yet to really hit their stride. 



I felt that A Saucerful of Secrets played it a little too safe with its more conventional song structures, ultimately living in the shadow of Syd and Piper. With Ummagumma, we get the sense that the band is as creative and twisted with their music as Barrett was, albeit in their own way. The freeform approach to their songwriting here is a welcome change of pace as they didn’t really possess the ability to come up with the shorter compelling songs featured on their inaugural album. Whether the group was conscious of that at the time is for them to know and for us, the audience, to speculate. As such, what you get out of Ummagumma largely depends on your tolerance for experimentation and whether you enjoy rather directionless instrumentals that transport you into dreamland. 



Wright’s Sysyphus is the darkest segment of the album with brooding and beautiful piano playing in part 2 and a nightmarish soundscape that permeates part 4. Waters brings us back to warmer pastures with Grantchester Meadows, with chirping birds layering a dreamy folk tune before plunging us back into his next song that is as mad as the title is. Gilmour’s slice of the creative pie with Narrow Way is the most melodically active segment of the whole, still fairly psychedelic and arguably more satisfying from a musically driven perspective. The band member who had it tough is Mason, it’s not as obvious to come up with whole songs that are primarily driven by percussion and that can be heard with The Grand Vizier’s Garden which he still does a decent job with. Aside from the new material, the band also takes liberties with their live renditions on the first side of the album that breathes new life into the songs, especially Set the Controls for the Heart of the Sun and A Saucerful of Secrets. However, the most engaging and definitely the most unsettling of the live performances lies with Careful With That Axe, Eugene which features Roger Waters possibly churning out the first black metal screams recorded, if you want to call them that. 



Ummagumma isn’t quintessential Pink Floyd if judged by the group’s musically accomplished songwriting and commercial success of the 70s, yet it is their most unique album and one that is sure to raise eyebrows with its heavy leanings into psychedelic soundscapes. If you wish to hear how crazy the band could get, this is the one, unequivocally.

review: I consider this album to be Pink Floyd's worst.  It is a soundtrack so maybe the music works better in conjunction with the film (which I've never seen) but the album is pretty weak.  There are some nice melodies in songs like "Cirrus Minor" and "Cymbeline" but most of the songs are instrumental mood pieces.  I suppose it might work if you're on acid.

Although this soundtrack might seem like a triviality at first sight, it's actually a significant if uneven recording for Pink Floyd. While they were busy indulging in wild ambitiousness on their studio albums at the time, this project sounds like it forced them into more concise songs. The closest thing to an exception is the avant-garde instrumental "Quicksilver", which might be as good as any of their other similar experiments. 



Some of the songs venture into uncharacteristic territory for the band. "The Nile Song" and its nearly-identical twin "Ibiza Bar" are thudding hard rockers, "Up The Khyber" is a short attempt at jazzy avant-garde, and "A Spanish Piece" is a bizarre little tidbit with some silly spoken banter. 



The best songs though focus on slow melodic ideas that would pave the way for the band's later success. "Cirrus Minor", "Crying Song", and "Cymbaline" are brooding, heady slices of psychedelia that are very effective and evocative. As some of it probably serves a better purpose for the movie, this album is far from solid. But it's worth it for those so inclined, even if just for those 3 great songs.

Since I never really liked the two first Syd Barret written Floyd records, for me the history begins really with More.



This is a soundtrack (just like Obscured By Clouds), I have not seen the film, however the record made me curious about that. Some of the tracks are songs on their own rights (some better, some weaker), the others are functional soundtracks (or scores better to say).



‘Cirrus Minor’ is brilliant, relaxing, psychedelic, lovely, so 1960s. ‘The Nile Song’ (and its melody theme reprise ‘Ibiza Bar’) is an outstanding one, a hard rocking song with great guitars. ‘Green Is The Colour’ and ‘Cymbaline’ are also great songs, the latter one is a very special piece by Gilmour, there is something childish and pleasant in that. Spacey themes are ‘Main Theme’ or ‘Quicksilver’, this was the real born of ambient music. ‘Up The Khyber’ and especially ‘A Spanish Piece’ have a weirder approach though. These remind me to the second disc of Ummagumma, however those experimental tracks were much more self-indulgent pieces, here they have a role, they are put among the ordinary songs, thus they don’t make the entire record a filler album.



Surely not their best, but still a historical release of psychedelic music. The Floyd couldn’t do wrong with the big line-up.

This is a record entirely made to a movie soundtrack so don’t expect great originality from Pink Floyd’s third album. The characteristic psychedelic sound have had almost vanished in prior of calm instrumental ambiences and calm melodies that fit very well on the film. In fact, Pink Floyd’s soundtrack is the best the movie has to offer.

We can listen some of their previous records clichés. The title track from “A Saucerful Of Secrets” appears in the sad church organ in Cirrus Minor but also in the crescendo drumming overture and subsequent psycho sounding of Up The Khyber. The album also offers the hard rocking tracks The Nile Song and Ibiza Bar, the country Green Is The Colour, and some spacey passages in Main Theme and specially QuickSilver (more again sounding like a track taken from the previous record).

The story of the film (and album) I transcribed it from IMDB: “The making of a heroin addict. Stefan, fresh from college, hitches from Germany to Paris, where he does a little breaking and entering to get some money and soon meets Estelle, a young American. Even through a friend warns him off Estelle, Stefan falls for her (she's quixotic). Although he's quickly petulant and possessive, she invites him to follow her to Ibiza for romance in the sun and sand. There Stefan meets another German, Wolf, whose connection with Estelle Stefan can't fathom. He also meets Kathy, whose relationship with Estelle is also unclear at first. What explains her disappearances and furtive behavior? And once Stefan understands, what will he do about it?”

Not an essential album, the more progressive sound derivates from their previous releases. Nevertheless, it shows the idea of globalization of the psychedelic movement. Basically, for Pink Floyd’s fans or great collectors.

The one album to catch me with most interest was more
it's backdrawn floating approach, adventureous yet compact is that fascination filled yet relaxed fluid effort that sums of a very comfortable sound, you know that all the way, relaxing and surrounding sound that is hard to find. 

It's not the album with the most striking or capturing overwhelming arrangments, this band tried creative trickery and  all over swaying around early on, and I guess with more they could just sit back and let things go fluently, quite unlike  than before.
This is why I liked more since the first time I heard it. It might not be the Most exciting or grand packed album you´ll ever here . But that's the better, what's great with more is that it 
just flows, it's not over energetic,  or overly grand
it's not the most loaded album with hooks and gripping melody strokes, but it's a very comfy album, It's like after going up and around on the first and second album for having a thrill and exploring, this is where they comfortably   just set back and let things flow. More fluidly blanced album and that's why it's a very favourable one, not beause it's so catchty or strikingly gripping it's in your speakers all the while with a boom, no it's just there, 
it's a very collected affair, It is not up there on a fuse, packed and oh so shining But it has a solidity unlike many other albums of it's era, the album is undemanding and it's without struggeling to be breath taking or mind blowing it's just flows on and around and creates a very Chilled atmosphere, it's not the  look here! approach  or force major approach of later acclaimed albums like "Dark side of the moon" or others alike, it's just there and therefore it's comfortable satifiying unlike anything I previously heard from this group. 
 
it's just there with fluid warmth, dreamy flowing warmth, it collects a feeling of relief and is well serving all the way.

More's fluid approach brings home the sprawling, all over approach, previously presented, and creates a more laidback approach. which serves the listner with capturing ease. the opening sway with bird vocals and surrounding fluid instruments swirling softly sets a very settled mood ahead for the rest of the album, it rocks out and travels too, 

"Nile Song" is percitulary packed with strenght, but never does the album stray from the well collected fuild approach, and this album doesn't loose track.

The solid ground swaying, brings us a more together sound than before, a collected less out of frame sound, very well performed ease. and flow "crying song" fine swaying flow at it's finest. it's said to be the only track on this third to feature the bands previous bandleader Syd Barett too, and it's great how together it flows. 

While it is countered with a bit of a rolling bounce with the organ poundings of "up the Khyber" for instance, the sound doesn't fall out astray, it's well rounded for it's 2 minutes and it's leaves a fine balance to the fluid swaying that brings everything together on Roger Waters Green is the colour, 

the laid back melody sways on with "Cymbaline", which cleverly flows and beatifully strums with ease, with slick and clever rhyming and attetion grabbing wordplay and a hooking yet laid back chorus. the solid deep organ flow of in the ending passage bring it all in in the end, and drift is accompanied by 

"party scene" a slighter of scale number that is perfectly settled on it's one minute roll away. as it opens for the space swirl drifting of the main theme. which twangs here and there to a solid twistin tom tom beat. Very together yet again.
As it drifts off, we get into a chunky groove that sets that solid strong rock mood again with strechin guitars and pounding drums, with "ibiza bar". With ease,

 it brings back the sound offered on "the nile song" with a solid stand and stil keeping the solid flow on the album, a very flowing together sound.

the laidback instrumental "more blues" follows and drifts and roles easy, and bit on and off beat but is very well rounded.
as a, right down to it,  short instrumental.

It carries a Genre-themeatic aproach which is also is eveident on the simliary lengthed " a spanish piece" that also brings a right down to it musical styling short and sweet up front. in between this we have the spacy obscure tinkeling and swaying quicksliver with sways on a more drifting approach for 7 minutes. The shorter pieces serve as good frame work, to this rather, dark, and hard to shape odd piece, which however also bring in to the swaying, laidback backdrawn approach of this score, while being so strange it still never moves out of frame but is well rounded and in place through out, albeit with a very ambient carachter.  
Dramatic theme comes last, a two minute number with a very solid builded rhythmn and pace, which creates the foundation for some bending guitars and grooving bass 
simple and compact. 

This rounds of in the swaying well rounded fashion, it started with it doesn't go out with a boom, but then again never aimed to do so. It's  a swaying experience. the well collected swaying that ends out on a soft fading out in takes a sombre farwell, just enough an balanced, this is a very fine  music piece and is an artisticly atmospheric one at that.

69 was a laid back year for music, and this album sums up a good feel of being swayingly melodic without being over packed or demanding. It flow with ease, and at this stage Pink Floyd really succeded with a conistent flowing sound all the way, and to me it is fully serving, The atmospheric laidback swaying of this album puts it in my essential solid sound file, it's also the perfect balance between the bouncy traveling psych of the mid 60's and that overly epic grand like sound of the 70's.

Pink Floyd's More contained at least three masterpieces:

"Cirrus Minor" may well be the starting point of slowcore, the drugged catatonic ballad "enhanced" to a fluorescent state by a dilating organ and vivid ambient sound-effects. Then "The Nile Song" was a blues/ heavy-rock meltdown, with a ferocious intensity rarely heard in 1969, the distortions reaching to a psychological breaking point. Another revelation, the electronic Eastern psychedelia "Main Theme" predates both kraut-rock and the electronic kosmische-rock of the 70's.

The album then had:

- A few ballads, like the quasi-country gentle strumming of "Crying Song" (magnified by psychedelic effects), the idylic, nostalgic "Green Is The Colour" (with Indian flutes), and the drama-rock "Cymbaline" (predating their 70's endeavours).

- A few experimental vignettes, such as the free jazz "Up The Khyber" (vibrated by a hissing Middle-Eastern organ and seismic effects), the shaman ceremony "Party Sequence", the stoned blues-jam "More Blues", and the abstract stellar constellation "Quicksilver".

Not too bad when considering that this album followed two masterpieces, and that it's a soundtrack and a commissioned work.
